premise: Statement1: Consumer advocate : there is no doubt that the government is responsible for the increased cost of gasoline, because the government's policies have significantly increased consumer demand for fuel, and as a result of increasing demand, the price of gasoline has risen steadily.
hypothesis: Premise1: The government can bear responsibility for that which it indirectly causes.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Consumer demand for gasoline cannot increase without causing gasoline prices to increase.

premise: Statement1: To suit the needs of corporate clients, advertising agencies have successfully modified a strategy originally developed for political campaigns. Statement2: This strategy aims to provide clients with free publicity and air time by designing an advertising campaign that is controversial, thus drawing prime-time media coverage and evoking public comment by officials.
hypothesis: Premise1: The usefulness of an advertising campaign is based solely on the degree to which the campaign's advertisements persuade their audiences.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Advertising agencies that specialize in campaigns for corporate clients are not usually chosen for political campaigns.

premise: Statement1: Consumer magazine : because front-loading washers use less water than top-loading washers, ordinary powder detergent does not dissolve readily in front-loading washers. Statement2: So, to get clothes really clean in a front-loading machine you need to use a detergent formulated especially for front-loading washers, instead of ordinary powder detergent.
hypothesis: Premise1: A laundry detergent does not get clothes really clean in a washer unless it dissolves readily in it.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Washers that use more water get clothes cleaner than those that use less.

premise: Statement1: The dean of computing must be respected by the academic staff and be competent to oversee the use of computers on campus. Statement2: The only deans whom academics respect are those who hold doctoral degrees, and only someone who really knows about computers can competently oversee the use of computers on campus. Furthermore, the board of trustees has decided that the dean of computing must be selected from among this university's staff. Statement3: Therefore, the dean of computing must be a professor from this university's computer science department.
hypothesis: Premise1: Among this university's staff members with doctoral degrees, only those in the computer science department really know about computers.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: At this university, every professor who holds a doctoral degree in computer science really knows about computers.

premise: Statement1: Consumer advocate : ephedra is a naturally occurring compound that some people use as a weight-loss aid. Statement2: Recently, the government prohibited the sale of dietary supplements containing ephedra on the grounds that ephedra has been shown to have grave side effects. This prohibition is unreasonable. Statement3: Echinacea is another natural compound that has been shown to have side effects, yet echinacea is widely available at health food stores.
hypothesis: Premise1: The side effects of echinacea and ephedra are comparably serious.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Before the prohibition, ephedra had been available in health food stores.

premise: Statement1: Often patients with ankle fractures that are stable, and thus do not require surgery, are given follow-up x-rays because their orthopedists are concerned about possibly having misjudged the stability of the fracture. Statement2: When a number of follow-up x-rays were reviewed, however, all the fractures that had initially been judged stable were found to have healed correctly. Statement3: Therefore, it is a waste of money to order follow-up x-rays of ankle fracture initially judged stable.
hypothesis: Premise1: X-rays of patients of many different orthopedists working in several hospitals were reviewed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Doctors who are general practitioners rather than orthopedists are less likely than orthopedists to judge the stability of an ankle fracture correctly.

premise: Statement1: More and more law firms specializing in corporate taxes are paid on a contingency-fee basis. Statement2: Under this arrangement, if a case is won, the firm usually receives more than it would have received if it had been paid on the alternate hourly rate basis. If the case is lost, the firm receives nothing. Statement3: Most firms are likely to make more under the contingency-fee arrangement.
hypothesis: Premise1: Since the majority of firms specialize in certain kinds of cases, they are able to assess accurately their chances of winning each potential case
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Firms working under the contingency-fee arrangement take in fewer cases per year than do firms working under the hourly rate arrangement.

premise: Statement1: It is popularly believed that a poem has whatever meaning is assigned to it by the reader. Statement2: But objective evaluation of poetry is possible only if this popular belief is false ; for the aesthetic value of a poem can not be discussed unless it is possible for at least two readers to agree on the correct interpretation of the poem.
hypothesis: Premise1: A given poem can be objectively evaluated only if the poem's aesthetic value can be discussed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If two readers agree about the meaning of a given poem, that ensures that an objective evaluation of the poem can be made.

premise: Statement1: Critic : rock music is musically bankrupt and socially destructive, but at least the album covers of rock lps from the 1960s and 1970s often featured innovative visual art. Statement2: But now, since the success of digital music has almost ended the production of lps, rock music has nothing going for it.
hypothesis: Premise1: Digital music is not distributed with accompanying innovative visual art.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Although very few lps are produced today, most of these are rock lps.

premise: Statement1: Music critic : fewer and fewer musicians are studying classical music, decreasing the likelihood that those with real aptitude for such music will be performing it. Audiences who hear these performances will not appreciate classical music's greatness and will thus decamp to other genres. Statement2: So to maintain classical music's current meager popularity, we must encourage more young musicians to enter the field.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most people who appreciate classical music come to do so through old recordings rather than live performances.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: It is possible to enjoy the music in a particular genre even when it is performed by musicians who are not ideally suited for that genre.

premise: Statement1: Twelve years ago and again five years ago, there were extended periods when the darfir republic's currency, the pundra, was weak : its value was unusually low relative to the world's most stable currencies. Both times a weak pundra made darfir's manufactured products a bargain on world markets, and darfir's exports were up substantially. Statement2: Now some politicians are saying that, in order to cause another similarly sized increase in exports, the government should allow the pundra to become weak again.
hypothesis: Premise1: After several decades of operating well below peak capacity, darfir's manufacturing sector is now operating at near-peak levels.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A sharp improvement in the efficiency of darfir's manufacturing plants would make darfir's products a bargain on world markets even without any weakening of the pundra relative to other currencies.

premise: Statement1: Educator : some experimental educational programs, based on the principle that children's first education should take place at home, instruct parents in how to be their child's" first teacher. This shows that these programs are successful and should be expanded. Statement2: The school performance of the children in these programs is better than average.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most of the parents participating in the programs have prior experience as educators.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some children who did not participate in the programs performed exceptionally well in school.

premise: Statement1: Super express shipping company has implemented a new distribution system that can get almost every package to its destination the day after it is sent. The company worries that this more efficient system will result in lower sales of its premium next-day delivery service, because its two day service will usually arrive the following day anyway. Statement2: The company plans to encourage sales of its next-day service by intentionally delaying delivery of its two-day packages so that they will not be delivered the following day, even if the package arrives at its destination city in time for next-day delivery.
hypothesis: Premise1: Deliberate delay of packages will not affect the company's image in a way that significantly reduces its ability to attract and retain customers
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The overnight service is too expensive to be attractive to most customers currently

premise: Statement1: The photographs that the store developed were quite unsatisfactory. Statement2: The customer claims to have handled the film correctly. Neither the film nor the camera was defective. If a store does not process pictures properly, the customer is owed a refund, so if the customer's claim is correct, the store owes her a refund.
hypothesis: Premise1: If neither the film nor the camera was defective, and the customer handled the film correctly, then the store processed it improperly.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the customer handled the film incorrectly, that is what caused the photographs that the store developed to be unsatisfactory.

premise: Statement1: Human intelligence is not possible without human emotions. Statement2: A computer is something that can never have emotions, so for that reason alone a computer will never be able to display intelligence.
hypothesis: Premise1: Being intelligent requires the capacity to have emotions.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Someone or something is intelligent only if it can identify its emotions.

premise: Statement1: The miscarriage of justice in the barker case was due to the mistaken views held by some of the forensic scientists involved in the case, who believed that they owed allegiance only to the prosecuting lawyers. Statement2: Justice was thwarted because these forensic scientists failed to provide evidence impartially to both the defense and the prosecution. Statement3: Hence it is not forensic evidence in general that should be condemned for this injustice.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most forensic scientists acknowledge a professional obligation to provide evidence impartially to both the defense and the prosecution.

premise: Statement1: Naturalist : sent2: the recent claims that the tasmanian tiger is not extinct are false. Statement2: The tasmanian tiger's natural habitat was taken over by sheep farming decades ago, resulting in the animal's systematic elimination from the area. Since then naturalists working in the region have discovered no hard evidence of its survival, such as carcasses or tracks. Statement3: In spite of alleged sightings of the animal, the tasmanian tiger no longer exists.
hypothesis: Premise1: The tasmanian tiger did not move and adapt to a different region in response to the loss of habitat.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Every naturalist working in the tasmanian tiger's natural habitat has looked systematically for evidence of the tiger's survival.

premise: Statement1: Tax reformer : the proposed tax reform legislation is being criticized by political groups on the right for being too specific and by political groups on the left for being too vague. hypothesis: Premise1: The proposed legislation is not made up of a set of statements some of which are overly specific and some of which are overly vague.

premise: Statement1: If one wants to succeed, then one should act as though one were genuinely confident about one's abilities, even if one actually distrusts one's skills. Statement2: Success is much more easily obtained by those who genuinely believe themselves capable of succeeding than by those filled with self-doubts.
hypothesis: Premise1: Genuine confidence is often a by-product of pretended self-confidence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Those who convince others that they are capable of succeeding usually have few self-doubts.

premise: Statement1: A company has developed a new sensing device that, according to the company's claims, detects weak, ultralow-frequency electromagnetic signals associated with a beating heart. These signals, which pass through almost any physical obstruction, are purportedly detected by the device even at significant distances. Statement2: Therefore, if the company 's claims are true, their device will radically improve emergency teams ability to locate quickly people who are trapped within the wreckage of collapsed buildings.
hypothesis: Premise1: The device gives a distinctive reading when the signals it detects come from human beings rather than from any other living beings.

premise: Statement1: Virginia and her brother william disagree over when their father was born : virginia claims it was in 1935 and william claims it was in 1933. sent2: the hospital where their father was born has no records for 1933 but has complete records for 1935 -- records that do not include a birth record for their father. hypothesis: Premise1: Either virginia's claim or william's claim is correct.

premise: Statement1: Human skin gives off an array of gaseous substances, including carbon dioxide and lactic acid, both of which attract mosquitoes. However, neither of these two substances, whether alone or combined with one another, will attract mosquitoes as much as a bare human arm will, even in complete darkness, where a mosquito has no visual cues. Statement2: Therefore, some other gaseous substance given off by human skin also attracts mosquitoes.
hypothesis: Premise1: Mosquitoes are not attracted to humans by body heat.

premise: Statement1: Downtown petropolis boasted over 100 large buildings 5 years ago. Statement2: Since then, 60 of those buildings have been demolished. hypothesis: Premise1: Significantly fewer than 60 new large buildings have been built in downtown petropolis during the past 5 years.

premise: Statement1: Acme's bank loan must be immediately repaid in full if acme's earnings fall below $ 1 million per year. If acme has to repay the entire loan immediately, it will have to declare bankruptcy. hypothesis: Premise1: Acme's earnings for last year, when accurately stated, are below $1 million

premise: Statement1: Economist : in today's post-industrial economy, higher education is essential for achieving prosperity. Statement2: Without a college degree or higher, citizens do not have access to the highest quartile of salaries, and only individuals earning salaries in this highest quartile have enough disposable income to buy real estate and invest in long term assets.
hypothesis: Premise1: Prosperity has to include ownership of real estate or long term assets.

premise: Statement1: Companies wishing to boost sales of merchandise should use in-store displays to catch customers'attention. Statement2: According to a marketing study, today's busy shoppers have less time for coupon-clipping and pay little attention to direct-mail advertising ; instead, they make two-thirds of their buying decisions on the spot at the store.
hypothesis: Premise1: In-store displays that catch customers' attention increase the likelihood that customers will decide on the spot to buy the company's merchandise.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Coupons and direct-mail advertising were at one time more effective means of boosting sales of merchandise than they are now.

premise: Statement1: Art critic : nowadays, museum visitors seldom pause to look at a work of art for even a full minute. Statement2: They look, perhaps take a snapshot, and move on. Statement3: This tells us something about how our relationship to art has changed over time. People have become less willing to engage with works of art than they once were.
hypothesis: Premise1: The amount of time spent looking at a work of art is a reliable measure of engagement with that work
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Visitors would enjoy their museum experiences more if they took more time with individual works of art

premise: Statement1: Critic : people today place an especially high value on respect for others ; yet, in their comedy acts, many of today's most popular comedians display blatant disrespect for others. But when people fail to live up to the very ideals they hold in highest esteem, exaggeration of such failings often forms the basis of successful comedy. Statement2: Thus the current popularity of comedians who display disrespect in their acts is hardly surprising.
hypothesis: Premise1: People who value an ideal especially highly do not always succeed in living up to this ideal.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People today fail to live up to their own ideals more frequently than was the case in the past.

premise: Statement1: Politician : a major social problem is children hurting other children. The results of a recent experiment by psychologists establish that watching violent films is at least partly responsible for this aggressive behavior. Statement2: The psychologists conducted an experiment in which one group of children watched a film of people punching bobo the clown dolls. A second group of children was not shown the film. Afterward, both groups of children played together in a room containing a bobo doll. Most of the children who had seen the film punched the bobo doll, while most of the other children did not.
hypothesis: Premise1: The children who had been shown the film were found to be no more likely than the children who had not been shown the film to punch other children.

premise: Statement1: Margaret came home after a long day of work to find her front door open and her waste basket knocked over with all of the contents strewn about the kitchen floor. Statement3: Her husband took the dog with him to work all day.
hypothesis: Premise1: Margaret's husband brought the dog home on his lunch break.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Margaret's husband accidently knocked it over on his way out the door this morning.

premise: Statement1: Editorial : despite the fact that recent military success has made the prime minister personally popular, her party will lose the next election unless the economy is no longer in recession ; the electorate will blame the state of the economy on her, even though she is hardly responsible for it and her policies will be beneficial in the long run.
hypothesis: Premise1: The prime minister's party will not win the next election if the electorate believes the prime minister is responsible for a continuing recession.

premise: Statement1: Advertisement : the new reflex computer represents a conceptual advance. Statement2: Unlike traditional computers, the reflex has a built-in monitoring function that continuously checks all other computer operations and lets you know if they are malfunctioning in any way, thus preventing the loss of data. Statement3: With the reflex, therefore, you'll never lose data again!
hypothesis: Premise1: The monitoring function of the reflex is not subject to frequent unpredictable malfunctioning.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The reflex computer continues to process data while it is warning of a malfunction.

premise: Statement1: Therapist : cognitive psychotherapy focuses on changing a patient's conscious beliefs. Statement2: Thus, cognitive psychotherapy is likely to be more effective at helping patients overcome psychological problems than are forms of psychotherapy that focus on changing unconscious beliefs and desires, since only conscious beliefs are under the patient's direct conscious control.
hypothesis: Premise1: It is difficult for any form of psychotherapy to be effective without focusing on mental states that are under the patient's direct conscious control.

premise: Statement1: A scientific study provides evidence that crows are capable of recognizing threatening people and can even pass their concerns on to other crows. Statement2: Researchers wearing rubber caveman masks trapped wild crows and then released them in the same area. Years later, people wearing the same masks near where the crows had been trapped were shrieked at and dive-bombed by crows.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some of the crows that shrieked at and dive-bombed people wearing the masks were not among the crows that had been trapped
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Even in places where crows have never been captured, most crows will shriek at and dive-bomb people wearing caveman masks

premise: Statement1: Physiological research has uncovered disturbing evidence linking a number of structural disorders to jogging. Among the ailments seemingly connected with this now-popular sport are spinal disk displacements, stress fractures of the feet and ankles, knee and hip joint deterioration, and tendonitis. Statement2: Furthermore, these injuries do not occur exclusively among beginning runners -- veteran joggers suffer an equal percentage of injuries. Statement3: What the accumulating data suggest is that the human anatomy is not able to withstand the stresses of jogging.
hypothesis: Premise1: The link between jogging and certain structural disorders appears to be a causal one.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The jogger's level of experience is a factor determining the likelihood of a jogging injury.

premise: Sent1:" life expectancy" is the average age at death of the entire live-born population. Statement1: In the middle of the nineteenth century, life expectancy in north america was 40 years, whereas now it is nearly 80 years. Statement2: Thus, in those days, people must have been considered old at an age that we now consider the prime of life.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most of the gains in life expectancy in the last 150 years have come from reductions in the number of infants who die in their first year of life.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many of the people who live to an advanced age today do so only because of medical technology that was unknown in the nineteenth century.

premise: Statement1: Some political thinkers hope to devise a form of government in which every citizen's rights are respected. But such a form of government is impossible. Statement2: For any government must be defined and controlled by laws that determine its powers and limits ; and it is inevitable that some individuals will learn how to interpret these laws to gain a greater share of political power than others have.
hypothesis: Premise1: In any form of government, if anybody gains a greater share of political power than others have, then somebody's rights will be violated.

premise: Statement1: Anthropological studies indicate that distinct cultures differ in their moral codes. Statement2: Thus, as long as there are distinct cultures, there are no values shared across cultures.
hypothesis: Premise1: As a result of advancing technology and global communication, we will someday all share the same culture and the same values.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Although specific moral values differ across cultures, more general moral principles, such as "friendship is good," are common to all cultures.

premise: Statement1: Insurers and doctors are well aware that the incidence of lower-back injuries among office workers who spend long hours sitting is higher than that among people who regularly do physical work of a type known to place heavy stresses on the lower back. Statement2: This shows that office equipment and furniture are not properly designed to promote workers'health.
hypothesis: Premise1: Consistent physical exercise is one of the most effective ways to prevent or recover from lower-back injuries.

premise: Statement1: Correctly measuring the productivity of service workers is complex. What if more letters are lost or delayed per worker at the same time that more are delivered? Statement3: But is this really true?
hypothesis: Premise1: The quality of services rendered can appropriately be ignored in computing productivity.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The delivery of letters is the primary activity of the postal service.

premise: Statement1: Sparva, unlike treland's other provinces, requires automobile insurers to pay for any medical treatment sought by someone who has been involved in an accident ; in the other provinces, insurers pay for nonemergency treatment only if they preapprove the treatment. Statement2: Clearly, sparva's less restrictive policy must be the explanation for the fact that altogether insurers there pay for far more treatments after accidents than insurers in other provinces, even though sparva does not have the largest population.
hypothesis: Premise1: There are fewer traffic accidents annually in sparva than in any of the provinces of comparable or greater population.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The cost of medical care in sparva is higher than the national average.

premise: Statement1: Guidebook writer : i have visited hotels throughout the country and have noticed that in those built before 1930 the quality of the original carpentry work is generally superior to that in hotels built afterward. Statement2: Clearly carpenters working on hotels before 1930 typically worked with more skill, care, and effort than carpenters who have worked on hotels built subsequently.
hypothesis: Premise1: The better the quality of original carpentry in a building, the less likely that building is to fall into disuse and be demolished.

premise: Statement1: The principal of school x has proposed a plan that would add an hour-long study period to the end of the school day. Statement2: She claims that the extension would improve the school 's average score on the math section of the state assessment by allowing students more time to complete math homework that they would n't otherwise have time to complete.
hypothesis: Premise1: The core components of school x's math curriculum are not aligned with the topics tested on the state math assessment.

premise: Statement1: It has been said that authors who write in order to give pleasure can not impart to their readers the truth of their subject matter. That claim can not be true. Statement2: If it were, one could determine the truthfulness of a book simply by looking at its sales figures. If the book were very popular, one could reasonably conclude that it gave people pleasure and therefore that at least some of what is written in the book is not true.
hypothesis: Premise1: A book will not give its readers pleasure unless it was intended by its author to have that effect.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In many cases, a book's readers are unconcerned about the truth of the book's contents.

premise: Statement1: Economist : in free market systems, the primary responsibility of corporate executives is to determine a nation's industrial technology, the pattern of work organization, location of industry, and resource allocation. They also are the decision makers, though subject to significant consumer control, on what is to be produced and in what quantities. Statement2: In short, a large category of major decisions is turned over to business executives. Statement3: Thus, business executives have become public officials.
hypothesis: Premise1: Making decisions about patterns of work organization, resource allocation, and location of industry is not the core of a public official's job.

premise: Statement1: Statistics from the national booksellers association indicate that during the last five years most bookstores have started to experience declining revenues from the sale of fiction, despite national campaigns to encourage people to read more fiction. Statement2: Therefore, these reading campaigns have been largely unsuccessful.
hypothesis: Premise1: Mail order book clubs have enjoyed substantial growth in fiction sales throughout the last five years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: During the last five years the most profitable items in bookstores have been newspapers and periodicals rather than novels.

premise: Statement1: J.j. thomson, the discoverer of the electron and a recipient of the nobel prize in physics, trained many physicists, among them seven nobel prize winners, 32 fellows of the royal society of london, and 83 professors of physics. Statement2: This shows that the skills needed for creative research can be taught and learned.
hypothesis: Premise1: At least one of the eminent scientists trained by j. j. thomson was not a creative researcher before coming to study with him.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Scientists who go on to be the most successful researchers often receive their scientific education in classes taught by renowned research scientists.

premise: Statement1: Auditor : xyz, a construction company, purchased 20 new trucks 3 years ago, and there is no record of any of those trucks being sold last year. Statement2: Records indicate, however, that xyz sold off all of its diesel-powered trucks last year. hypothesis: Premise1: None of the 20 trucks was sold before last year.

premise: Statement1: Old rose varieties have less vibrant colors than modern rose varieties, but are superior in fragrance. Statement2: This situation has arisen because the modern rose varieties were developed to meet customer demand and marketed mainly through catalogs : colors, but not fragrances, can be photographed.
hypothesis: Premise1: Breeding roses to enhance one trait often has unanticipated consequences for other traits.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People in earlier times would have found the vibrant colors of modern roses too harsh.

premise: Statement1: Psychologist : it is well known that becoming angry often induces temporary incidents of high blood pressure. Statement2: A recent study further showed, however, that people who are easily angered are significantly more likely to have permanently high blood pressure than are people who have more tranquil personalities. Statement3: Coupled with the long-established fact that those with permanently high blood pressure are especially likely to have heart disease, the recent findings indicate that heart disease can result from psychological factors.
hypothesis: Premise1: The physiological factors that cause permanently high blood pressure generally make people quick to anger.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Those who are easily angered are less likely to recover fully from episodes of heart disease than are other people.

premise: Statement1: If a person chooses to walk rather than drive, there is one less vehicle emitting pollution into the air than there would be otherwise. Statement2: Therefore if people would walk whenever it is feasible for them to do so, then pollution will be greatly reduced.
hypothesis: Premise1: People sometimes drive when it is feasible to walk instead.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Taking public transportation rather than driving is not always feasible.

premise: Statement1: Researcher : people are able to tell whether a person is extroverted just by looking at pictures in which the person has a neutral expression. Statement2: Since people are also able to tell whether a chimpanzee behaves dominantly just by looking at a picture of the chimpanzee's expressionless face, and since both humans and chimpanzees are primates, we conclude that this ability is probably not acquired solely through culture but rather as a result of primate biology.
hypothesis: Premise1: Extroversion in people and dominant behavior in chimpanzees are both indicators of a genetic predisposition to assertiveness.

premise: Statement1: Businessman : sales are the most important part of any business. Statement2: Marketing and communications are never as effective as a strong sales team. Statement3: Persuasive salesmen can sell as many units of an inferior product as average salesmen with better quality products. Statement4: Our company should eliminate every department except the sales team.
hypothesis: Premise1: The sales team's effectiveness depends on contributions from the other departments.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The company would be better off with only a sales team.

premise: Statement1: Although there is no record of poet edmund spenser 's parentage, we do know that as a youth spenser attended the merchant tailors'school in london for a period between 1560 and 1570. records from this time indicate that the merchant tailors'guild then had only three members named spenser : robert spenser, listed as a gentleman ; nicholas spenser, elected the guild 's warden in 1568 ; and john spenser, listed as a" journeyman cloth-maker. " Statement2: Of these, the last was likely the least affluent of the three-and most likely edmund 's father, since school accounting records list edmund as a scholar who attended the school at a reduced fee.
hypothesis: Premise1: The merchant tailors' school did not reduce its fees for the children of the more affluent guild members.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Anybody in sixteenth century london who made clothing professionally would have had to be a member of the merchant tailors' guild.

premise: Statement1: The legislature of the philippines voted recently to abolish the death penalty. In contrast, the death penalty remains legal in the united states. Statement2: The difference in the legality of capital punishment demonstrates that the majority of american citizens believe in the death penalty, while the majority of filipino citizens do not.
hypothesis: Premise1: The legal status of capital punishment in the united states and the philippines aligns with how the majority of citizens in those respective countries view the death penalty.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are not strong voices opposing the death penalty in the united states.

premise: Statement1: Robin : when a region's economy is faltering, many people lose their jobs. Statement2: As a result, spending on consumer goods declines, leading in turn to more lost jobs and a worsening of the economy. Statement3: Eventually, the economy becomes so bad that prices collapse ; the lower prices encourage people to increase spending on consumer goods, and this higher spending results in economic improvement. Statement4: Terry : sent5: people can not increase their spending if they have no jobs and no money for anything other than basic necessities, so price collapses can not lead to economic improvement.
hypothesis: Premise1: Even people who do not lose their jobs spend less in bad economic times and thus have savings available to spend when prices collapse.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People who have lost their jobs must continue to buy some basic goods such as food, even during bad economic times.

premise: Statement1: Opponents of peat harvesting in this country argue that it would alter the ecological balance of our peatrich wetlands and that, as a direct consequence of this, much of the country's water supply would be threatened with contamination. But this can not be true, for in ireland, where peat has been harvested for centuries, the water supply is not contaminated. Statement2: We can safely proceed with the harvesting of peat.
hypothesis: Premise1: The original ecology of the peat-harvesting areas of ireland was virtually identical to that of the undisturbed wetlands of this country.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The activities of other industries in coming years are likely to have adverse effects on the water supply of this country.

premise: Statement1: Older united states automobiles have been identified as contributing disproportionately to global air pollution. The requirement in many jurisdictions that automobiles pass emission-control inspections has had the effect of taking many such automobiles out of service in the united states, as they fail inspection and their owners opt to buy newer automobiles. Statement2: Thus the burden of pollution such older united states automobiles contribute to the global atmosphere will be gradually reduced over the next decade.
hypothesis: Premise1: There is a thriving market for used older united states automobiles that are exported to regions that have no emission-control regulations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Even if all the older automobiles in the united states were retired from service, air pollution from united states automobiles could still increase if the total number of automobiles in use should increase significantly.

premise: Statement1: Builder : within ten years, most of the new homes constructed in north america will have steel frameworks rather than wood ones. Statement2: After all, two-by-fours and two-by-sixes -- the sizes of lumber most commonly used in home construction -- are deteriorating in quality and increasing in cost, while environment-friendly steel is decreasing in cost. In addition, unlike wood, steel will not warp, rot, or split.
hypothesis: Premise1: Training home construction workers to work with steel is very costly.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Steel-framed homes do not have to be treated with pesticides or other chemicals that can contribute to indoor air pollution.

premise: Statement1: People who have political power tend to see new technologies as a means of extending or protecting their power, whereas they generally see new ethical arguments and ideas as a threat to it. Statement2: Therefore, technical ingenuity usually brings benefits to those who have this ingenuity, whereas ethical inventiveness brings only pain to those who have this inventiveness.
hypothesis: Premise1: Politically powerful people tend to reward those who they believe are useful to them and to punish those who they believe are a threat.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: New technologies are often used by people who strive to defeat those who currently have political power.

premise: Statement1: When amphibians first appeared on earth millions of years ago, the amount of ultraviolet radiation penetrating earth's atmosphere was much greater than it is today. Statement2: Therefore, current dramatic decreases in amphibian populations can not be the result of recent increases in ultraviolet radiation penetrating earth's atmosphere.
hypothesis: Premise1: The eggs of modern amphibians are not significantly more vulnerable to ultraviolet radiation than the eggs of the first amphibians were.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Modern amphibians are not as likely as the first amphibians were to live in habitats that shield them from ultraviolet radiation.

premise: Statement1: Psychiatrist : in treating first-year students at this university, i have noticed that those reporting the highest levels of spending on recreation score at about the same level on standard screening instruments for anxiety and depression as those reporting the lowest levels of spending on recreation. Statement2: This suggests that the first-year students with high levels of spending on recreation could reduce that spending without increasing their anxiety or depression.
hypothesis: Premise1: Among adults between the ages of 40 and 60, increased levels of spending on recreation are strongly correlated with decreased levels of anxiety and depression.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Several of the psychiatrist's patients who are first-year students at the university have reduced their spending on recreation from very high levels to very low levels without increasing their anxiety or depression.

premise: Statement1: Scientist : there is a lot of concern that human behavior may be responsible for large-scale climate change. Statement2: But this should be seen as more of an opportunity than a problem. Statement3: If human behavior is responsible for climate change, then we can control future climate change to make it less extreme than previous climate shifts.
hypothesis: Premise1: Human beings can control the aspects of their behavior that have an impact on climate change.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: At least some previous large-scale climate changes have been caused by human behavior.

premise: Statement1: More and more computer programs that provide solutions to mathematical problems in engineering are being produced, and it is thus increasingly unnecessary for practicing engineers to have a thorough understanding of fundamental mathematical principles. Statement2: Consequently, in training engineers who will work in industry, less emphasis should be placed on mathematical principles, so that space in the engineering curriculum will be available for other important subjects.
hypothesis: Premise1: The effective use of computer programs that provide solutions to mathematical problems in engineering requires an understanding of mathematical principles.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Development of composites and other such new materials has meant that the curriculum for engineers who will work in industry must allow time for teaching the properties of these materials.

premise: Statement1: Chef : this mussel recipe's first step is to sprinkle the live mussels with cornmeal. Statement2: The cornmeal is used to clean them out : they take the cornmeal in and eject the sand that they contain. Statement3: But i can skip this step, because the mussels available at seafood markets are farm raised and therefore do not contain sand.
hypothesis: Premise1: The mussels the chef is using for the mussel recipe came from a seafood market.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The chef's mussel recipe was written before farm-raised mussels became available.

premise: Statement1: Drama critic : audiences will enjoy warner's latest play, about the disintegration of a family, because of the superb acting. Statement2: The chemistry between the actors could not be more intense, and ziegler, who plays the child, captures convincingly the guilt and despair experienced as the family members grow more estranged from each other.
hypothesis: Premise1: The plot of the play is similar in some respects to plots of warner's other works.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Generally, audiences enjoy romantic comedies but find tragedies upsetting.

premise: Statement1: In a study of the effect of radiation from nuclear weapons plants on people living in areas near them, researchers compared death rates in the areas near the plants with death rates in areas that had no such plants. Statement2: Finding no difference in these rates, the researchers concluded that radiation from the nuclear weapons plants poses no health hazards to people living near them.
hypothesis: Premise1: Exposure to nuclear radiation can cause many serious diseases that do not necessarily result in death.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Nuclear power plants were not included in the study.

premise: Statement1: When a certain gland becomes cancerous in humans, it produces high levels of a particular protein. Statement2: A blood test can determine the level of this protein well before a cancer of the gland could be detected by other means. Statement3: Some doctors recommend that aggressive anticancer treatment should be begun as early as possible for anyone who is tested and is found to have high levels of the protein.
hypothesis: Premise1: Enlargement of the gland, a common condition infrequently associated with cancer, results in high levels of the protein.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The blood test for the protein has been in use for some time to monitor the condition of patients who have been diagnosed as having cancer of the gland.

premise: Statement1: In north america there has been an explosion of public interest in, and enjoyment of, opera over the last three decades. Statement2: The evidence of this explosion is that of the 70 or so professional opera companies currently active in north america, 45 were founded over the course of the last 30 years.
hypothesis: Premise1: There were fewer than 45 professional opera companies that had been active 30 years ago and that ceased operations during the last 30 years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: All of the 70 professional opera companies are commercially viable operations.

premise: Statement1: When a society undergoes slow change, its younger members find great value in the advice of its older members. But when a society undergoes rapid change, young people think that little in the experience of their elders is relevant to them, and so do not value their advice. Statement2: Thus, we may measure the rate at which a society is changing by measuring the amount of deference its younger members show to their elders.
hypothesis: Premise1: The deference young people show to their elders varies according to how much the young value their elders' advice.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: How much deference young people show to their elders depends on how much of the elders' experience is practically useful to them.

premise: Statement1: In 1955, legislation in a certain country gave the government increased control over industrial workplace safety conditions. Among the high-risk industries in that country, the likelihood that a worker will suffer a serious injury has decreased since 1955. sent3: the legislation, therefore, has increased overall worker safety within high-risk industries.
hypothesis: Premise1: Because of technological innovation, most workplaces in the high-risk industries do not require as much unprotected interaction between workers and heavy machinery as they did in 1955.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The number of work-related injuries occurring within industries not considered high-risk has increased annually since 1955.

premise: Statement1: The indonesian mimic octopus is able to camouflage itself from predators by blending into the background. Sometimes, though, even when a predator is nearby, the mimic octopus takes on a color pattern that is not present in the immediate environment. Statement2: A marine biologist of cephalopods claims that this behavior is a sign of an octopus engaging in play, meaning that the behavior does not confer any specific advantage.
hypothesis: Premise1: The color pattern an indonesian mimic octopus assumes when a predator is nearby, but that does not match its immediate environment, has been found to parallel that of various jellyfish venomous to the octopus's predators.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The indonesian mimic octopus engages in other forms of behavior that seem to serve no purpose, yet several marine biologists are reluctant to describe such behavior as play.

premise: Statement1: Journalist : the advice of social scientists is frequently overlooked by politicians making social policy. Statement2: Because it is not unreasonable to discount scientific assertions backed by weak evidence, politicians should not generally be criticized for ignoring social science, for social scientists, unlike physical scientists, seldom agree on the claims made even within their own specialty.
hypothesis: Premise1: The failure of scientists to agree that a claim within their specialty is true can indicate that the evidence for the claim is not strong.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Politicians should follow the advice of experts on issues about which those experts agree among themselves.

premise: Statement1: After the first bite, the mystery recipe contest judge has detected cumin, red pepper flakes, and lemon grass in the winning dish. These seasonings have a tendency to be potent, producing distinct flavors. Statement2: Since the quantity of any seasoning in a recipe depends directly on how potent that seasoning tastes in the dish, the judge has determined that the dish contains cumin, lemon grass, and red pepper flakes, in order of decreasing relative quantity.
hypothesis: Premise1: The bite that the judge took was representative of the whole dish.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The winning dish contains at least one other seasoning ingredient besides cumin, lemon grass, and red pepper flakes.

premise: Statement1: Kernland imposes a high tariff on the export of unprocessed cashew nuts in order to ensure that the nuts are sold to domestic processing plants. If the tariff were lifted and unprocessed cashews were sold at world market prices, more farmers could profit by growing cashews. Statement2: However, since all the processing plants are in urban areas, removing the tariff would seriously hamper the government's effort to reduce urban unemployment over the next five years.
hypothesis: Premise1: A lack of profitable crops is driving an increasing number of small farmers in kernland off their land and into the cities.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Buying unprocessed cashews at lower than world market prices enables cashew processors in kernland to sell processed nuts at competitive prices.

premise: Statement1: Thirty years ago, the percentage of the british people who vacationed in foreign countries was very small compared with the large percentage of the british population who travel abroad for vacations now. Foreign travel is, and always has been, expensive from britain. Statement2: Therefore, british people must have, on average, more money to spend on vacations now than they did 30 years ago.
hypothesis: Premise1: If more of the british people of 30 years ago had had enough money to vacation abroad, more would have done so.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the percentage of british people vacationing abroad was lower 30 years ago, then the british people of 30 years ago must have spent more money on domestic vacations.

premise: Statement1: Young people believe efforts to reduce pollution, poverty, and war are doomed to failure. This pessimism is probably harmful to humanity's future, because people lose motivation to work for goals they think are unrealizable. Statement2: We must do what we can to prevent this loss of motivation and therefore must enable our children to believe that better futures are possible.
hypothesis: Premise1: Enabling people to believe that better futures are possible will help prevent the loss of motivation that results from pessimistic beliefs about the future.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If future generations believe that the future can be better, then pollution, poverty, and war will be eliminated.

premise: Statement1: Editorialist : to ensure justice in the legal system, citizens must be capable of criticizing anyone involved in determining the punishment of criminals. Statement2: But when the legal system's purpose is seen as deterrence, the system falls into the hands of experts whose specialty is to assess how potential lawbreakers are affected by the system's punishments. Statement3: Because most citizens lack knowledge about such matters, justice is not then ensured in the legal system.
hypothesis: Premise1: Citizens without knowledge about how the legal system's punishments affect potential lawbreakers are incapable of criticizing experts in that area.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In order to be just, a legal system must consider the effect that punishment will have on individual criminals.

premise: Statement1: The iliad and the odyssey were both attributed to homer in ancient times. But these two poems differ greatly in tone and vocabulary and in certain details of the fictional world they depict. Statement2: So they are almost certainly not the work of the same poet.
hypothesis: Premise1: Works known to have been written by the same modern writer are as different from each other in the respects mentioned as are the iliad and the odyssey.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Both the iliad and the odyssey were the result of an extended process of oral composition in which many poets were involved.

premise: Statement1: Nutritionist : recently a craze has developed for home juicers, $ 300 machines that separate the pulp of fruits and vegetables from the juice they contain. Statement2: Outrageous claims are being made about the benefits of these devices : drinking the juice they produce is said to help one lose weight or acquire a clear complexion, to aid digestion, and even to prevent cancer. Statement3: But there is no indication that juice separated from the pulp of the fruit or vegetable has any properties that it does not have when unseparated. Statement4: Save your money. If you want carrot juice, eat a carrot.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most people find it much easier to consume a given quantity of nutrients in liquid form than to eat solid foods containing the same quantity of the same nutrients.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: To most people who would be tempted to buy a home juicer, $300 would not be a major expense.

premise: Statement1: The city of workney, in raising bus fares from $ 1.00 to $ 1.25, proposed that 18 fare tokens be sold for $ 20.00 to alleviate the extra burden of the fare increase on the city's low-income residents. Statement2: Critics suggested alternatively that 9 fare tokens be sold for $ 10.00, because a $ 20.00 outlay would be prohibitive for low-income riders.
hypothesis: Premise1: The outlay of $10. 00 for the purchase of 9 fare tokens would not be prohibitive for low-income bus riders.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Low-income residents of workney will continue to ride the buses in the same numbers despite the fare increase.

premise: Statement1: Millions of irreplaceable exhibits in natural history museums are currently allowed to decay. Statement2: Yet without analyses of eggs from museums, the studies linking pesticides with the decline of birds of prey would have been impossible. Statement3: Therefore, funds must be raised to preserve at least those exhibits that will be most valuable to science in the future.
hypothesis: Premise1: It can be known at this time what data will be of most use to scientific investigators in the future
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If a museum exhibit is irreplaceable, its preservation is of an importance that overrides economic considerations

premise: Statement1: Analyst : the increasing complexity of computers may lead those who pursue a career in computer programming to think that job security and higher wages can be attained by becoming more specialized as the field becomes more complex. Even though specialists earn higher wages than generalists within computer programming, this move is ill-advised because one risks specializing in a technology that will become obsolete. Statement2: Consider the plight of people who used to repair eight-track tape players.
hypothesis: Premise1: The current technological knowledge of most specialists within computer programming could also be applied to many technologies that will replace present ones.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The average wages earned by generalists within computer programming will never be as high as the average wages earned by specialists within computer programming.

premise: Statement1: Parent : the city education department is unable to distinguish between annoyances and important problems. Statement2: For instance, prohibiting students from having cell phones is an overreaction. If a student uses one and thus interferes with instruction, confiscate it. Statement3: All in all, we need educational leadership that can solve problems, not create them.
hypothesis: Premise1: Students having cell phones does not constitute an important problem for the city schools.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Students have no need for cell phones in school.

premise: Statement1: For similar cars and drivers, automobile insurance for collision damage has always cost more in greatport than in fairmont. Police studies, however, show that cars owned by greatport residents are, on average, slightly less likely to be involved in a collision than cars in fairmont. Statement2: Clearly, therefore, insurance companies are making a greater profit on collision-damage insurance in greatport than in fairmont.
hypothesis: Premise1: Repairing typical collision damage does not cost more in greatport than in fairmont.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are no more motorists in greatport than in fairmont.

premise: Statement1: Researchers examined 100 people suffering from herniated disks in their backs. Five of them were found to have a defect in a particular gene. The researchers also examined 100 people who had no problems with the disks in their backs ; none had the genetic defect. Statement2: They concluded that the genetic defect increases the likelihood of herniated disks.
hypothesis: Premise1: When the researchers examined the families of the 5 subjects who had the defective gene, they found that 30 family members also had the defective gene, and each of them suffered from herniated disks.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The researchers also examined a group of 100 people who did not have the defective gene; 80 were found to have herniated disks in their backs.

premise: Statement1: Biologists studying plant life in the prehistoric rainforest by utilizing samples of ancient ice and fossilized evidence of biological processes found that most of the plants removed an astonishingly small amount of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Statement2: One biologist found that a certain tree currently found in the rainforest absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere at a greater rate than any tree on the planet. Statement3: The biologist hypothesized that the prevalence of this tree in the prehistoric rainforest accounts for the other plants'failure to remove larger amounts of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
hypothesis: Premise1: The trees currently found in the rainforest are largely hybrids and a product of crossbreeding that occurred in the late twentieth century.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has remained steady since prehistoric times.

premise: Statement1: Lobsters and other crustaceans eaten by humans are more likely to contract gill diseases when sewage contaminates their water. Under a recent proposal, millions of gallons of local sewage each day would be rerouted many kilometers offshore. Statement2: Although this would substantially reduce the amount of sewage in the harbor where lobsters are caught, the proposal is pointless, because hardly any lobsters live long enough to be harmed by those diseases.
hypothesis: Premise1: Humans often become ill as a result of eating lobsters with gill diseases.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Contaminants in the harbor other than sewage are equally harmful to lobsters.

premise: Statement1: Twenty years ago the republic of rosinia produced nearly 100 million tons of potatoes, but last year the harvest barely reached 60 million tons. Statement2: Agricultural researchers, who have failed to develop new higher-yielding strains of potatoes, are to blame for this decrease, since they have been concerned only with their own research and not with the needs of rosinia.
hypothesis: Premise1: Strains of potatoes most commonly grown in rosinia could not have produced the yields last year that they once did.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Agricultural research in rosinia is funded by government grants.

premise: Statement1: People who have specialized knowledge about a scientific or technical issue are systematically excluded from juries for trials where that issue is relevant. Statement2: Thus, trial by jury is not a fair means of settling disputes involving such issues.
hypothesis: Premise1: The more a juror knows about a particular scientific or technical issue involved in a trial, the more likely it is that the juror will be prejudiced in favor of one of the litigating parties before the trial begins.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The more complicated the issue being litigated, the less likely it is that a juror without specialized knowledge of the field involved will be able to comprehend the testimony being given.

premise: Statement1: Mayor pollister always repays his contributors by passing the laws they desire. Statement2: Since mayor pollister recently passed the city's first clean air act, he must have had a contributor to repay.
hypothesis: Premise1: Mayor pollister would not have passed the law unless he was doing so for a contributor.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The city will be benefited by the passage of the clean air act.

premise: Statement1: It is the mark of a superior conductor that he or she has the authority to insist, even with a top orchestra, that rehearsal work must be intensified. Statement2: This authority can not simply be claimed ; the conductor must earn it by winning the orchestra's respect for the artistic interpretations he or she is currently pursuing.
hypothesis: Premise1: Top orchestras can appreciate the merits of an interpretation even before they have brought it to full realization.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Top orchestras are always ready to put in additional work on rehearsals if the conductor considers additional rehearsing necessary.

premise: Statement1: Outsourcing is the practice of obtaining from an independent supplier a product or service that a company has previously provided for itself. Statement2: Since a company 's chief objective is to realize the highest possible year-end profits, any product or service that can be obtained from an independent supplier for less than it would cost the company to provide the product or service on its own should be outsourced.
hypothesis: Premise1: Successful outsourcing requires a company to provide its suppliers with information about its products and plans that can fall into the hands of its competitors and give them a business advantage.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: When a company decides to use an independent supplier for a product or service, the independent supplier sometimes hires members of the company's staff who formerly made the product or provided the service that the independent supplier now supplies.

premise: Statement1: In 1960's studies of rats, scientists found that crowding increases the number of attacks among the animals significantly. Statement2: But in recent experiments in which rhesus monkeys were placed in crowded conditions, although there was an increase in instances of" coping" behavior-such as submissive gestures and avoidance of dominant individuals-attacks did not become any more frequent. Statement3: Therefore it is not likely that, for any species of monkey, crowding increases aggression as significantly as was seen in rats.
hypothesis: Premise1: Rhesus monkeys respond with aggression to a wider range of stimuli than any other monkeys do.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some of the coping behavior displayed by rhesus monkeys is similar to behavior rhesus monkeys use to bring to an end an attack that has begun.

premise: Statement1: Psychologist : identical twins are virtually the same genetically. Statement2: Moreover, according to some studies, identical twins separated at birth and brought up in vastly different environments show a strong tendency to report similar ethical beliefs, dress in the same way, and have similar careers. Statement3: Thus, many of our inclinations must be genetic in origin, and not subject to environmental influences.
hypothesis: Premise1: Identical twins who grow up together tend to develop different beliefs, tastes, and careers in order to differentiate themselves from each other.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: While some studies of identical twins separated at birth reveal a high percentage of similar personality traits, they also show a few differences.

premise: Statement1: In response to viral infection, the immune systems of mice typically produce antibodies that destroy the virus by binding to proteins on its surface. Statement2: Mice infected with the herpesvirus generally develop keratitis, a degenerative disease affecting part of the eye. Statement3: Since proteins on the surface of cells in this part of the eye closely resemble those on the herpesvirus surface, scientists hypothesize that these cases of keratitis are caused by antibodies to the herpesvirus.
hypothesis: Premise1: Mice infected with a new strain of the herpesvirus that has different surface proteins did not develop keratitis
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Mice that are infected with the herpesvirus but do not develop keratitis produce as many antibodies as infected mice that do develop keratitis

premise: Statement1: Automobile dealer's advertisement : the highway traffic safety institute reports that the pz 1000 has the fewest injuries per accident of any car in its class. Statement2: This shows that the pz 1000 is one of the safest cars available today.
hypothesis: Premise1: Cars in the class to which the pz 1000 belongs are more likely to be involved in accidents than are other types of cars.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The difference between the number of injuries per accident for the pz 1000 and that for other cars in its class is quite pronounced.

premise: Statement1: A student has taken twelve courses and received a b in a majority of them. Statement2: The student is now taking another course and will probably, given her record, receive a b in it.
hypothesis: Premise1: The student previously studied alone but is receiving help from several outstanding students during the present course.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The current course is a continuation of one of the twelve courses in which the student received a b.

premise: Statement1: Building a space station, in which astronauts would live for a considerable time, is essential even if the space station project were to contribute no new knowledge about space or earth that could not otherwise be obtained. Statement2: For future missions to explore mars, we will need the medical knowledge that the space station project will give us about the limits of human capacities to live in spacecraft for an extended time.
hypothesis: Premise1: The exploration of mars will be carried out by people traveling in spacecraft and not by robots alone
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Living in a spaceship for an extended time presents insurmountable medical problems

premise: Statement1: Curator : since ancient times, the fine arts were developed and sustained with the aid of large subsidies from the aristocracies and religious institutions that were the public sectors of their day ; it is doubtful that the arts would have survived without these subsidies. Statement2: Clearly, contemporary societies should fulfill their obligation as stewards of cultural heritage without the assistance of aristocracies or religious institutions, so governments must help finance the maintenance, advancement, and enrichment of the fine arts today.
hypothesis: Premise1: Serving as stewards of cultural heritage requires that contemporary societies help to maintain the fine arts.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Maintenance, advancement, and enrichment of the fine arts in any era require governmental subsidies.

premise: Statement1: The supernova event of 1987 is interesting in that there is still no evidence of the neutron star that current theory says should have remained after a supernova of that size. This is in spite of the fact that many of the most sensitive instruments ever developed have searched for the tell-tale pulse of radiation that neutron stars emit. Statement2: Thus, current theory is wrong in claiming that supernovas of a certain size always produce neutron stars.
hypothesis: Premise1: Sensitive astronomical instruments have detected neutron stars much farther away than the location of the 1987 supernova.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most supernova remnants that astronomers have detected have a neutron star nearby.

premise: Statement1: Once children begin to read they acquire new vocabulary most naturally as the indirect result of reading difficult, challenging material. Statement2: The major alternative -- direct studying of new vocabulary items -- is less natural ; most people are not so inclined. From age six to age thirteen, children learn thousands of new words a year, mostly through reading ; direct studying accounts for less than one-tenth of all new words learned. Statement3: So it is bound to be inefficient for young students to learn new vocabulary by direct studying.
hypothesis: Premise1: Children from age six to age thirteen spend vastly much more time reading than they spend directly studying new vocabulary.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Reading difficult material sometimes leaves one confused as to the meaning of certain words.

premise: Statement1: Electronic cigarettes should not be subject to the same regulation as other products that contain nicotine. Statement2: Recent studies indicate that electronic cigarettes help people quit smoking by providing nicotine without the harmful tar and additive chemicals. Statement3: Although electronic cigarettes also contain their own additives, they are much less harmful in the short-term than traditional cigarettes. Statement4: People who smoke electronic cigarettes are ten times less likely to die from cancer than smokers of traditional cigarettes.
hypothesis: Premise1: The current regulations are designed to prevent children from using nicotine.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The additives in electronic cigarettes have not been tested as thoroughly as those in traditional cigarettes.

premise: Statement1: Global ecological problems reduce to the problem of balancing supply and demand. Statement2: Supply is strictly confined by the earth's limitations. Demand, however, is essentially unlimited, as there are no limits on the potential demands made by humans. Statement3: The natural tendency for there to be an imbalance between demand and sustainable supply is the source of these global problems. Statement4: Therefore, any solutions require reducing current human demand.
hypothesis: Premise1: Actual human demand exceeds the earth's sustainable supply.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Supply and demand tend to balance themselves in the long run.

premise: Statement1: Fares on the city-run public buses in greenville are subsidized by city tax revenues, but among the beneficiaries of the low fares are many people who commute from outside the city to jobs in greenville. Statement2: Some city councillors argue that city taxes should be used primarily to benefit the people who pay them, and therefore that bus fares should be raised enough to cover the cost of the service.

hypothesis: Premise1: Elementary and middle school students who used to buy high-calorie soft drinks at school will not bring them to school or drink extra high-calorie beverages at home as a substitute.

premise: Statement1: People who browse the web for medical information often can not discriminate between scientifically valid information and quackery. premise: Statement1: In many families adults speak to babies in simplified language. Yet constantly repeating simple phrases like" nice kitty. See the kitty? " Does not provide extra help to children in learning a language. premise: Statement1: Speaker : contemporary business firms need to recognize that avoiding social responsibility leads to the gradual erosion of power. Statement3: Though the" long run" may require decades or even centuries in some instances, society ultimately acts to reduce power when society thinks it is not being used responsibly. hypothesis: Premise1: Public relations programs can cause society to consider an institution socially responsible even when it is not.

premise: Statement1: A recent study of perfect pitch -- the ability to identify the pitch of an isolated musical note -- found that a high percentage of people who have perfect pitch are related to someone else who has it. hypothesis: Premise1: People who have relatives with perfect pitch generally receive no more musical training than do others.

premise: Statement1: Since jackie is such a big fan of moral vacuum's music, she will probably like the cruel herd's new album. premise: Statement1: A table made entirely from the trunk of a tree said to have lived a thousand years was recently claimed to be that of a much younger tree. Statement2: In order to rebut this charge, the craftsman summoned a team of dendrochronologists to prove that the tree lived to be at least to 1,000 years old. Statement3: Dendrochronology, or the technique of using tree rings to date wood, is based on the fact that for each passing year a tree develops exactly one ring, as seen in a horizontal cross-section of the trunk. Statement4: Given that dendrochronology is accurate for trees that lived less than 2,000 total years, the dendrochronologists will be able to determine whether the work comes from a tree that lived to be at least 1,000 years old.
hypothesis: Premise1: The wood used in the table is large enough to contain a span of one thousand tree rings.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The tree was not less than 1, 000 years old when it was cut down.

premise: Statement1: Employer : in the current economic climate, the best way to run a business is to pay employees the least amount possible to do the job. Statement2: The supply of labor is far outpacing demand since the number of college graduates increases every year and the average age of retirement is also increasing. Statement3: Even if the employee is unhappy, he or she will often remain on the job due to the competition in the job market. Keeping payroll costs low allows more resources to be devoted to innovation, delivering a higher quality product to customers.
hypothesis: Premise1: Automation is the leading cause for unemployment.

premise: Statement1: The french novelist colette ( 1873-1954 ) has been widely praised for the vividness of her language. Statement2: But many critics complain that her novels are indifferent to important moral questions. This charge is unfair. Statement3: Each of her novels is a poetic condensation of a major emotional crisis in the life of an ordinary person of her time. Such emotional crises almost invariably raise important moral questions.

premise: Statement1: If legislators are to enact laws that benefit constituents, they must be sure to consider what the consequences of enacting a proposed law will actually be. premise: Statement1: Manufacturing plants in arundia have recently been acquired in substantial numbers by investors from abroad. premise: Statement1: There have been no new cases of naturally occurring polio in north america in recent years. premise: Statement1: Electrical engineers have repeatedly demonstrated that the best solid-state amplifiers are indistinguishable from the best vacuum-tube amplifiers with respect to the characteristics commonly measured in evaluating the quality of an amplifier's musical reproduction. premise: Statement1: Press secretary : our critics claim that the president's recent highway project cancellations demonstrate a vindictive desire to punish legislative districts controlled by opposition parties. premise: Statement1: Astronomer : in most cases in which a planet has been detected orbiting a distant star, the planet's orbit is distinctly oval, whereas the orbits of earth and several other planets around our sun are approximately circular. premise: Statement1: Activist : all drugs should be legalized. premise: Statement1: Columnist : the relief from the drudgery of physical labor that much modern technology affords its users renders them dependent on this technology, and, more importantly, on the elaborate energy systems required to run it. premise: Statement1: Surviving seventeenth-century dutch landscapes attributed to major artists now equal in number those attributed to minor ones. premise: Statement1: Paperback books wear out more quickly than hardcover books do, but paperback books cost much less. premise: Statement1: English and the austronesian language mbarbaram both use the word" dog" for canines. Statement2: These two languages are unrelated, and since speakers of the two languages only came in contact with one another long after the word" dog" was first used in this way in either language, neither language could have borrowed the word from the other. Statement3: Thus this case shows that sometimes when languages share words that are similar in sound and meaning the similarity is due neither to language relatedness nor to borrowing.

premise: Statement1: Insect infestations in certain cotton-growing regions of the world have caused dramatic increases in the price of cotton on the world market. hypothesis: Premise1: Tests of a newly developed, inexpensive pesticide have shown it to be both environmentally safe and effective against the insects that have infested cotton crops.

premise: Statement1: Researcher : it is commonly believed that species belonging to the same biological order, such as rodents, descended from a single common ancestor. premise: Statement1: Loss of the gocha mangrove forests has caused coastal erosion, reducing fish populations and requiring the gocha fishing cooperative ( gfc ) to partially fund dredging and new shore facilities. premise: Statement1: Editorial : in ledland, unemployed adults receive government assistance. premise: Statement1: Because it was long thought that few people would watch lengthy televised political messages, most televised political advertisements, like commercial advertisements, took the form of short messages. Statement4: Clearly, then, many more people are interested in watching lengthy televised political messages than was previously thought.

premise: Statement1: Fossilized teeth of an extinct species of herbivorous great ape have on them phytoliths, which are microscopic petrified remains of plants. hypothesis: Premise1: Plants of every type eaten by the apes left phytoliths on their teeth.

premise: Statement1: Two lakes in the pawpaw mountains, quapaw and highwater, were suffering from serious declines in their fish populations ten years ago. premise: Statement1: Crops can be traded on the futures market before they are harvested.
