premise: Statement1: Early in the twentieth century, lake konfa became very polluted. Statement2: Recently fish populations have recovered as release of industrial pollutants has declined and the lake's waters have become cleaner. Statement3: Fears are now being voiced that the planned construction of an oil pipeline across the lake's bottom might revive pollution and cause the fish population to decline again. Statement4: However, a technology for preventing leaks is being installed. Statement5: Therefore, provided this technology is effective, the fears are groundless.
hypothesis: Premise1: The pipeline's construction will disturb sediments at the bottom of the lake, dispersing into the water toxic pollutants that have settled there.

premise: Statement1: Certain groups of asian snails include both" left-handed" and" right-handed" species, with shells coiling to the left and right, respectively. Statement2: Some left-handed species have evolved from right-handed ones. Statement3: Also, researchers found that snaileating snakes in the same habitat have asymmetrical jaws, allowing them to grasp right-handed snail shells more easily. Statement4: If these snakes ate more right-handed snails over time, this would have given left-handed snails an evolutionary advantage over right-handed snails, with the left handed snails eventually becoming a new species. Statement5: Thus, the snakes'asymmetrical jaws probably helped drive the emergence of the left-handed snail species.
hypothesis: Premise1: Experiments show that the snail-eating snakes in this habitat fail more often in trying to eat left-handed snails than in trying to eat right-handed snails.

premise: Statement1: Economist : the wages of many of the lowest-paid corporate employees in this country would be protected from cuts by enacting a maximum wage law that prohibits executives at any corporation from earning more than, say, 50 times what the corporation's lowest-paid employees in this country earn. A maximum wage law would remove this incentive for these executives to cut the wages of their lowest-paid employees. Statement2: Currently, some executives try to increase corporate profits -- and their own salaries -- by cutting the pay and benefits of their corporations'employees.

premise: Statement1: Readers give more credibility and respond more positively to negative restaurant reviews if the critics begin their reviews by discussing the things they enjoyed about the restaurant. hypothesis: Premise1: Newspapers and magazines will often only publish the beginning of a restaurant review to save space.

premise: Statement1: A recent study showed that the immune system blood cells of the study's participants who drank tea but no coffee took half as long to respond to germs as did the blood cells of participants who drank coffee but no tea. premise: Statement1: Data from satellite photographs of the tropical rain forest in melonia show that last year the deforestation rate of this environmentally sensitive zone was significantly lower than in previous years. premise: Statement1: During its caterpillar stage, the leopard magpie moth feeds on a plant called the natal grass cycad and by so doing laces its body with macrozamin, a toxin that makes the moth highly unpalatable to would-be predators. premise: Statement1: Animal feed should not include genetically modified plants. premise: Statement1: In setting environmental standards for industry and others to meet, it is inadvisable to require the best results that state-of-the-art technology can achieve. premise: Statement1: Early in the development of a new product line, the critical resource is talent. premise: Statement1: Poetry journal patron : everybody who publishes in the brick wall review has to agree in advance that if a poem is printed in one of its regular issues, the magazine also has the right to reprint it, without monetary compensation, in its annual anthology. premise: Statement1: Although the discount stores in goreville's central shopping district are expected to close within five years as a result of competition from a spendless discount department store that just opened, those locations will not stay vacant for long. premise: Statement1: A country's employment in the manufacturing industry has dramatically decreased over the last thirty years. premise: Statement1: Herpetologist : some psychologists attribute complex reasoning to reptiles, claiming that simple stimulus-response explanations of some reptiles'behaviors, such as food gathering, can not account for the complexity of such behavior. premise: Statement1: Everyone likes repertory theater. Statement2: Actors like it because playing different roles each night decreases their level of boredom. hypothesis: Premise1: In a repertory theater, plays can be rescheduled to meet audience demand.

premise: Statement1: Anthropologist : one of the distinctive traits of humans is the ability to support a large brain with a small gut, which requires getting more calories from less food. premise: Statement1: Proposed new safety rules for the beach city airport would lengthen considerably the minimum time between takeoffs from the airport. premise: Statement1: The city of workney, in raising bus fares from $ 1.00 to $ 1.25, proposed that 18 fare tokens be sold for $ 20.00 to alleviate the extra burden of the fare increase on the city's poor people. hypothesis: Premise1: The outlay of $10. 00 for the purchase of 9 fare tokens would not be prohibitive for bus riders who are poor.

premise: Statement1: Lightbox, inc., owns almost all of the movie theaters in washington county and has announced plans to double the number of movie screens it has in the county within five years. premise: Statement1: Columnist : over the last 20 years the demand in north america for japanese-made automobiles has increased, whereas the japanese demand for north american-made automobiles has been stagnant. What north american exporters continue to ignore is that the japanese drive on the left side of the road. Statement3: Therefore, one obstacle to reducing the automotive trade imbalance will be removed if north american manufacturers simply produce more cars with right-side steering wheels.

hypothesis: Premise1: Keeping a corporation's profits high is likely to advance the important interests of the corporation's shareholders.

premise: Statement1: The vomeronasal organ ( vno ) is found inside the noses of various animals. While its structural development and function are clearer in other animals, most humans have a vno that is detectable, though only microscopically. premise: Statement1: Journalist : the new mayor is undeniably bold. Statement2: His assertions are made with utter certainty and confidence. Statement3: While these kinds of assertions may make him popular with the public, they also demonstrate that he is not an introspective person.
hypothesis: Premise1: Introspective people do not make assertions with utter certainty and confidence.

premise: Statement1: A smoker trying to quit is more likely to succeed if his or her doctor greatly exaggerates the dangers of smoking. premise: Statement1: Mayor : false alarms from home security systems waste so much valuable police time that in many communities police have stopped responding to alarms from homes whose systems frequently produce false alarms. hypothesis: Premise1: The threat of fines is likely to cause many residents to deactivate their security systems.

premise: Statement1: It is undeniable that regular consumption of fast food can lead to serious health problems. premise: Statement1: When a group of children who have been watching television programs that include acts of violence is sent to play with a group of children who have been watching programs that do not include acts of violence, the children who have been watching violent programs commit a much greater number of violent acts in their play than do the children who have been watching nonviolent programs. premise: Statement1: Physician : in an experiment, 50 patients with chronic back pain were divided into two groups. Most of the patients in the first group, but very few in the second group, reported a significant reduction in pain. premise: Statement1: High schools should only teach abstinence. Statement2: Students who practice abstinence will never experience an unplanned pregnancy or contract a sexually-transmitted disease.

premise: Statement1: Environmentalist : when bacteria degrade household cleaning products, vapors that are toxic to humans are produced. premise: Statement1: Pollster : when opinion researchers need a population sample that reflects the demographic characteristics of the national population, they choose their sample on the basis of national census data. Not everyone participates in the national census, despite its being mandatory. premise: Statement1: Compared to us, people who lived a century ago had very few diversions to amuse them. Statement2: Therefore, they likely read much more than we do today.

premise: Statement1: Although withholding information from someone who would find that information painful is sometimes justified, there is no such justification if the person would benefit from having the information. premise: Statement1: Mate is a beverage found in much of south america. premise: Statement1: We can learn about the living conditions of a vanished culture by examining its language. "

premise: Statement1: The five senses have traditionally been viewed as distinct yet complementary. Each sense is thought to have its own range of stimuli that are incapable of stimulating the other senses. premise: Statement1: College is increasingly unaffordable for everyone that is not independently wealthy, outpacing inflation every year since the early 1970s. Statement2: This year the average cost of tuition at a private, four-year university is more than $ 31,000 per year. In 1971, tuition cost less than $ 2,000, even after adjusting for inflation. Statement3: The trend is similar at public, four-year institutions. Statement4: Fortunately, with the advent of the internet, independent learning is easier than ever before. Online courses can also replicate a traditional college education, with options such as math, science, and liberal arts courses. Statement6: As a result, high school students would be wise to weigh their options before choosing to attend a traditional four-y ear college.

premise: Statement1: Environmentalist : an increased number of oil spills and the consequent damage to the environment indicate the need for stricter safety standards for the oil industry. Statement2: Since the industry refuses to take action, it is the national government that must regulate industry safety standards. In particular, the government has to at least require oil companies to put double hulls on their tankers and to assume financial responsibility for accidents. Statement3: Industry representative : the industry alone should be responsible for devising safety standards because of its expertise in handling oil and its understanding of the cost entailed. Statement4: Implementing the double-hull proposal is not currently feasible because it creates new safety issues. Statement5: Furthermore, the cost would be burdensome to the industry and consumers.
hypothesis: Premise1: The only effective sources of increased stringency in safety standards for oil tankers are action by the industry itself or national government regulation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The requirement of two hulls on oil tankers, although initially costly, will save money over time by reducing cleanup costs.

premise: Statement1: After the second world war, the charter of the newly formed united nations established an eleven-member security council and charged it with taking collective action in response to threats to world peace. Statement2: The charter further provided that the five nations that were then the major powers would permanently have sole authority to cast vetoes. Statement3: The reason given for this arrangement was that the burden of maintaining world peace would rest on the world's major powers, and no nation should be required to assume the burden of enforcing a decision it found repugnant.
hypothesis: Premise1: No nation that was not among the major powers at the end of the second world war would become a major power
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Decisions reached by a majority of nations in response to threats to world peace would be biased in favor of one or more major powers

premise: Statement1: Until he was dismissed amid great controversy, hastings was considered one of the greatest intelligence agents of all time. Soon after the dismissal, however, it was shown that he had never been incompetent. Statement2: It is clear that if his dismissal was justified, then hastings was either incompetent or else disloyal. Statement3: Thus, one is forced to conclude that hastings must have been disloyal.
hypothesis: Premise1: Hastings's dismissal was justified.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If someone was disloyal or incompetent, then his dismissal was justified.

premise: Statement1: The press reports on political campaigns these days as if they were chess games. Statement2: One candidate's campaign advisor makes a move ; the other candidate's advisor makes a countermove. Statement3: The press then reports on the campaign advisors and not on the candidates. Statement4: The losers in this chess game are the voters. Statement5: They are deprived of the information they need to make informed decisions because the press is ignoring substantive policy issues and reporting only on the process of the campaign. Statement6: It is clear that the campaign advisors should stay out of the limelight and let the press report on the most revealing positions on substantive issues the candidates have taken.
hypothesis: Premise1: The candidates in the election are taking positions on substantive policy issues.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There is no difference between reporting on the political process and reporting on substantive issues.

premise: Statement1: Tiger sharks are common in the waters surrounding tenare island. Statement2: Usually tiger sharks feed on smaller sharks, but sometimes they have attacked tourists swimming and surfing at tenare's beaches. Statement3: This has hurt tenare's tourism industry, which is second only to its fishing industry in annual revenues. Statement4: In order to help the economy, therefore, the mayor of the island has proposed an ongoing program to kill any tiger sharks within a mile of the beaches.
hypothesis: Premise1: The small sharks on which tiger sharks prey feed on fish that are commercially important to the island's fisheries.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Not all tourists who come to tenare island enjoy swimming or surfing.

premise: Statement1: Columnist : shortsighted motorists learn the hard way about the wisdom of preventive auto maintenance ; such maintenance almost always pays off in the long run. Statement2: Our usually shortsighted city council should be praised for using similar wisdom when they hired a long-term economic development adviser. In hiring this adviser, the council made an investment that is likely to have a big payoff in several years. Statement3: Other cities in this region that have devoted resources to economic development planning have earned large returns on such an investment.
hypothesis: Premise1: The columnist's city has a much smaller population and economy than the other cities did when they began devoting resources to economic development planning.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Even some cars that receive regular preventive maintenance break down, requiring costly repairs.

premise: Statement1: Moralist : immoral actions are those that harm other people. Statement2: But since such actions eventually harm those who perform them, those who act immorally do so only through ignorance of some of their actions'consequences rather than through a character defect.
hypothesis: Premise1: Those who, in acting immorally, eventually harm themselves do not intend that harm.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: None of those who knowingly harm themselves lack character defects.

premise: Statement1: Mayor : the city council has proposed additional funding for the town library. Statement2: However, the library 's own studies show that fewer than 10 % of town residents use the library. The city council claims that the library is underutilized because the books, electronic media, and computer equipment are outdated, and that if these were updated, more residents would use the library. Statement3: However, the last time the town allocated additional funding to the library, usage of the library did not increase. Statement4: Therefore the town should not allocate additional funding for the library.
hypothesis: Premise1: The last time the town allocated additional funding for the library, the funding was used to repair the crumbling staircase and ramp leading to the library entrance.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Usage of the library did increase in 1994, when the town voted to allocate funds for an advertising campaign promoting reading.

premise: Statement1: Microbiologist : a lethal strain of salmonella recently showed up in a european country, causing an outbreak of illness that killed two people and infected twenty-seven others. Investigators blame the severity of the outbreak on the overuse of antibiotics, since the salmonella bacteria tested were shown to be drug-resistant. Statement2: But this is unlikely because patients in the country where the outbreak occurred can not obtain antibiotics to treat illness without a prescription, and the country 's doctors prescribe antibiotics less readily than do doctors in any other european country.
hypothesis: Premise1: People in the country where the outbreak occurred often consume foods produced from animals that eat antibiotics-laden livestock feed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Physicians in the country where the outbreak occurred have become hesitant to prescribe antibiotics since they are frequently in short supply.

premise: Statement1: Patti : most parents are eager for their preschoolers to learn as much as possible. Statement2: However, instead of providing general opportunities for their children to learn, parents often direct their children's learning to their own personal concerns. Statement3: Because children have a natural curiosity and thirst for knowledge, they learn an enormous amount simply through growing and adapting to the world. Statement4: Therefore, this type of directed learning is unlikely to improve a child's preschool education.
hypothesis: Premise1: The type of directed learning in question is not a necessary part of the process of growing and adapting to the world.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The type of directed learning in question is likely to enhance the general opportunities for children to learn.

premise: Statement1: In 1860 bavarian quarry workers discovered the impression of a feather in a limestone slab dating to the mesozoic era. But there in limestone lay the imprint of a fully aerodynamic, three-inch-long feather. Statement2: It had previously been assumed that birds developed only after the close of the mesozoic era and after the disappearance of pterosaurs, a species characteristic of that era. Statement3: This, therefore, must have been the earliest bird -- certainly, the earliest found to that date.
hypothesis: Premise1: In the mesozoic era, no creatures other than birds had such feathers.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The feather belonged to a mesozoic creature that was neither a pterosaur nor a bird, but an intermediate between them.

premise: Statement1: In a certain democratic country the legislature passed a new tax law over the principled objections of the parliamentary opposition. Statement2: Some opposition leaders broke the new law by refusing to pay the new tax. They defended their lawbreaking by citing the historical precedent in the country of activists'having to break laws in winning for women the right to vote.
hypothesis: Premise1: The opposition leaders, unlike the activists, were part of the democratic process that they are defying.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Although they had principled objections to the new law, the opposition leaders derived a personal monetary benefit from breaking the law.

premise: Statement1: Columnist : much of north america and western europe is more heavily forested and has less acid rain and better air quality now than five decades ago. Statement2: Though this may be due largely to policies advocated by environmentalists, it nonetheless lends credibility to the claims of people who reject predictions of imminent ecological doom and argue that environmental policies that excessively restrict the use of natural resources may diminish the wealth necessary to adopt and sustain the policies that brought about these improvements.
hypothesis: Premise1: Nations sustain their wealth largely through industrial use of the natural resources found within their boundaries.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The concern demonstrated by a nation for the health and integrity of its natural ecosystems leads to an increase in that nation's wealth.

premise: Statement1: An overly centralized economy, not the changes in the climate, is responsible for the poor agricultural production in country x since its new government came to power. Statement2: Neighboring country y has experienced the same climatic conditions, but while agricultural production has been falling in country x, it has been rising in country y.
hypothesis: Premise1: The crops that have always been grown in country x are different from those that have always been grown in country y.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Both country x and country y have been experiencing drought conditions.

premise: Statement1: Critic : works of literature often present protagonists who scorn allegiance to their society and who advocate detachment rather than civic-mindedness. However, modern literature is distinguished from the literature of earlier eras in part because it more frequently treats such protagonists sympathetically. Statement2: Sympathetic treatment of such characters suggests to readers that one should be unconcerned about contributing to societal good. Statement3: Thus, modern literature can damage individuals who appropriate this attitude, as well as damage society at large.
hypothesis: Premise1: It is to the advantage of some individuals that they be concerned with contributing to societal good.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some individuals must believe that their society is better than most before they can become concerned with benefiting it.

premise: Statement1: The profitability of a business is reduced by anything that undermines employee morale. Statement2: This is why paying senior staff with stock options, which allows them to earn more when the enterprise prospers, is not a wise policy because it increases dramatically the difference in income between senior staff and employees who are paid only a fixed salary.
hypothesis: Premise1: Large income differences between fixed-salary employees and senior staff tend to undermine employee morale.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Employees whose incomes rise as the profits of their employers rise are more productive than those paid only a fixed salary.

premise: Statement1: Harry trevalga : you and your publication have unfairly discriminated against my poems. Statement2: I have submitted thirty poems in the last two years and you have not published any of them! Statement3: It is all because i won the fenner poetry award two years ago and your poetry editor thought she deserved it. Statement4: Publisher : ridiculous! Statement5: Our editorial policy and practice is perfectly fair, since our poetry editor judges all submissions for publication without ever seeing the names of the poets, and hence can not possibly have known who wrote your poems.
hypothesis: Premise1: The poetry editor cannot recognize the poems submitted by harry trevalga as his unless trevalga's name is attached to them.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The poetry editor's decisions on which poems to publish are not based strictly on judgments of intrinsic merit.

premise: Statement1: It is widely assumed that people need to engage in intellectual activities such as solving crossword puzzles or mathematics problems in order to maintain mental sharpness as they age. In fact, however, simply talking to other people -- that is, participating in social interaction, which engages many mental and perceptual skills -- suffices. Statement2: Evidence to this effect comes from a study showing that the more social contact people report, the better their mental skills.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many medical conditions and treatments that adversely affect a person's mental sharpness also tend to increase that person's social isolation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The tasks evaluating mental sharpness for which data were compiled by the study were more akin to mathematics problems than to conversation.

premise: Statement1: A recent epidemiological study found that businesspeople who travel internationally on business are much more likely to suffer from chronic insomnia than are businesspeople who do not travel on business. International travelers experience the stresses of dramatic changes in climate, frequent disruption of daily routines, and immersion in cultures other than their own, stresses not commonly felt by those who do not travel. Statement2: Thus, it is likely that these stresses cause the insomnia.
hypothesis: Premise1: Businesspeople who already suffer from chronic insomnia are no more likely than businesspeople who do not to accept assignments from their employers that require international travel.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Experiencing dramatic changes in climate and disruption of daily routines through international travel can be beneficial to some people who suffer from chronic insomnia.

premise: Statement1: The everyday behavior of whales is particularly difficult to study because introducing novel stimuli, such as divers or submarines, into the whales'environment causes whales to behave in unusual ways. Statement2: Some biologists plan to train sea lions to carry video cameras on their backs and, on command, to swim along with whales. Statement3: They argue that since whales are accustomed to there being sea lions nearby, using the sea lions to film the whales would allow biologists to study the everyday behavior of the whales.
hypothesis: Premise1: The behavior of the sea lions under human command will be within the range of sea lion behavior to which the whales are accustomed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Sea lions carrying video cameras will be able to film whales at a much closer range than divers can.

premise: Statement1: Science fiction creates an appetite for interstellar space exploration among certain people. Unfortunately, this appetite can not be satisfied with any technology humanity will soon possess. Statement2: Since gaps between expectations and reality spur discontent, no doubt one effect of science fiction has been to create an unproductive dissatisfaction with the way the world actually is.
hypothesis: Premise1: The fact that the appetite for interstellar space exploration cannot be satisfied with any technology humanity will soon possess has created a gap between reality and some people's expectations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the appetites science fiction has created in people could all be satisfied with technologies that humanity will soon possess, then science fiction could not create an unproductive dissatisfaction with the way the world is.

premise: Statement1: Public health will improve more quickly in the wake of new medical discoveries if medical researchers abandon their practice of waiting until their findings are published in peer-reviewed journals before informing the press of important research results. Statement2: This is because the public release of new medical information allows people to use that information in order to improve their health, but the peer-review process is unavoidably very slow.
hypothesis: Premise1: People would use new medical information even if it were not first published in peer-reviewed journals.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: New medical information that is first published in peer-reviewed journals does not usually receive public attention.

premise: Statement1: Efforts to get the public to exercise regularly, which have emphasized the positive health effects of exercise rather than the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle, have met with little success. Statement2: In contrast, efforts to curb cigarette smoking, which have emphasized the dangers of smoking rather than the positive health effects of quitting, have been highly successful. Statement3: Thus, efforts to get the public to exercise regularly would be more successful if they emphasized the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle rather than the positive health effects of exercise.
hypothesis: Premise1: Efforts to curb cigarette smoking would not be more successful if they emphasized the positive health effects of quitting rather than the dangers of smoking.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The majority of people who successfully quit smoking cite health concerns as the primary motivation for their success.

premise: Statement1: The number of serious traffic accidents ( accidents resulting in hospitalization or death ) that occurred on park road from 1986 to 1990 was 35 percent lower than the number of serious accidents from 1981 to 1985. sent2: the speed limit on park road was lowered in 1986. sent3: hence, the reduction of the speed limit led to the decrease in serious accidents.
hypothesis: Premise1: The annual number of vehicles using park road decreased significantly and steadily from 1981 to 1990.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Until 1986 accidents were classified as "serious" only if they resulted in an extended hospital stay.

premise: Statement1: Researcher : we have found that some cases of high blood pressure can be treated effectively with medicine. Statement2: Since it is generally accepted that any illness caused by stress is treatable only by the reduction of stress, some cases of high blood pressure must not be caused by stress.
hypothesis: Premise1: Medicine used to treat high blood pressure does not itself reduce stress.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The reduction of stress in a person's life can at times lower that person's blood pressure.

premise: Statement1: Tenants who do not have to pay their own electricity bills do not have a financial incentive to conserve electricity. Statement2: Thus, if more landlords install individual electricity meters on tenant dwellings so that tenants can be billed for their own use, energy will be conserved as a result.
hypothesis: Premise1: Landlords who pay for their tenants' electricity have a strong incentive to make sure that the appliances they provide for their tenants are energy efficient.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many initiatives have been implemented to educate people about how much money they can save through energy conservation.

premise: Statement1: Radio stations with radio data system ( rds ) technology broadcast special program information that only radios with an rds feature can receive. Statement2: Between 1994 and 1996, the number of rds radio stations in verdland increased from 250 to 600. however, since the number of rds-equipped radios in verdland was about the same in 1996 as in 1994, the number of verdlanders receiving the special program information probably did not increase significantly.
hypothesis: Premise1: Few if any of the rds radio stations that began broadcasting in verdland after 1994 broadcast to people with rds-equipped radios living in areas not previously reached by rds stations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In 1996 most verdlanders who lived within the listening area of an rds station already had a radio equipped to receive rds.

premise: Statement1: For years, university administrators, corporations, and government agencies have been predicting an imminent and catastrophic shortage of scientists and engineers. But since there is little noticeable upward pressure on the salaries of scientists and engineers, and unemployment is as high in these fields as any other, these doomsayers are turning out to be wrong.
hypothesis: Premise1: The number of students in university programs in science and engineering has increased significantly in the last five years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The proportion of all research in science and engineering being carried out by corporations is larger than it was five years ago.

premise: Statement1: Literary critic : often the heirs of a successful writer decide to publish the manuscripts and the letters the dead writer left behind, regardless of the merit of the work. However, many writers have manuscripts that they judge to be unworthy of publication and with which they would not like to be publicly associated even after they die. Statement2: Hence a successful writer who decides not to publish a recently completed manuscript should destroy it immediately.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most successful writers' judgments of their recently completed work is unnecessarily harsh and is often later revised.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some writers whose work becomes both popular and respected after they die received no literary recognition during their lifetimes.

premise: Statement1: Several recent studies establish that most people would want to be informed if they had any serious medical condition. Statement2: In each study, over 80 percent of the people surveyed indicated that they would want to be told.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some of the researchers conducting the studies had no background in medicine.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In another recent study, most of the people surveyed indicated that they would not want to be told if they had a serious medical condition.

premise: Statement1: Tiger beetles are such fast runners that they can capture virtually any nonflying insect. However, when running toward an insect, the beetles intermittently stop, and then, a moment later, resume their attack. Statement2: Perhaps they can not maintain their pace and must pause for a moment's rest ; but an alternative hypothesis is that while running tiger beetles are unable to process the resulting rapidly changing visual information, and so quickly go blind and stop.
hypothesis: Premise1: The beetles maintain a fixed time interval between pauses, although when an insect that had been stationary begins to flee, the beetle increases its speed after its next pause.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If, when a beetle pauses, it has not gained on the insect it is pursuing, the beetle generally ends its pursuit.

premise: Statement1: Appliance dealer : appliance manufacturers commonly modify existing models without giving the modified versions new model names. Some people have complained that this practice makes it impossible for consumers to be certain that the appliance they are about to purchase is identical to the one they may have seen at a neighbor's or read about in a consumer magazine. Statement2: Yet manufacturers'modifications to existing models are invariably improvements that benefit the buyer. Statement3: Therefore, consumers have little reason to object to this practice.
hypothesis: Premise1: Improved versions of appliances typically become available before vendors have stopped selling the older versions of the appliance with the same model name.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Appliances usually carry a model number that provides substantially more detailed information about the product than does the model name.

premise: Statement1: Human beings can exhibit complex, goal-oriented behavior without conscious awareness of what they are doing. Statement2: Thus, merely establishing that nonhuman animals are intelligent will not establish that they have consciousness.
hypothesis: Premise1: Complex, goal-oriented behavior requires intelligence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some intelligent human behavior is neither complex nor goal-oriented.

premise: Statement1: Newspaper : increases in produce prices apparently have led to an increase in the planting of personal gardens. Statement2: The two largest retail seed companies each reported sales increases of around 19 percent in the last year alone, a year in which the price of produce spiked.
hypothesis: Premise1: A large retail seed company went out of business early last year.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Personal gardens are usually popular in economic downturns.

premise: Statement1: Advertisement : my five-year-old identical twins wear matching clothing. Statement2: I washed two of their soiled matching shirts, one in my regular laundry detergent, and one in new wave laundry detergent. Statement3: The shirt washed in wave came out cleaner than the other shirt, so wave cleans better than my regular laundry detergent.
hypothesis: Premise1: The shirt laundered in the regular laundry detergent was not significantly less clean than the other shirt before being laundered.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Clothes washed in wave laundry detergent will come out cleaner than clothes washed in any other brand of laundry detergent

premise: Statement1: There were several early attempts to forge a reconciliation between shintoism and buddhism based on mutual respect among their adherents. Statement2: The evidence for this includes extant sculptures depicting shinto gods wearing buddhist vestments.
hypothesis: Premise1: The depiction of shinto gods wearing buddhist vestments was not intended to represent the triumph of shintoism over buddhism.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No sculptures that have not survived depicted shinto gods wearing buddhist vestments.

premise: Statement1: Art critic : abstract paintings are nonrepresentational, and so the only measure of their worth is their interplay of color, texture, and form. But for a painting to spur the viewer to political action, instances of social injustice must be not only represented, but also clearly comprehensible as such. Statement2: Therefore, abstract painting can never be a politically significant art form.
hypothesis: Premise1: Paintings that fail to move a viewer to political action cannot be politically significant.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Abstract painting cannot stimulate people to act.

premise: Statement1: In a study conducted in pennsylvania, servers in various restaurants wrote" thank you" on randomly selected bills before presenting the bills to their customers. Statement2: Tips on these bills were an average of three percentage points higher than tips on bills without the message. Statement3: Therefore, if servers in pennsylvania regularly wrote" thank you" on restaurant bills, their average income from tips would be significantly higher than it otherwise would have been.
hypothesis: Premise1: Regularly seeing "thank you" written on their bills would not lead restaurant patrons to revert to their earlier tipping habits.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The "thank you" messages would have the same impact on regular patrons of a restaurant as they would on occasional patrons of the same restaurant.

premise: Statement1: Manufacturers of writing paper need to add mineral" filler" to paper pulp if the paper made from the pulp is to look white. To make writing paper that looks white from recycled paper requires more filler than is required to make such paper from other sources. Statement2: Without such filler, paper products look grayish. Statement3: Therefore, barring the more efficient use of fillers in paper manufacturing or the development of paper-whitening technologies that do not require mineral fillers, if writing paper made from recycled paper comes to replace other types of writing paper, paper manufacturers will have to use more filler than they now use.
hypothesis: Premise1: Grayish writing paper will not be a universally acceptable alternative to white writing paper.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Certain kinds of paper cannot be manufactured from recycled paper.

premise: Statement1: Acme corporation offers unskilled workers excellent opportunities for advancement. Statement2: As evidence, consider the fact that the president of the company, ms. garon, worked as an assembly line worker, an entry-level position requiring no special skills, when she first started at acme.
hypothesis: Premise1: Acme regularly hires top graduates of business schools and employs them briefly in each of a succession of entry-level positions before promoting them to management.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Acme's vice president of operations also worked as an assembly line worker when he first started at acme.

premise: Statement1: Computer manufacturers have sought to make computer chips ever smaller, since decreasing the size of a computer's central processing unit ( cpu ) chip -- without making that cpu chip any less sophisticated -- will proportionally increase the speed of the cpu chip and the computer containing it. Statement2: But since cpu chips can not be made significantly smaller without decreasing their sophistication, computers can not currently be made significantly faster.
hypothesis: Premise1: Computers cannot currently be made faster unless their cpu chips are made smaller.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If both the size and the sophistication of a cpu chip are decreased, the speed of that chip will decrease.

premise: Statement1: Commentator : many people argue that the release of chlorofluorocarbons into the atmosphere is harming humans by damaging the ozone layer, thus allowing increased amounts of ultraviolet radiation to reach earth. But 300,000 years ago a supernova greatly damaged the ozone layer, with no significant effect on our earliest ancestors. Statement2: Because the supernova's disruption was much greater than the estimated effect of chlorofluorocarbons today, there is no reason to think that these chemicals in the atmosphere harm humans in this way.
hypothesis: Premise1: Our earliest ancestors possessed genetic characteristics making them more resistant than we are to the harmful effects of ultraviolet radiation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Extraterrestrial influences on the ozone layer tend to occur less often than terrestrial ones.

premise: Statement1: In his book, published in 1892, grey used the same metaphor that jordan used in her book, which was published in 1885. the metaphor is so unusual that there is little chance that two different people independently created it. Statement2: Therefore, it is highly likely that grey read jordan's book.
hypothesis: Premise1: A text that was probably known to both jordan and grey was published in 1860 and also contained the same unusual metaphor.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: According to most scholars, grey was generally a more inventive writer than jordan and developed many original metaphors.

premise: Statement1: Native speakers perceive sentences of their own language as sequences of separate words. This is shown by the fact that travelers who do not know a local language hear an unintelligible, uninterrupted stream of sound, not sentences with distinct words. Statement2: But this perception is an illusion.
hypothesis: Premise1: Native speakers' perceptions of their own language are not more accurate than are the perceptions of persons who do not know that language.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: It is impossible to understand sentences if they are in fact uninterrupted streams of sound.

premise: Statement1: Even if many more people in the world excluded meat from their diet, world hunger would not thereby be significantly reduced.
hypothesis: Premise1: The amount of land needed to produce enough meat to feed one person for a week can grow enough grain to feed more than ten people for a week.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Both herds and crops are susceptible to devastating viral and other diseases.

premise: Statement1: Half a dozen q40 airliners have crashed in the past two years. Statement2: The manufacturer has countered claims that the q40's design is faulty by pointing out that, in each of the accidents, investigations indicated pilot error as the cause.
hypothesis: Premise1: In four of the crashes, the pilot's error involved disregarding instructions or information supplied by air-traffic control
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: One of the crashes occurred in very poor visibility when the pilot was attempting to land.

premise: Statement1: Journalist : in physics journals, the number of articles reporting the results of experiments involving particle accelerators was lower last year than it had been in previous years. Statement2: Several of the particle accelerators at major research institutions were out of service the year before last for repairs, so it is likely that the low number of articles was due to the decline in availability of particle accelerators.
hypothesis: Premise1: Recent changes in the editorial policies of several physics journals have decreased the likelihood that articles concerning particle accelerator research will be accepted for publication.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Every article based on experiments with particle accelerators that was submitted for publication last year actually was published.

premise: Statement1: A study of kindergarten students has shown that children from households that own many books, an average of 54 books, show average or high reading achievement levels, while the households of children with lower reading achievement levels tend to own fewer books, 5 books on average.
hypothesis: Premise1: When children have books of their own, they develop more advanced reading skills because adults are more likely to read to them regularly.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Children whose parents buy them books feel happier about their lives.

premise: Statement1: Over 40,000 lead seals from the early byzantine empire remain today. Statement2: Apart from the rare cases where the seal authenticated a document of special importance, most seals had served their purpose when the document was opened. Statement3: Lead was not expensive, but it was not free : most lead seals would have been recast once they had served their purpose. Statement4: Thus the number of early byzantine documents sealed in such a fashion must have been many times the number of remaining lead seals.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most of the lead seals produced during the early byzantine empire were affixed to documents that were then opened during that period.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: During the time of the early byzantine empire there were at most 40, 000 documents of enough importance to prevent the removing and recycling of the seal.

premise: Statement1: Tv meteorologist : our station's weather forecasts are more useful and reliable than those of the most popular news station in the area. Statement2: After all, the most important question for viewers in this area is whether it will rain, and on most of the occasions when we have forecast rain for the next day, we have been right. The same can not be said for either of our competitors.
hypothesis: Premise1: The meteorologist's station forecast rain more often than did the most popular news station in the area.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The meteorologist's station has a policy of not making weather forecasts more than three days in advance.

premise: Statement1: A scientific team compared gold samples from several ancient artifacts with gold samples from an ancient mine in western asia. The ratios of the trace elements in these samples were all very similar, and they were unlike the trace-element ratios from any other known mine. Statement2: It is therefore likely that the gold in the artifacts was dug from the ancient mine.
hypothesis: Premise1: The ancient mine tapped into a large underground deposit that also supplied nearby riverbeds with significant quantities of gold. Go on to the next page
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Ancient gold artifacts were often constructed from gold taken from earlier artifacts.

premise: Statement1: During a press conference, council member johns stated that the advisory council appointed by mayor smith has been one of the least effective in recent history. Statement2: When asked to support that claim, johns pointed out that most citizens could not name any member of the advisory council.
hypothesis: Premise1: The public's familiarity with the composition of the advisory council is an indication of the effectiveness of the advisory council's work.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Only council members are qualified to judge the quality of the advisory council's work.

premise: Statement1: A recent study suggests that alzheimer's disease, which attacks the human brain, may be caused by a virus. Statement2: In the study, blood from 11 volunteers, each of whom had the disease, was injected into rats. premise: Statement1: Between 1951 and 1963, it was illegal in the country 25. sent2: of geronia to manufacture, sell, or transport any alcoholic beverages. premise: Statement1: Spiders of many species change color to match the pigmentation of the flowers they sit on. premise: Statement1: In the late 1980's, the population of sea otters in the north pacific ocean began to decline. premise: Statement1: Skeletal remains of early humans indicate clearly that our ancestors had fewer dental problems than we have. Statement2: So, most likely, the diet of early humans was very different from ours.

premise: Statement1: A survey has shown that public perceptions of comparative risks differ radically from the assessments of risk-management experts. premise: Statement1: Beverage company representative : sent2: the plastic rings that hold six-packs of beverage cans together pose a threat to wild animals, which often become entangled in the discarded rings and suffocate as a result. premise: Statement1: Economist : if minimum wage levels are low, employers have a greater incentive to hire more workers than to buy productivity-enhancing new technology. premise: Statement1: Astronomer : mount shalko is the perfect site for the proposed astronomical observatory. premise: Statement1: Typically during thunderstorms most lightning strikes carry a negative electric charge ; only a few carry a positive charge. premise: Statement1: Most water companies in the united states add fluoride to tap water to help prevent cavities. premise: Statement1: Researchers have studied the cost-effectiveness of growing halophytes -- salt-tolerant plant species -- for animal forage. premise: Statement1: In arumville, among those young children with elevated levels of lead in their blood, few live in houses containing lead-based paint, but many live in areas where vehicular traffic is heavy. premise: Statement1: Urban planner : when a city loses population due to migration, property taxes in that city tend to rise. premise: Statement1: To prevent a newly built dam on the chiff river from blocking the route of fish migrating to breeding grounds upstream, the dam includes a fish pass, a mechanism designed to allow fish through the dam. premise: Statement1: Psychologist : birth-order effects, the alleged effects of when one was born relative to the births of siblings, have not been detected in studies of adult personality that use standard personality tests. premise: Statement1: Five years ago, the town of bayside, in the katonic river valley, had catastrophic flooding one spring, and consequently, most insurers now refuse to write flood insurance for houses in bayside. premise: Statement1: In colorado subalpine meadows, nonnative dandelions co-occur with a native flower, the larkspur. Statement2: Bumblebees visit both species, creating the potential for interactions between the two species with respect to pollination. Statement4: The control plots yielded significantly more larkspur seeds than the dandelion-free plots, leading the researchers to conclude that the presence of dandelions facilitates pollination ( and hence seed production ) in the native species by attracting more pollinators to the mixed plots.

premise: Statement1: Sparrow airlines is planning to reduce its costs by cleaning its planes'engines once a month, rather than the industry standard of every six months. Furthermore, cleaning an engine reduces its fuel consumption by roughly 1.2 percent.

premise: Statement1: In polluted environments, dolphins gradually accumulated toxins in their body fat, and the larger the dolphin the more accumulated toxin it can tolerate. Nearly 80 percent of the toxins a female dolphin has accumulated pass into the fat-rich milk her nursing calf ingests. Statement2: Therefore, the unusually high mortality rate among dolphin calves in the industrially contaminated waters along florida's gulf coast is probably the result of their being poisoned by their mother's milk.

premise: Statement1: Advertisers are often criticized for their unscrupulous manipulation of people's tastes and wants. premise: Statement1: The average temperature of the lobster-rich waters off the coast of foerkland has been increasing for some years. Statement2: In warmer water, lobsters grow faster. premise: Statement1: It is said that people should accept themselves as they are instead of being dissatisfied with their own abilities. premise: Statement1: In response to mounting public concern, an airplane manufacturer implemented a program with the well-publicized goal of reducing by half the total yearly amount of hazardous waste generated by its passenger-jet division. premise: Statement1: Geologists recently discovered marks that closely resemble worm tracks in a piece of sandstone. premise: Statement1: Nutritionists believe that a person's daily requirement for vitamins can readily be met by eating five servings of fruits and vegetables daily. However, most people eat far less than this. Statement2: Thus, most people need to take vitamin pills.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many commonly consumed foods that are neither fruits nor vegetables are fortified by manufacturers with the vitamins found in fruits and vegetables.

premise: Statement1: Bacteria that benefit human beings when they are present in the body are called commensals. Statement2: The bacterium helicobacter pylori plays a primary role in the development of stomach ulcers. Statement3: But since stomach ulcers occur in less than 10 percent of those harboring h. pylori, and since it allegedly strengthens immune response, many scientists now consider it a commensal. But this is surely misguided. Statement4: Only about 10 percent of the people who harbor mycobacter tuberculosis -- a bacterium that can cause tuberculosis -- get sick from it, yet no one would call m. tuberculosis a commensal.
hypothesis: Premise1: People who harbor m. tuberculosis derive no benefit from its presence.

premise: Statement1: The familiar slogan" survival of the fittest" is popularly used to express the claim, often mistakenly attributed to evolutionary biologists, that the fittest are most likely to survive. However, biologists use the term" fittest" to mean" most likely to survive, " so the slogan is merely claiming that the most likely to survive are the most likely to survive. Statement2: While this claim is clearly true, it is a tautology and so is neither informative nor of scientific interest.
hypothesis: Premise1: The truth of a purported scientific claim is not sufficient for it to be of scientific interest.

premise: Statement1: City council member : demand for electricity has been increasing by 1.5 percent a year, and there simply is no more space to build additional power plants to meet future demand increases. hypothesis: Premise1: Existing power plants do not have the capacity to handle all of the projected increase in demand for electricity.

premise: Statement1: Letter to the editor : after baerton's factory closed, there was a sharp increase in the number of claims filed for job-related injury compensation by the factory's former employees. premise: Statement1: Several industries have recently switched at least partly from older technologies powered by fossil fuels to new technologies powered by electricity. premise: Statement1: Linguist : in english, the past is described as" behind" and the future" ahead, " whereas in aymara the past is" ahead" and the future" behind. premise: Statement1: Solicitor : loux named zembaty executor of her will. Statement2: Her only beneficiary was her grandson, of whom she was very fond. premise: Statement1: The kiffer forest preserve, in the northernmost part of the abbimac valley, is where most of the bears in the valley reside. premise: Statement1: Psychologists observing a shopping mall parking lot found that, on average, drivers spent 39 seconds leaving a parking space when another car was quietly waiting to enter it, 51 seconds if the driver of the waiting car honked impatiently, but only 32 seconds leaving a space when no one was waiting. Statement2: This suggests that drivers feel possessive of their parking spaces even when leaving them, and that this possessiveness increases in reaction to indications that another driver wants the space.

premise: Statement1: Giordano bruno, the renaissance philosopher, must have been a spy for england during the time he served in the french embassy there. premise: Statement1: Finnish author jaakko mikkeli was accused by norwegian author kirsten halden of plagiarizing a book that she had written and that had been published 20 years before mikkeli's. premise: Statement1: Currently, warm-blooded pets are allowed to enter the country only if they have a certificate showing that they have been vaccinated against rabies. Statement3: Therefore, a more effective means of preventing pets with rabies from entering the country would be to administer blood tests and refuse entry to any pets whose blood did not contain a high level of rabies antibodies.

premise: Statement1: Lindsey has been judged to be a bad songwriter simply because her lyrics typically are disjointed and subjective. premise: Statement1: Though sucking zinc lozenges has been promoted as a treatment for the common cold, research has revealed no consistent effect. premise: Statement1: For the average person who needs a transfusion, blood from a relative is more likely to be infected with hepatitis than is blood from a blood bank. premise: Statement1: Editorial : the threat of harsh punishment for a transgression usually decreases one's tendency to feel guilt or shame for committing that transgression, and the tendency to feel guilt or shame for committing a transgression reduces a person's tendency to commit transgressions. premise: Statement1: Satellite navigation systems ( satnavs ) for cars, in which computer voices announce directions as you drive, save fuel and promote safety. premise: Statement1: The more television children watch, the less competent they are in mathematical knowledge. premise: Statement1: Market research traditionally entails surveying consumers about why they buy and use particular products and brands. premise: Statement1: Amphibian populations are declining in numbers worldwide. Not coincidentally, the earth's ozone layer has been continuously depleted throughout the last 50 years. premise: Statement1: The format of network television news programs generally allows advocates of a point of view only 30 seconds to convey their message. Statement3: The format of newspaper stories, on the other hand, leads readers to pursue details of stories headed by the most important facts and so has the opposite effect on regular readers -- that of maintaining the expectation of careful discussion of public issues. Statement4: Thus, it would be in the employees'best interest for their union to accept cuts in retirement benefits.

premise: Statement1: To increase the productivity of its attorneys, x law firm has decided to implement a part-time program, which would allow attorneys to have more flexibility in the number of days per week they work. Statement2: Research has shown that giving employees the option of working part time is associated with increased company loyalty.
hypothesis: Premise1: An increase in company loyalty may lead to an increase in productivity in the attorneys
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: An increase in productivity is always signaled by a change in the number of days per week worked by employees

premise: Statement1: Department store manager : sent2: there is absolutely no reason to offer our customers free gift wrapping again this holiday season. Statement2: If most customers take the offer, it will be expensive and time-consuming for us. On the other hand, if only a few customers want it, there is no advantage in offering it.
hypothesis: Premise1: Either few customers would want free gift wrapping or most customers would want it.

premise: Statement1: Even though trading in ivory has been outlawed by international agreement, some piano makers still use ivory, often obtained illegally, to cover piano keys. Statement2: Recently, experts have devised a synthetic ivory that, unlike earlier ivory substitutes, has found favor with concert pianists throughout the world. Statement3: But because piano makers have never been major consumers of ivory, the development of the synthetic ivory will therefore probably do little to help curb the killing of elephants, from whose tusks most natural ivory is obtained.
hypothesis: Premise1: The most common use for natural ivory is in ornamental carvings, which are prized not only for the quality of their workmanship but also for the authenticity of their materials.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The new synthetic ivory can be manufactured to resemble in color and surface texture any of the various types of natural ivory that have commercial uses.

premise: Statement1: Rhizobium bacteria living in the roots of bean plants or other legumes produce fixed nitrogen, which is one of the essential plant nutrients and which for nonlegume crops, such as wheat, normally must be supplied by applications of nitrogen-based fertilizer. premise: Statement1: During the period in which there are no competitive races, two runners -- runners a and runners b -- take part in an experiment measuring their vo2 max, the volume of oxygen an athlete can use. Statement2: During these sessions, the runners engaged in moderate aerobic activity, or a sustained heart rate between 146-154 beats per minute. Statement3: At the end of the sessions, runner a had a greater vo2 max than did runner b. therefore, once the two runners begin identical intensive training -- sessions involving over 168 beats per minute-- for the race season, runner a will continue to have the greater vo2 max, assuming that neither become injured and that both train with similar intensity.
hypothesis: Premise1: Regarding their vo2 maxes, runners respond equally to intensive training.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The amount one trains does not influence one's vo2 max.

premise: Statement1: In a town containing a tourist attraction, hotel and restaurant revenues each increased more rapidly over the past year than did revenue from the sale of passes to the attraction, which are valid for a full year. hypothesis: Premise1: The local board of tourism reports that the average length of stay for tourists remained unchanged over the past year.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: While the cost of passes is unchanged since last year, hotel and meal prices have risen.

premise: Statement1: The retail price of decaffeinated coffee is considerably higher than that of regular coffee. However, the process by which coffee beans are decaffeinated is fairly simple and not very costly. Statement2: Therefore, the price difference can not be accounted for by the greater cost of providing decaffeinated coffee to the consumer.
hypothesis: Premise1: The beans used for producing decaffeinated coffee do not cost much more before processing than the beans used for producing regular coffee.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Processing regular coffee costs more than processing decaffeinated coffee.

premise: Statement1: In modern deep-diving marine mammals, such as whales, the outer shell of the bones is porous. Statement2: This has the effect of making the bones light enough so that it is easy for the animals to swim back to the surface after a deep dive. Statement3: The outer shell of the bones was also porous in the ichthyosaur, an extinct prehistoric marine reptile. Statement4: We can conclude from this that ichthyosaurs were deep divers.
hypothesis: Premise1: In most modern and prehistoric marine reptile species that are not deep divers, the outer shell of the bones is porous.

premise: Statement1: A study of 8,000 randomly chosen adults in a european country found that 75 percent of those who consumed alcohol an average of twice or more per week over a six-month period suffered at least one cold during that period. Statement2: We can tentatively conclude from this that consuming alcohol tends to increase one's susceptibility to colds.

premise: Statement1: Guidebook writer : i have visited hotels throughout the country and have noticed that in those built before 1930 the quality of the original carpentry work is generally superior to that in hotels built afterward. Statement2: Clearly carpenters working on hotels before 1930 typically worked with more skill, care, and effort than carpenters who have worked on hotels built subsequently.
hypothesis: Premise1: The better the quality of original carpentry in a building, the less likely that building is to fall into disuse and be demolished.

premise: Statement1: Spectroscopic analysis has revealed the existence of frozen nitrogen, methane, and carbon monoxide on the surface of pluto. Statement2: Such ices have a tendency to vaporize, producing an atmosphere. Statement3: Since the proportion of any gas in such an atmosphere depends directly on how readily the corresponding ice vaporizes, astronomers have concluded that the components of pluto's atmosphere are nitrogen, carbon monoxide, and methane, in order of decreasing abundance.
hypothesis: Premise1: There is no frozen substance on the surface of pluto that vaporizes more readily than methane but less readily than carbon monoxide.

premise: Statement1: On average, corporations that encourage frequent social events in the workplace show higher profits than those that rarely do. Statement2: This suggests that the ez corporation could boost its profits by having more staff parties during business hours.
hypothesis: Premise1: The great majority of corporations that encourage frequent social events in the workplace do so at least in part because they are already earning above-average profits.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Frequent social events in a corporate workplace leave employees with less time to perform their assigned duties than they would otherwise have.

premise: Statement1: A study followed a group of teenagers who had never smoked and tracked whether they took up smoking and how their mental health changed. Statement2: After one year, the incidence of depression among those who had taken up smoking was four times as high as it was among those who had not. Statement3: Since nicotine in cigarettes changes brain chemistry, perhaps thereby affecting mood, it is likely that smoking contributes to depression in teenagers.
hypothesis: Premise1: Participants who were depressed at the start of the study were no more likely to be smokers after one year than those who were not depressed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The study did not distinguish between participants who smoked only occasionally and those who were heavy smokers.

premise: Statement1: Waste management companies, which collect waste for disposal in landfills and incineration plants, report that disposable plastics make up an ever-increasing percentage of the waste they handle. Statement2: It is clear that attempts to decrease the amount of plastic that people throw away in the garbage are failing.

premise: Statement1: Sociologist : research shows, contrary to popular opinion, that, all other things being equal, most people who have pets are less happy than most people who do not. hypothesis: Premise1: Most people who have pets feel happier because they have pets.

premise: Statement1: Political scientist : the dissemination of political theories is in principle able to cause change in existing social structures. Statement2: However, all political theories are formulated in the educationally privileged setting of the university, leading to convoluted language that is alienating to many individuals outside academia who would be important agents of change. Statement3: It follows that, with respect to political theory, there is a special role for those outside the university context to render it into accessible, clear language.
hypothesis: Premise1: Persons within academic settings are less willing or less able than persons outside to write in a straightforward way.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Persons outside academic settings are the most important agents of change to the social structure.

premise: Statement1: In defending the hyperion school of journalism from charges that its program is of little or no value to its students, the dean of the school pointed to its recent success in placing students : 65 percent of its graduates went on to internships or jobs in print or broadcast journalism.
hypothesis: Premise1: More than half of the school's students came from jobs in journalism to improve their skills.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some newspaper editors do not regard journalism schools as a necessary part of the training of a journalist.

premise: Statement1: The current pattern of human consumption of resources, in which we rely on nonrenewable resources, for example metal ore, must eventually change. Statement2: Since there is only so much metal ore available, ultimately we must either do without or turn to renewable resources to take its place.
hypothesis: Premise1: We cannot indefinitely replace exhausted nonrenewable resources with other nonrenewable resources.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are renewable resource replacements for all of the nonrenewable resources currently being consumed.

premise: Statement1: Recently discovered prehistoric rock paintings on small islands off the northern coast of norway have archaeologists puzzled. Statement2: The predominant theory about northern cave paintings was that they were largely a description of the current diets of the painters. This theory can not be right, because the painters must have needed to eat the sea animals populating the waters north of norway if they were to make the long journey to and from the islands, and there are no paintings that unambiguously depict such creatures.
hypothesis: Premise1: The cave paintings that were discovered on the islands depicted many land animals.

premise: Statement1: Economist : as should be obvious, raising the minimum wage significantly would make it more expensive for businesses to pay workers for minimum-wage jobs. hypothesis: Premise1: Businesses typically pass the cost of increased wages on to consumers without adversely affecting profits.

premise: Statement1: Regular weightlifting is necessary for good health. Statement2: Weightlifting with heavy resistance, especially with compound movements, helps break down and rebuild stronger muscle fibers, resulting in strength and size gains.
hypothesis: Premise1: Strength and size gains are indicators of good health.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Regular weightlifting is necessary for good health.

premise: Statement1: The level of triglycerides in the blood rises when triglycerides are inadequately metabolized. Research shows that patients with blood triglyceride levels above 1 milligram per milliliter are twice as prone to heart attacks as others. Statement2: Thus, it is likely that consuming large amounts of fat, processed sugar, or alcohol, each known to increase triglyceride levels in the blood, is a factor causing heart disease.
hypothesis: Premise1: Heart disease interferes with the body's ability to metabolize triglycerides.

premise: Statement1: Critic : works of modern literature can not be tragedies as those of ancient playwrights and storytellers were unless their protagonists are seen as possessing nobility, which endures through the calamities that befall one. Statement2: In an age that no longer takes seriously the belief that human endeavors are governed by fate, it is therefore impossible for a contemporary work of literature to be a tragedy.
hypothesis: Premise1: Those whose endeavors are not regarded as governed by fate will not be seen as possessing nobility.

premise: Statement1: A professor of business placed a case-study assignment for her class on her university's computer network. She later found out that instead of reading the assignment on the computer screen, 50 out of the 70 students printed it out on paper. Statement2: Thus, it is not the case that books delivered via computer will make printed books obsolete.
hypothesis: Premise1: Studies consistently show that most computer users will print reading material that is more than a few pages in length rather than read it on the computer screen.

premise: Statement1: The city's transportation department has noticed that the city's extensive public bus system generally runs behind schedule on days when traffic is heavier in the city. Statement2: The transportation commissioner concluded that the traffic prevents the buses from reaching their stops on time, despite the existence of a number of bus-only lanes throughout the city. hypothesis: Premise1: The city's bus-only lanes do not contribute substantially to gridlock by reducing the number of lanes available to other traffic.

premise: Statement1: Patel : it is often thought that direct experience, unlike language and culture, can always serve as common ground for communication among human beings. Statement2: But people from different cultures inhabit different sensory worlds. Statement3: Because selective screening of sensory data admits some perceptions while filtering out others, one person's experience, perceived through one set of culturally patterned sensory screens, is quite different from what anyone from another culture would experience when encountering the same sensory data.
hypothesis: Premise1: No two people from different cultures have the same sensory screens.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Two people from the same culture sometimes have different sensory screens.

premise: Statement1: Politician : the cohesion of a society depends on its members'accepting a set of basic principles. When these principles are routinely called into question, the society begins to erode. Statement2: Any society, therefore, that allows schools to present the society's set of basic principles as simply one of several alternatives, with the suggestion that students may choose which to accept on the basis of the principles'worth, is inviting its own demise.
hypothesis: Premise1: Given the chance to assess the merits of the principles governing their societies, individuals will rarely find those principles acceptable.

premise: Statement1: In 1992 outlaw fishing boats began illegally harvesting lobsters from the territorial waters of the country of belukia. Soon after, the annual tonnage of lobster legally harvested in belukian waters began declining ; in 1996, despite there being no reduction in the level of legal lobster fishing activity, the local catch was 9,000 tons below pre-1992 levels. Statement2: It is therefore highly likely that the outlaw fishing boats harvested about 9,000 tons of lobster illegally that year.
hypothesis: Premise1: The illegal lobster harvesting was not so extensive that the population of catchable lobsters in belukia's territorial waters had sharply declined by 1996.

premise: Statement1: Grow-again ointment is a proven treatment for reversing male hereditary baldness. Five drops daily is the recommended dose, and exceeding this quantity does not increase the product's effectiveness. premise: Statement1: A favored theory to explain the extinction of dinosaurs, together with many other species, has been the globally catastrophic collision of a large asteroid with the earth. Supporting evidence is an extraterrestrial chemical element in a layer of dust found worldwide at a geological level laid down contemporaneously with the supposed event. Statement2: A new competing theory contends that any asteroid impact was irrelevant, because it was massive volcanic activity that caused the extinctions by putting enough dust into the atmosphere to cool the planet. Statement3: The deccan region of india contains extensive volcanic flows that occurred within the same time period as the supposed asteroid impact and the extinctions.

premise: Statement1: In the past, when there was no highway speed limit, the highway accident rate increased yearly, peaking a decade ago. Statement2: At that time, the speed limit on highways was set at 90 kilometers per hour ( kph ) ( 55 miles per hour ). Every year since the introduction of the highway speed limit, the highway accident rate has been at least 15 percent lower than that of its peak rate. Statement3: Thus, setting the highway speed limit at 90 kph ( 55 mph ) has reduced the highway accident rate by at least 15 percent.
hypothesis: Premise1: Thanks to changes in automobile design in the past ten years, drivers are better able to maintain control of their cars in dangerous situations.

premise: Statement1: Voters commonly condemn politicians for being insincere, but politicians often must disguise their true feelings when they make public statements. Statement2: If they expressed their honest views-about, say, their party 's policies-then achieving politically necessary compromises would be much more difficult. Statement3: Clearly, the very insincerity that people decry shows that our government is functioning well.
hypothesis: Premise1: Achieving political compromises is not all that is necessary for the proper functioning of a government.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some political compromises are not in the best long-term interest of the government.

premise: Statement1: Professor robinson : a large meteorite impact crater in a certain region was thought to be the clue to explaining the mass extinction of plant and animal species that occurred at the end of the mesozoic era. Statement2: However, the crystalline structure of rocks recovered at the site indicates that the impact that formed this crater was not the culprit. Statement3: When molten rocks crystallize, they display the polarity of earth's magnetic field at that time. But the recrystallized rocks recovered at the site display normal magnetic polarity, even though earth's magnetic field was reversed at the time of the mass extinction.
hypothesis: Premise1: The crater indicates an impact of more than sufficient size to have caused the mass extinction.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The recovered rocks recrystallized shortly after they melted.

premise: Statement1: An independent computer service company tallied the service requests it receives for individual brands of personal computers. premise: Statement1: The continents of south america and africa were once joined together as one landmass. Statement2: Evidence for this claim can be found in the fact that fossils of mesosaurs, extinct reptiles, have been unearthed in both western africa and eastern south america.
