premise: Statement1: One of the effects of lead poisoning is an inflammation of the optic nerve, which causes those who have it to see bright haloes around light sources. In order to produce the striking yellow effects in his" sunflowers" paintings, van gogh used naples yellow, a pigment containing lead. Statement2: Since in his later paintings, van gogh painted bright haloes around the stars and sun, it is likely that he was suffering from lead poisoning caused by ingesting the pigments he used.
hypothesis: Premise1: In van gogh's later paintings he painted some things as he saw them.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The effects of naples yellow could not have been achieved using other pigments.

premise: Statement1: Novelists can not become great as long as they remain in academia. Statement2: Powers of observation and analysis, which schools successfully hone, are useful to the novelist, but an intuitive grasp of the emotions of everyday life can be obtained only by the kind of immersion in everyday life that is precluded by being an academic.
hypothesis: Premise1: Novelists cannot be great without an intuitive grasp of the emotions of everyday life.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No great novelist lacks powers of observation and analysis.

premise: Statement1: Researchers gave 100 first-graders after-school lessons in handwriting. Statement2: They found that those whose composition skills had improved the most had learned to write letters the most automatically. Statement3: This suggests that producing characters more automatically frees up mental resources for other activities.
hypothesis: Premise1: Over the course of the lessons, the first-graders who showed greater improvement in their ability to write letters automatically also generally showed greater improvement in their composition skills.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Among the first-graders who received the lessons in handwriting, those who started out with strong composition skills showed substantial improvement in how automatically they could write letters.

premise: Statement1: Numerous paintings and engravings representing warfare can be found in remains of all civilizations going back to and including the neolithic period, when agriculture was first developed. Statement2: However, no paintings or engravings of warfare are found dating from before the neolithic period. Statement3: Therefore, warfare must have first developed as a result of the transition to an agricultural society.
hypothesis: Premise1: There was no warfare prior to the period in which paintings and engravings of warfare were first created.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Warfare in the neolithic period was always motivated by territorial disputes over agricultural land.

premise: Statement1: Researcher : the role of chemicals called pheromones in determining the sexual behavior of some animals is well documented. But, even though humans also produce these chemicals, it is clear that psychological factors have taken over this role in us. Statement2: Whereas for animals these behaviors are involuntary, which is a clear sign of chemical control, humans, by virtue of their free will, choose how they behave, and thus psychological factors take over. Statement3: So pheromones are merely a vestige of our evolutionary past.
hypothesis: Premise1: Voluntary action cannot have a chemical explanation
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Free will can be found only in humans

premise: Statement1: Commentator : the theory of trade retaliation states that countries closed out of any of another country 's markets should close some of their own markets to the other country in order to pressure the other country to reopen its markets. Statement2: If every country acted according to this theory, no country would trade with any other.
hypothesis: Premise1: For any two countries, at least one has some market closed to the other.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Countries close their markets to foreigners to protect domestic producers.

premise: Statement1: Most plants have developed chemical defenses against parasites. Humans ingest these natural pesticides without harm every day. Statement2: The average plant contains about 40 natural pesticides -- chemical compounds toxic to bacteria, fungi, and other parasites. Statement3: Therefore, the additional threat posed by synthetic pesticides sprayed on crop plants by humans is minimal.
hypothesis: Premise1: The synthetic pesticides sprayed on crop plants by humans usually have chemical structures similar to those of the natural pesticides produced by the plants.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Natural plant pesticides generally serve only as defenses against specific parasites, whereas synthetic pesticides are often harmful to a wide variety of organisms.

premise: Statement1: Scientific and technological discoveries have considerable effects on the development of any society. Statement2: It follows that predictions of the future condition of societies in which scientific and technological discovery is particularly frequent are particularly untrustworthy.
hypothesis: Premise1: Forecasts of scientific and technological discoveries, or forecasts of their effects, are not entirely reliable.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The development of a society requires scientific and technological discoveries.

premise: Statement1: Quillium is the most popular blood pressure regulating prescription drug on the market. Giant pharma, inc., the largest prescription drug manufacturer in the country, owns the patent on quillium. Giant pharma stock is hitting unprecedented high valuations. Statement2: As a result, quillium is by far the most effective drug available in treating irregular blood pressure.
hypothesis: Premise1: The most lucrative and popular pharmaceuticals are not always the most effective.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Giant pharma's high stock prices are attributable to recent patent acquisitions other than quillium.

premise: Statement1: One-year-olds ordinarily prefer the taste of sweet food to that of salty food. Yet if one feeds a one-year-old salty food rather than sweet food, then over a period of about a year he or she will develop a taste for the salty flavor and choose to eat salty food rather than sweet food. Statement2: Thus, a young child's taste preferences can be affected by the type of food he or she has been exposed to.
hypothesis: Premise1: Two-year-olds do not naturally prefer salty food to sweet food.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A child's taste preferences usually change between age one and age two.

premise: Statement1: In the nation of partoria, large trucks currently account for 6 percent of miles driven on partoria's roads but are involved in 12 percent of all highway fatalities. The very largest trucks-those with three trailers-had less than a third of the accident rate of single- and double-trailer trucks. Statement2: Clearly, therefore, one way for partoria to reduce highway deaths would be to require shippers to increase their use of triple-trailer trucks.
hypothesis: Premise1: So far only the best, most experienced drivers for partorian trucking companies have been driving triple-trailer trucks.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Very few fatal collisions involving trucks in partoria are collisions between two trucks.

premise: Statement1: Advertisement : at most jewelry stores, the person assessing the diamond is the person selling it, so you can see why an assessor might say that a diamond is of higher quality than it really is. Statement2: But because all diamonds sold at gem world are certified in writing, you're assured of a fair price when purchasing a diamond from gem world.
hypothesis: Premise1: The written certifications of diamonds at gem world are provided by an independent company of gem specialists.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The diamonds sold at gem world are generally of higher quality than those sold at other jewelry stores.

premise: Statement1: We are in a new industrial revolution that requires management trainees to develop" action learning" from real experience within business and industry, rather than getting tied up with theory and academia. Statement2: Business schools seem unable, on their own, to tear themselves away from their largely academic roots and move closer to the realities of today's business and industry ; too often, trainees in business schools find themselves studying hypothetical cases instead of real ones. Furthermore, business schools have been slow to respond to the needs of business. Statement3: Therefore, business schools should allow business executives to set curricula for management trainees that could then be taught by academics.
hypothesis: Premise1: Today's business executives have valuable insight into business that academics in business schools do not have.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Academics are not capable of teaching curricula suitable for relevant management training.

premise: Statement1: Citizen : our legislators need to act quickly to counter the effects of the recession, especially the present level of unemployment, which is the highest ever. Statement2: We urgently need a major tax cut for our upper-income citizens. There would then be a correspondingly large increase in investment that would create new jobs. Statement3: If this measure is not taken, investment will not grow.
hypothesis: Premise1: Upper-income citizens could use the money gained from the tax cut in ways that increase investment
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The greater the tax cut given to a group of people, the more likely it is that members of that group will invest the money

premise: Statement1: The use of growth-promoting antibiotics in hog farming can weaken their effectiveness in treating humans because such use can spread resistance to those antibiotics among microorganisms. Statement2: But now the smee company, one of the largest pork marketers, may stop buying pork raised on feed containing these antibiotics. Statement3: Smee has 60 percent of the pork market, and farmers who sell to smee would certainly stop using antibiotics in order to avoid jeopardizing their sales. Statement4: So if smee makes this change, it will probably significantly slow the decline in antibiotics'effectiveness for humans.
hypothesis: Premise1: Other major pork marketers will probably stop buying pork raised on feed containing growth-promoting antibiotics if smee no longer buys such pork.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The decline in hog growth due to discontinuation of antibiotics can be offset by improved hygiene.

premise: Statement1: Economist : our economy's weakness is the direct result of consumers'continued reluctance to spend, which in turn is caused by factors such as high-priced goods and services. Statement2: This reluctance is exacerbated by the fact that the average income is significantly lower than it was five years ago. Statement3: Thus, even though it is not a perfect solution, if the government were to lower income taxes, the economy would improve.
hypothesis: Premise1: Consumers will be less reluctant to spend money if income taxes are lowered.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Increasing consumer spending will cause prices for goods and services to decrease.

premise: Statement1: Farmer : worldwide, just three grain crops-rice, wheat, and corn-account for most human caloric intake. Statement2: To maintain this level of caloric intake and also keep pace with global population growth, yields per acre from each of these crops will have to increase at least 1.5 percent every year, given that the supply of cultivated land is diminishing. Statement3: Therefore, the government should increase funding for research into new ways to improve yields
hypothesis: Premise1: Increasing government funding for research into new ways to improve the yields per acre of rice, wheat, and corn crops would help to increase total worldwide annual production of food from these crops
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Increasing the yields per acre of rice, wheat, and corn is more important than increasing the yields per acre of other crops

premise: Statement1: Asbestos, an almost indestructible mineral once installed as building insulation, poses no health risk unless the asbestos is disturbed and asbestos fibers are released into the environment. Statement2: Since removing asbestos from buildings disturbs it, thereby releasing asbestos fibers, the government should not require removal of all asbestos insulation.
hypothesis: Premise1: Much of the time, removed asbestos is buried in landfills and forgotten, with no guarantee that it will not be disturbed again.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Asbestos poses far less risk to health than does smoking, drug and alcohol abuse, improper diet, or lack of exercise.

premise: Statement1: Overexposure to certain wavelengths of strong sunlight is the main cause of melanoma, a virulent form of skin cancer. Adequate sunblock, according to doctors, is any preparation that prevents sunburn even if the person is exposed to strong sunlight for a significant length of time. Statement2: For this reason, doctors now urge everyone to put adequate sunblock on skin exposed to strong sunlight.
hypothesis: Premise1: There is no evidence that there are wavelengths of sunlight that lead to both sunburn and melanoma.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are people who have allergic reactions to certain chemicals found in many sunblocks.

premise: Statement1: Essayist : one of the claims of laissez-faire economics is that increasing the minimum wage reduces the total number of minimum-wage jobs available. Statement2: In a recent study, however, it was found that after an increase in the minimum wage, fast-food restaurants kept on roughly the same number of minimum-wage employees as before the increase. Statement3: Therefore, laissez-faire economics is not entirely accurate.
hypothesis: Premise1: Minimum-wage job availability at fast-food restaurants included in the study was representative of minimum-wage job availability in general.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The national unemployment rate did not increase following the increase in the minimum wage.

premise: Statement1: Emissions from automobiles that burn gasoline and automobiles that burn diesel fuel are threatening the quality of life on our planet, contaminating both urban air and global atmosphere. Statement2: Therefore, the only effective way to reduce such emissions is to replace the conventional diesel fuel and gasoline used in automobiles with cleaner-burning fuels, such as methanol, that create fewer emissions.
hypothesis: Premise1: Reducing the use of automobiles would not be a more effective means to reduce automobile emissions than the use of methanol.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Automobile emissions constitute the most serious threat to the global environment.

premise: Statement1: Most banks that issue credit cards charge interest rates on credit card debt that are ten percentage points higher than the rates those banks charge for ordinary consumer loans. These banks'representatives claim the difference is fully justified, since it simply covers the difference between the costs to these banks associated with credit card debt and those associated with consumer loans.
hypothesis: Premise1: Two to three percent of the selling price of every item bought with a given credit card goes to the bank that issued that credit card.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most car rental companies require that their customers provide signed credit card charge slips or security deposits.

premise: Statement1: Petrochemical industry officials have said that the extreme pressure exerted on plant managers during the last five years to improve profits by cutting costs has done nothing to impair the industry 's ability to operate safely. Statement2: However, environmentalists contend that the recent rash of serious oil spills and accidents at petrochemical plants is traceable to cost-cutting measures.
hypothesis: Premise1: Despite major cutbacks in most other areas of operation, the petrochemical industry has devoted more of its resources to environmental and safety measures in the last five years than in the preceding five years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There is evidence that the most damaging of the recent oil spills would have been prevented had cost-cutting measures not been instituted.

premise: Statement1: Private industry is trying to attract skilled research scientists by offering them high salaries. Statement2: As a result, most research scientists employed in private industry now earn 50 percent more than do comparably skilled research scientists employed by the government. Statement3: So, unless government-employed research scientists are motivated more by a sense of public duty than by their own interests, the government is likely to lose its most skilled research scientists to private industry, since none of these scientists would have problems finding private-sector jobs.
hypothesis: Premise1: The government does not provide its research scientists with unusually good working conditions or fringe benefits that more than compensate for the lower salaries they receive.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Research scientists employed in the private sector generally work longer hours than do researchers employed by the government.

premise: Statement1: Teacher to a student : you agree that it is bad to break promises. Statement2: But when we speak to each other we all make an implicit promise to tell the truth, and lying is the breaking of that promise. Statement3: So even if you promised jeanne that you would tell me she is home sick, you should not tell me that, if you know that she is well.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some implicit promises are worse to break than some explicit ones.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Breaking a promise leads to worse consequences than does telling a lie.

premise: Statement1: Political scientist : some analysts point to the government's acceptance of the recent protest rally as proof that the government supports freedom of popular expression. Statement2: But the government supports no such thing. Statement3: Supporting freedom of popular expression means accepting the expression of ideas that the government opposes as well as the expression of ideas that the government supports. Statement4: The message of the protest rally was one that the government entirely supports.
hypothesis: Premise1: The government would not have accepted a protest rally whose message it opposed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The message of the recent protest rally did not concern any function of the government.

premise: Statement1: Eva : a" smart highway" system should be installed, one that would monitor areawide traffic patterns and communicate with computers in vehicles or with programmable highway signs to give drivers information about traffic congestion and alternate routes. Statement2: Such a system, we can infer, would result in improved traffic flow in and around cities that would do more than improve drivers'tempers ; it would decrease the considerable loss of money and productivity that now results from traffic congestion. Statement3: Louis : there are already traffic reports on the radio. Statement4: Why would a" smart highway" system be any better? .
hypothesis: Premise1: Traffic flow in and around cities is not now so congested that significant improvement is impossible
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Older vehicles could not be fitted with equipment to receive signals sent by a "smart highway" system

premise: Statement1: Psychologist : satisfaction and happiness with one's family can not exist unless families have established strong values of mutual trust, love, and respect. Statement2: Such bonds are formed and strengthened only through making these key values the basis for all actions within the family. Statement3: It is obvious then that widespread reliance on movies and electronic media for entertainment has an inherently corrosive effect on one's family life.
hypothesis: Premise1: People who rely on movies and electronic information for entertainment are unable to form strong values of trust, love, and respect with their families.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Newspapers and other forms of print media strengthen, rather than weaken, family values.

premise: Statement1: Last year, mayor stephens established a special law-enforcement task force with the avowed mission of eradicating corruption in city government. The mayor's handpicked task force has now begun prosecuting a dozen city officials. Statement2: Since all of these officials were appointed by mayor bixby, mayor stephens'predecessor and longtime political foe, it is clear that those being prosecuted have been targeted because of their political affiliations.
hypothesis: Premise1: Almost all of the officials who have served in city government for any length of time are appointees of mayor bixby.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: All of the members of the anticorruption task force had other jobs in city government before the task force was formed.

premise: Statement1: Long-term and short-term relaxation training are two common forms of treatment for individuals experiencing problematic levels of anxiety. Statement2: Yet studies show that on average, regardless of which form of treatment one receives, symptoms of anxiety decrease to a normal level within the short-term-training time period. Statement3: Thus, for most people the generally more expensive long-term training is unwarranted.
hypothesis: Premise1: Recipients of long-term training are much less likely than recipients of short-term training to have recurrences of problematic levels of anxiety.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A decrease in symptoms of anxiety often occurs even with no treatment or intervention by a mental health professional.

premise: Statement1: There is no reason why the work of scientists has to be officially confirmed before being published. Statement2: There is a system in place for the confirmation or disconfirmation of scientific findings, namely, the replication of results by other scientists. Poor scientific work on the part of any one scientist, which can include anything from careless reporting practices to fraud, is not harmful. Statement3: It will be exposed and rendered harmless when other scientists conduct the experiments and obtain disconfirmatory results.
hypothesis: Premise1: Scientific experiments can go unchallenged for many years before they are replicated.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most scientists work as part of a team rather than alone.

premise: Statement1: The public is well aware that high blood cholesterol levels raise the risk of stroke caused by blood clots. Statement2: But a recent report concludes that people with low blood cholesterol levels are at increased risk of the other lethal type of stroke -- cerebral hemorrhage, caused when a brain artery bursts. The report suggests that because blood cholesterol plays a vital role in maintaining cell membranes, low blood cholesterol weakens artery walls, making them prone to rupture. Statement3: The conclusion thus supports a long-standing contention by japanese researchers that western diets better protect against cerebral hemorrhage than do non-western diets.
hypothesis: Premise1: Western diets result in higher blood cholesterol levels than do non-western diets.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: High blood cholesterol levels preclude the weakening of artery walls.

premise: Statement1: Motor oil serves to lubricate engines and thus retard engine wear. Statement2: A study was conducted to assess the effectiveness of various brands of motor oil by using them in taxicabs over a 6,000-mile test period. All the oils did equally well in retarding wear on pistons and cylinders, the relevant parts of the engine. Statement3: Hence, cheaper brands of oil are the best buys.
hypothesis: Premise1: Ability to retard engine wear is not the only property of motor oil important to the running of an engine.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Cheaper brands of motor oil are often used by knowledgeable automobile mechanics for their own cars.

premise: Statement1: The earth 's rivers constantly carry dissolved salts into its oceans. Statement2: Clearly, therefore, by taking the resulting increase in salt levels in the oceans over the past hundred years and then determining how many centuries of such increases it would have taken the oceans to reach current salt levels from a hypothetical initial salt-free state, the maximum age of the earth 's oceans can be accurately estimated.
hypothesis: Premise1: The quantities of dissolved salts deposited by rivers in the earth's oceans have not been unusually large during the past hundred years
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There is no method superior to that based on salt levels for estimating the maximum age of the earth's oceans

premise: Statement1: Deep tillage is even more deleterious to the world's topsoil supply than previously believed. For example, farmers who till deeply are ten times more likely to lose topsoil to erosion than are farmers who use no-till methods. Statement2: Results like these make it clear that farmers who now till deeply should strive, by using other topsoil aeration techniques, to incorporate no-till methods instead.
hypothesis: Premise1: Tilling by any method other than deep tillage is not a viable option.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Topsoil erosion does not make farmers want to till more deeply.

premise: Statement1: Organization president : the stationery and envelopes used in all of the mailings from our national headquarters are made from recycled paper, and we never put anything but letters in the envelopes. When the envelopes have windows, these windows are also made from recycled material. Statement2: Therefore the envelopes, and thus these mailings, are completely recyclable.
hypothesis: Premise1: The envelope windows made from recycled material are recyclable.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The mailings from the organization's national headquarters always use envelopes that have windows.

premise: Statement1: Psychologists recently conducted a study in which people from widely disparate cultures were asked to examine five photographs. Statement2: Each photograph depicted the face of a person expressing one of five basic human emotions -- fear, happiness, disgust, anger, and sadness. The people in the study were asked to identify the emotion being expressed in each photograph. Statement3: For each photograph, everyone identified the same emotion. Statement4: This shows that people are genetically predisposed to associate certain facial expressions with certain basic emotions.
hypothesis: Premise1: If there is a behavior common to people of widely disparate cultures, then there is probably a genetic predisposition to that behavior.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The people whose faces were depicted in the photographs were not all from the same culture.

premise: Statement1: Critic : it has been argued that the real value of a product is not equal to the price people are willing to pay for it, on the grounds that this price often exceeds the cost of the labor required to create it, and the excess goes to the investor as an unjustifiably acquired profit. Statement2: But since the existence of the product is as dependent on the investor's initial capital outlay as on the laborer's toil, this argument is fallacious.
hypothesis: Premise1: The real value of a product is not completely determined by the cost of the labor needed to bring the product into existence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People who make the initial investment required to create a product have a right to a greater percentage of the receipts from its sale than do those whose contributions occur later.

premise: Statement1: Citizens of parktown are worried by the increased frequency of serious crimes committed by local teenagers. In response, the city government has instituted a series of measures designed to keep teenagers at home in the late evening. Statement2: Even if the measures succeed in keeping teenagers at home, however, they are unlikely to affect the problem that concerns citizens, since most crimes committed by local teenagers take place between 3 p.m. and 6 p.m.
hypothesis: Premise1: The crimes committed by teenagers in the afternoon are mostly small thefts and inconsequential vandalism.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Any decrease in the need for police patrols in the late evening would not mean that there could be more intensive patrolling in the afternoon.

premise: Statement1: Legislator : we should not waste any more of the taxpayers'money on the government's job-creation program. Statement2: The unemployment rate in this country has actually risen since the program was begun, so the program has clearly been a failure.
hypothesis: Premise1: The unemployment rate would not have risen even more than it has if the job- creation program had not been in existence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the job-creation program had been run more efficiently, it could have better served its purpose.

premise: Statement1: Company president : most of our best sales representatives came to the job with a degree in engineering but little or no sales experience. Statement2: Thus, when we hire sales representatives, we should favor applicants who have engineering degrees but little or no sales experience over applicants with extensive sales experience but no engineering degrees.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most of the people hired by the company as sales representatives have had a degree in engineering but no sales experience.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most of the people who apply for a sales representative position with the company do not have a degree in engineering.

premise: Statement1: In western economies, more energy is used to operate buildings than to operate transportation. Much of the decline in energy consumption since the oil crisis of 1973 is due to more efficient use of energy in homes and offices. Statement2: New building technologies, which make lighting, heating, and ventilation systems more efficient, have cut billions of dollars from energy bills in the west. Statement3: Since energy savings from these efficiencies save several billion dollars per year today, we can conclude that 50 to 100 years from now they will save more than $ 200 billion per year ( calculated in current dollars ).
hypothesis: Premise1: Technology used to make buildings energy efficient will not become prohibitively expensive over the next century.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Another oil crisis will occur in the next 50 to 100 years.

premise: Statement1: Logging industry official : harvesting trees from old-growth forests for use in manufacture can reduce the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, since when large old trees die in the forest they decompose, releasing their stored carbon dioxide. Statement2: Harvesting old-growth forests would, moreover, make room for rapidly growing young trees, which absorb more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere than do trees in old-growth forests.
hypothesis: Premise1: Much of the organic matter from old-growth trees, unusable as lumber, is made into products that decompose rapidly.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A young tree contains less than half the amount of carbon dioxide that is stored in an old tree of the same species.

premise: Statement1: Ancient humans in eastern north america hunted mammoths until the mammoth disappeared from the area around 13,000 years ago. Statement2: Recently, a fossil bone with an engraving that depicts a mammoth was found in an ancient settlement in eastern north america. Statement3: This shows that the settlement was occupied at a time when mammoths lived in this area.
hypothesis: Premise1: The engraving was made during the time when the settlement was occupied
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The fossil on which the engraving was made was not a mammoth bone

premise: Statement1: Some members have criticized the club's president for inviting dr. hines to speak at the annual awards banquet without consulting other club members beforehand. So the current president acted appropriately in the way in which she invited dr. hines. Statement2: But a few years ago the previous club president hired a tax accountant even though he had not discussed it with club members beforehand.
hypothesis: Premise1: The club's previous president acted appropriately in hiring the tax accountant without first consulting other club members.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: At the time the previous club president hired the tax accountant, most club members did not expect to be consulted about such matters.

premise: Statement1: Since empathy is essential for people to be willing to follow moral codes that sometimes require them to ignore their own welfare to help others, civilized society could not exist without empathy.
hypothesis: Premise1: Civilized society can exist only if there are people who are willing to at least sometimes ignore their own welfare to help others.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People who feel empathy tend to ignore their own welfare for the sake of others.

premise: Statement1: In an experiment, some volunteers were assigned to take aerobics classes and others to take weight-training classes. Statement2: After three months, each performed an arduous mathematical calculation. Just after that challenge, the measurable stress symptoms of the volunteers in the aerobics classes were less than those of the volunteers in the weight-training classes. Statement3: This provides good evidence that aerobic exercise helps the body handle psychological stress.
hypothesis: Premise1: On average, the volunteers assigned to the aerobics classes got a greater amount of aerobic exercise overall during the experiment, including any exercise outside the classes, than did the volunteers assigned to the weight-training classes.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: On average, the volunteers who were assigned to the aerobics classes got more exercise in the months in which they took those classes than they had been getting before beginning the experiment.

premise: Statement1: A mathematical theorem proved by one mathematician should not be accepted until each step in its proof has been independently verified. Statement2: Computer-assisted proofs generally proceed by conducting a vast number of calculations -- surveying all the possible types of instances in which the theorem could apply and proving that the theorem holds for each type. Statement3: In most computer-assisted proofs there are astronomically many types of instances to survey, and no human being could review every step in the proof. Statement4: Hence, computer-assisted proofs involving astronomically many types of instances should not be accepted.
hypothesis: Premise1: The use of an independent computer program does not satisfy the requirement for independent verification of each step in a proof that is extended enough to be otherwise unverifiable.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Computers cannot be used to assist in generating proofs of mathematical theorems that involve only a very limited number of steps.

premise: Statement1: The master plan for the new park calls for the planting of trees of any species native to this area, except for those native trees that grow to be very large, such as the cottonwood. Statement2: The trees that the community group donated were purchased at three rivers nursery, which sells mostly native trees and shrubs. Statement3: Thus, the donated trees are probably consistent with the master plan.
hypothesis: Premise1: Three rivers nursery does not sell any tree species that grow to be very large.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Three rivers nursery sells cottonwood trees.

premise: Statement1: In a study of patients who enrolled at a sleep clinic because of insomnia, those who inhaled the scent of peppermint before going to bed were more likely to have difficulty falling asleep than were patients who inhaled the scent of bitter orange. Statement2: Since it is known that inhaling bitter orange does not help people fall asleep more easily, this study shows that inhaling the scent of peppermint makes insomnia worse.
hypothesis: Premise1: The patients who inhaled the scent of bitter orange were, on average, suffering from milder cases of insomnia than were the patients who inhaled the scent of peppermint.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some of the patients who enrolled in the sleep clinic also had difficulty staying asleep once they fell asleep.

premise: Statement1: Sociologist : many rural residents claim to only approve of modest lifestyles ; yet, many of these same rural residents often buy magazines focused on the lives of celebrities. Statement2: However, it is proven that humans are often fascinated by the very things they condemn. Statement3: Therefore, it is predictable that these celebrity magazines would be popular with these rural residents.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most rural residents who buy the magazines focused on celebrities do not regard the celebrity lifestyles as modest.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: All people who buy magazines focused on the lives of celebrities condemn such lifestyles.

premise: Statement1: Last year, pharmaceutical manufacturers significantly increased the amount of money they spent promoting new drugs, which they do mainly by sending sales representatives to visit physicians in their offices. Statement2: However, two years ago there was an average of 640 such visits per representative, whereas last year that figure fell to 501. sent3: so the additional promotion must have been counterproductive, making physicians less willing to receive visits by pharmaceutical sales representatives.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most pharmaceutical manufacturers increased the size of their sales forces so that their sales representatives could devote more time to each physician.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The more visits a physician receives from a pharmaceutical sales representative, the more likely he or she is to prescribe drugs made by that representative's company.

premise: Statement1: To decrease the number of crimes in city y, the city's police commissioner proposed taking some police officers from low-crime districts of the city and moving them to high-crime districts of the city. Statement2: His proposal is based on city y crime data that show that the number of crimes in any district of the city decreases when additional police officers are moved into that district.
hypothesis: Premise1: Districts of the city from which police officers are removed do not experience significant crime increases shortly after the removal of those officers.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: City x experienced a drastic reduction in crime after implementing a proposal similar to that proposed by the police commissioner of city y.

premise: Statement1: Historian : fifteenth-century advances in mapmaking contributed to the rise of modern nation-states. Statement2: In medieval europe ( from the fifth to the fifteenth century ), sovereignty centered in cities and towns and radiated outward, with boundaries often ambiguously defined. Statement3: The conceptual shift toward the modern state began in the late fifteenth century, when mapmakers learned to reflect geography accurately by basing maps on latitude-longitude grids. Statement4: By the midseventeenth century, nearly all maps showed boundary lines.
hypothesis: Premise1: During the fifteenth century in europe, mapmakers simplified the borders of sovereignty by drawing clear lines of demarcation between political powers.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Borders did not become codified in europe until certain treaties were signed in the early nineteenth century.

premise: Statement1: A person reading a new book for pleasure is like a tourist traveling to a new place. Statement2: The reader reads, just as the tourist travels, to enlarge understanding rather than simply to acquire information. Statement3: Thus, it is better to read fewer books and spend more time on each rather than to quickly read as many as one can, just as it is better to travel to fewer places and spend more time in each rather than to spend a small amount of time in many different places.
hypothesis: Premise1: Tourists gain much more understanding of a place once they have spent several days at that place than they do in their first few days there.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Tourists typically learn something about the places they visit even when they are there only to relax.

premise: Statement1: In the united states, of the people who moved from one state to another when they retired, the percentage who retired to florida has decreased by three percentage points over the past ten years. Statement2: Since many local businesses in florida cater to retirees, this decline is likely to have a noticeably negative economic effect on these businesses.
hypothesis: Premise1: The total number of people who retired and moved to another state for their retirement has increased significantly over the past ten years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The number of people who move out of florida to accept employment in other states has increased over the past ten years.

premise: Statement1: Cognitive scientist : using the pioneering work of comparative psychologist gordon gallup as a model, several studies have investigated animals'capacity for mirror self-recognition ( msr ). Statement2: Most animals exposed to a mirror respond only with social behavior, such as aggression. However, in the case of the great apes, repeated exposure to mirrors leads to self-directed behaviors, such as exploring the inside of the mouth, suggesting that these animals recognize the reflection as an image of self. Statement3: The implication of these studies is that the great apes have a capacity for self-awareness unique among nonhuman species.
hypothesis: Premise1: If an animal does not have the capacity for msr, it does not have the capacity for self-awareness.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Animals that do not exhibit msr may demonstrate a capacity for self-awareness in other ways.

premise: Statement1: Despite increasing international efforts to protect the natural habitats of endangered species of animals, the rate at which these species are becoming extinct continues to rise. Statement2: It is clear that these efforts are wasted.
hypothesis: Premise1: Species that would have become extinct have been saved due to the establishment of animal refuges.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Scientists estimate that at least 2000 species become extinct every year.

premise: Statement1: Genuine happiness consists not in pleasurable feelings but instead in one's sense of approval of one's character and projects. Statement2: Thus the happy life, in fact, tends to be the good life, where the good life is understood not -- as it usually is these days -- as a life of material well-being but rather as a morally virtuous life.
hypothesis: Premise1: People who approve of their own character and projects tend to lead morally virtuous lives.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Attaining happiness is the real goal of people who strive for material well-being.

premise: Statement1: Safety consultant : judged by the number of injuries per licensed vehicle, minivans are the safest vehicles on the road. Statement2: However, in carefully designed crash tests, minivans show no greater ability to protect their occupants than other vehicles of similar size do. Statement3: Thus, the reason minivans have such a good safety record is probably not that they are inherently safer than other vehicles, but rather that they are driven primarily by low-risk drivers.
hypothesis: Premise1: Minivans generally have worse braking and emergency handling capabilities than other vehicles of similar size.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Minivans tend to carry more passengers at any given time than do most other vehicles.

premise: Statement1: Essayist : when the first prehistoric migrations of humans from asia to north america took place, the small bands of new arrivals encountered many species of animals that would be extinct only 2,000 years later. Statement2: Since it is implausible that hunting by these small bands of humans could have had such an effect, and since disease-causing microorganisms not native to north america were undoubtedly borne by the new arrivals as well as by the animals that followed them, these microorganisms were probably the crucial factor that accounts for the extinctions.
hypothesis: Premise1: Very few species of north american animals not hunted by the new arrivals from asia were extinct 2, 000 years after the first migrations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Individual humans and animals can carry a disease-causing microorganism without themselves suffering from the disease.

premise: Statement1: One test to determine whether a person has been infected with tuberculosis consists of injecting the person with proteins extracted from the tuberculosis bacterium. Statement2: Once a person has been infected by a bacterium, the person's immune system subsequently recognizes certain proteins present in that bacterium and attacks the bacterium. This recognition also takes place in the test and results in a skin irritation at the injection site. Statement3: Hence the physicians who designed the test reasoned that anyone who reacts in this manner to an injection with the tuberculosis proteins has been infected with tuberculosis.
hypothesis: Premise1: The ability of the proteins present in the tuberculosis bacterium to trigger the skin irritation is exclusive to that bacterium.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: All of the proteins present in disease-causing bacteria can be recognized by the body's immune system.

premise: Statement1: The makers of activite, a natural dietary supplement, claim that it promotes energy and mental alertness. Statement2: To back up their claim, they offer a month's supply of activite free to new customers. Statement3: Clearly, activite must be effective, since otherwise it would not be in the company's interest to make such an offer.
hypothesis: Premise1: The makers of activite charge a handling fee that is considerably more than what it costs them to pack and ship their product.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are less expensive dietary supplements on the market that are just as effective as activite.

premise: Statement1: To cut costs, a high school modified its airconditioning system to increase its efficiency. The modified system, however, caused the humidity in the school air to decrease by 18 percent. Statement2: Twenty-four hours after the decrease in air humidity, a 25 percent increase in the number of visits to the school nurse was reported. Statement3: This shows that a decrease in humidity can make people ill.
hypothesis: Premise1: At least some of the visits to the school nurse after the system was modified were due to illness.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: It takes 24 hours after a person is infected with a virus for that person to exhibit symptoms.

premise: Statement1: Early in the twentieth century, lake konfa became very polluted. Statement2: Recently fish populations have recovered as release of industrial pollutants has declined and the lake's waters have become cleaner. Statement3: Fears are now being voiced that the planned construction of an oil pipeline across the lake's bottom might revive pollution and cause the fish population to decline again. Statement4: However, a technology for preventing leaks is being installed. Statement5: Therefore, provided this technology is effective, the fears are groundless.
hypothesis: Premise1: The pipeline's construction will disturb sediments at the bottom of the lake, dispersing into the water toxic pollutants that have settled there.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The leak-preventing technology has been in use for several years without any pipeline in which it is installed developing serious leaks.

premise: Statement1: Certain groups of asian snails include both" left-handed" and" right-handed" species, with shells coiling to the left and right, respectively. Statement2: Some left-handed species have evolved from right-handed ones. Statement3: Also, researchers found that snaileating snakes in the same habitat have asymmetrical jaws, allowing them to grasp right-handed snail shells more easily. Statement4: If these snakes ate more right-handed snails over time, this would have given left-handed snails an evolutionary advantage over right-handed snails, with the left handed snails eventually becoming a new species. Statement5: Thus, the snakes'asymmetrical jaws probably helped drive the emergence of the left-handed snail species.
hypothesis: Premise1: Experiments show that the snail-eating snakes in this habitat fail more often in trying to eat left-handed snails than in trying to eat right-handed snails.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some right-handed snails in this habitat have shells with a very narrow opening that helps prevent snakes from extracting the snails from inside their shells.

premise: Statement1: Economist : the wages of many of the lowest-paid corporate employees in this country would be protected from cuts by enacting a maximum wage law that prohibits executives at any corporation from earning more than, say, 50 times what the corporation's lowest-paid employees in this country earn. A maximum wage law would remove this incentive for these executives to cut the wages of their lowest-paid employees. Statement2: Currently, some executives try to increase corporate profits -- and their own salaries -- by cutting the pay and benefits of their corporations'employees.
hypothesis: Premise1: If such a maximum wage law were enacted in the economist's country, one or more corporate executives would not cut the pay and benefits of their corporations' lowest-paid employees in the economist's country.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No corporate executives in the economist's country would raise the wages of their corporations' lowest-paid employees in the economist's country unless such a maximum wage law linked executive wages to those of their corporations' lowest-paid employees in the economist's country.

premise: Statement1: Readers give more credibility and respond more positively to negative restaurant reviews if the critics begin their reviews by discussing the things they enjoyed about the restaurant. Statement2: Studies show that readers enjoy hearing positive comments before being told unpleasant details. Statement3: Therefore, any restaurant critic should make sure to start every review by giving ample praise about the positive aspects of the restaurant before launching into any negative commentary.
hypothesis: Premise1: Newspapers and magazines will often only publish the beginning of a restaurant review to save space.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Restaurant critics often receive bribes from restaurants to write positive reviews.

premise: Statement1: A recent study showed that the immune system blood cells of the study's participants who drank tea but no coffee took half as long to respond to germs as did the blood cells of participants who drank coffee but no tea. Statement2: Thus, drinking tea boosted the participants'immune system defenses.
hypothesis: Premise1: In the study, drinking coffee did not cause the blood cell response time to double.

premise: Statement1: Data from satellite photographs of the tropical rain forest in melonia show that last year the deforestation rate of this environmentally sensitive zone was significantly lower than in previous years. Statement2: The melonian government, which spent millions of dollars last year to enforce laws against burning and cutting of the forest, is claiming that the satellite data indicate that its increased efforts to halt the destruction are proving effective.
hypothesis: Premise1: Rainfall during the usually dry 6-month annual burning season was abnormally heavy last year.

premise: Statement1: During its caterpillar stage, the leopard magpie moth feeds on a plant called the natal grass cycad and by so doing laces its body with macrozamin, a toxin that makes the moth highly unpalatable to would-be predators. Statement2: Since the natal grass cycad is now endangered and facing extinction, the leopard magpie moth is also in danger of extinction.

premise: Statement1: Animal feed should not include genetically modified plants. hypothesis: Premise1: Potatoes are not normally a part of the diet of laboratory rats.

premise: Statement1: In setting environmental standards for industry and others to meet, it is inadvisable to require the best results that state-of-the-art technology can achieve. Statement2: Current technology is able to detect and eliminate even extremely minute amounts of contaminants, but at a cost that is exorbitant relative to the improvement achieved. alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The only contaminants worth measuring are generated by industry

premise: Statement1: Early in the development of a new product line, the critical resource is talent. New marketing ventures require a degree of managerial skill disproportionate to their short-term revenue prospects. hypothesis: Premise1: The current revenue and profitability of an established product line can be maintained even if the company's best managers are assigned elsewhere.

premise: Statement1: Poetry journal patron : everybody who publishes in the brick wall review has to agree in advance that if a poem is printed in one of its regular issues, the magazine also has the right to reprint it, without monetary compensation, in its annual anthology. The brick wall review makes enough money from sales of its anthologies to cover most operating expenses. premise: Statement1: Although the discount stores in goreville's central shopping district are expected to close within five years as a result of competition from a spendless discount department store that just opened, those locations will not stay vacant for long. premise: Statement1: Scientists have discovered a new species of butterfly that lives only in a small region of central america and is active only at night. Statement2: During the day, it rests in treetops, where its green color matches the foliage perfectly. Statement3: Therefore, the scientists must have discovered the butterfly at night.
hypothesis: Premise1: There is no way for the scientists to detect the butterfly during the day.

premise: Statement1: A country's employment in the manufacturing industry has dramatically decreased over the last thirty years. During that same period, the country has led the world in developing automation. Statement2: Therefore, automation is the reason behind the decreased unemployment in the manufacturing industry.
hypothesis: Premise1: The manufacturing industry uses automation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The manufacturing industry is driving the automation.

premise: Statement1: Herpetologist : some psychologists attribute complex reasoning to reptiles, claiming that simple stimulus-response explanations of some reptiles'behaviors, such as food gathering, can not account for the complexity of such behavior. Statement2: But since experiments show that reptiles are incapable of making major alterations in their behavior, for example, when faced with significant changes in their environment, these animals must be incapable of complex reasoning.
hypothesis: Premise1: If reptiles were capable of complex reasoning, they would sometimes be able to make major changes in their behavior.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Reptile behavior appears more complex in the field than laboratory experiments reveal it to be.

premise: Statement1: Everyone likes repertory theater. Statement2: Actors like it because playing different roles each night decreases their level of boredom. Statement3: Stagehands like it because changing sets every night means more overtime and, thus, higher pay. Statement4: Theater managers like it because, if plays that reflect audience demand are chosen for production, most performances generate large revenues. Statement5: It is evident, therefore, that more theaters should change to repertory.
hypothesis: Premise1: In a repertory theater, plays can be rescheduled to meet audience demand.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In a repertory theater, storage space for sets for more than one production must be available.

premise: Statement1: Anthropologist : one of the distinctive traits of humans is the ability to support a large brain with a small gut, which requires getting more calories from less food. Statement2: It was likely the development of cooking that made this possible. Statement3: After all, our ancestors developed large brains around the time that they began to control fire. Statement4: And even today, people who choose to eat only raw food have difficulty getting enough calories.
hypothesis: Premise1: The human body uses more calories to process raw food than it uses to process cooked food.

premise: Statement1: Proposed new safety rules for the beach city airport would lengthen considerably the minimum time between takeoffs from the airport. The city 's operating budget depends heavily on taxes generated by tourist spending, and most of the tourists come by plane. hypothesis: Premise1: The response to the adoption of the new safety rules would not include an increase in the number of passengers per flight.

premise: Statement1: The city of workney, in raising bus fares from $ 1.00 to $ 1.25, proposed that 18 fare tokens be sold for $ 20.00 to alleviate the extra burden of the fare increase on the city's poor people. hypothesis: Premise1: The outlay of $10. 00 for the purchase of 9 fare tokens would not be prohibitive for bus riders who are poor.

premise: Statement1: Lightbox, inc., owns almost all of the movie theaters in washington county and has announced plans to double the number of movie screens it has in the county within five years. Yet attendance at lightbox's theaters is only just large enough for profitability now and the county's sent3: population is not expected to increase over the next ten years. premise: Statement1: Columnist : over the last 20 years the demand in north america for japanese-made automobiles has increased, whereas the japanese demand for north american-made automobiles has been stagnant. Statement2: Until recently, this imbalance could plausibly be attributed to japanese models'superior fuel efficiency and reliability, but now cars made in north america are by these standards the equal of any japanese model. What north american exporters continue to ignore is that the japanese drive on the left side of the road. Statement3: Therefore, one obstacle to reducing the automotive trade imbalance will be removed if north american manufacturers simply produce more cars with right-side steering wheels.

premise: Statement1: Editorialist : in a large corporation, one of the functions of the corporation's president is to promote the key interests of the shareholders. Statement2: Therefore, the president has a duty to keep the corporation's profits high.
hypothesis: Premise1: Keeping a corporation's profits high is likely to advance the important interests of the corporation's shareholders.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In considering where to invest, most potential shareholders are interested in more than just the profitability of a corporation.

premise: Statement1: The vomeronasal organ ( vno ) is found inside the noses of various animals. While its structural development and function are clearer in other animals, most humans have a vno that is detectable, though only microscopically. Statement2: When researchers have been able to stimulate vno cells in humans, the subjects have reported experiencing subtle smell sensations. Statement3: It seems, then, that the vno, though not completely understood, is a functioning sensory organ in most humans.
hypothesis: Premise1: It is not known whether the researchers succeeded in stimulating only vno cells in the human subjects' noses.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Relative to its occurrence in certain other animals, the human vno appears to be anatomically rudimentary and underdeveloped.

premise: Statement1: Journalist : the new mayor is undeniably bold. Statement2: His assertions are made with utter certainty and confidence. Statement3: While these kinds of assertions may make him popular with the public, they also demonstrate that he is not an introspective person.
hypothesis: Premise1: Introspective people do not make assertions with utter certainty and confidence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Politicians who make assertions with utter certainty and confidence are popular with the public.

premise: Statement1: A smoker trying to quit is more likely to succeed if his or her doctor greatly exaggerates the dangers of smoking. Statement2: Similar strategies can be used to break other habits. Statement3: But since such strategies involve deception, individuals can not easily adopt them unless a doctor or some other third party provides the warning.
hypothesis: Premise1: People generally do not find it easy to deceive themselves.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A doctor is justified in deceiving a patient whenever doing so is likely to make the patient healthier.

premise: Statement1: Mayor : false alarms from home security systems waste so much valuable police time that in many communities police have stopped responding to alarms from homes whose systems frequently produce false alarms. Statement2: This policy reduces wastage of police time but results in a loss of protection for some residents. Statement3: To achieve a comparable reduction in wastage without reducing protection for residents, the council has enacted a measure to fine residents for repeated false alarms.
hypothesis: Premise1: The threat of fines is likely to cause many residents to deactivate their security systems.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many home security systems have never produced false alarms.

premise: Statement1: It is undeniable that regular consumption of fast food can lead to serious health problems. However, most people agree that fast food companies should not be held liable for the effects their product has on the health of consumers. Statement2: Few people, however, disagree with holding tobacco companies liable for the serious health problems caused by cigarette usage. Statement3: Therefore, fast food must not be as unhealthy as cigarettes.
hypothesis: Premise1: That health concerns are the only reason people believe tobacco companies should be held liable
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: That the long-term health impact is the same for cigarettes as fast food

premise: Statement1: A member of the british parliament is reputed to have said, " the first purpose of good social reform is to increase the sum total of human happiness. Statement2: So, any reform which makes somebody happy is achieving its purpose. Statement3: Since the reform i propose would make my constituents happy, it is a good social reform. "
hypothesis: Premise1: Increasing some people's happiness might not increase the sum total of human happiness if others are made unhappy.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The proposed reform would affect only the member of parliament's constituents and would make them happy.

premise: Statement1: When a group of children who have been watching television programs that include acts of violence is sent to play with a group of children who have been watching programs that do not include acts of violence, the children who have been watching violent programs commit a much greater number of violent acts in their play than do the children who have been watching nonviolent programs. Statement2: Therefore, children at play can be prevented from committing violent acts by not being allowed to watch violence on television.
hypothesis: Premise1: There are no other differences between the two groups of children that might account for the difference in violent behavior.

premise: Statement1: Physician : in an experiment, 50 patients with chronic back pain were divided into two groups. Statement2: Small magnets were applied to the backs of one group ; the other group received no treatment. Most of the patients in the first group, but very few in the second group, reported a significant reduction in pain. Statement3: This shows that magnetic fields are probably effective at relieving some back pain.
hypothesis: Premise1: A patient's merely knowing that a treatment has been applied can lead to improvement in his or her condition.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There was wide variation in the specific causes of the chronic back pain suffered by the patients in the experiment.

premise: Statement1: High schools should only teach abstinence. Statement2: Students who practice abstinence will never experience an unplanned pregnancy or contract a sexually-transmitted disease.
hypothesis: Premise1: Religious organizations support the teaching of abstinence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Failing to teach about contraceptives increases the spread of sexually-transmitted diseases.

premise: Statement1: Environmentalist : when bacteria degrade household cleaning products, vapors that are toxic to humans are produced. Statement2: Unfortunately, household cleaning products are often found in landfills. Statement3: Thus, the common practice of converting landfills into public parks is damaging human health.
hypothesis: Premise1: In at least some landfills that have been converted into public parks there are bacteria that degrade household cleaning products.

premise: Statement1: Pollster : when opinion researchers need a population sample that reflects the demographic characteristics of the national population, they choose their sample on the basis of national census data. Not everyone participates in the national census, despite its being mandatory. Statement2: If, however, census participation became voluntary, as some have proposed, the participation rate would be much lower. Statement3: So if census participation became voluntary, polls designed to discover the opinions of the national population would have less accurate results.
hypothesis: Premise1: The group of people who would participate in a voluntary national census would differ in its demographic characteristics from the group of people who would participate in a mandatory national census.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Using data from the national census is not the only way for opinion researchers to get a population sample that reflects the demographic characteristics of the national population.

premise: Statement1: Compared to us, people who lived a century ago had very few diversions to amuse them. Statement2: Therefore, they likely read much more than we do today.
hypothesis: Premise1: On average, people who lived a century ago had considerably less leisure time than we do today.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The number of books sold today is larger than it was a century ago.

premise: Statement1: Although withholding information from someone who would find that information painful is sometimes justified, there is no such justification if the person would benefit from having the information. Statement2: Therefore, even though it would be painful for jason to learn that his supervisor is displeased with his work, his colleague jane should nonetheless inform jason of this fact, for knowing that his supervisor was displeased would enable jason to improve his supervisor's opinion of his work.
hypothesis: Premise1: Jason would benefit if he were able to improve his supervisor's opinion of his work.

premise: Statement1: Mate is a beverage found in much of south america. Statement2: While it is uncertain where mate was first made, there are more varieties of it found in paraguay than anywhere else. Statement3: Also, mate is used more widely there than anywhere else. Statement4: Therefore, paraguay is likely the place where mate originated.
hypothesis: Premise1: Typically, the longer a beverage has been in use in a particular place, the more widely that beverage is used there.

premise: Statement1: We can learn about the living conditions of a vanished culture by examining its language. Statement2: Thus, it is likely that the people who spoke proto-indo-european, the language from which all indoeuropean languages descended, lived in a cold climate, isolated from ocean or sea, because protoindo-european lacks a word for" sea, " yet contains words for" winter, "" snow, " and" wolf. "
hypothesis: Premise1: Some languages lack words for prominent elements of the environments of their speakers.
