premise: Statement1: The five senses have traditionally been viewed as distinct yet complementary. Each sense is thought to have its own range of stimuli that are incapable of stimulating the other senses. Statement2: However, recent research has discovered that some people taste a banana and claim that they are tasting blue, or see a color and say that it has a specific smell. Statement3: This shows that such people, called synesthesiacs, have senses that do not respect the usual boundaries between the five recognized senses.
hypothesis: Premise1: Synesthesiacs demonstrate a general, systematic impairment in their ability to use and understand words.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Synesthesiacs can be temporarily rid of their synesthetic experiences by the use of drugs.

premise: Statement1: College is increasingly unaffordable for everyone that is not independently wealthy, outpacing inflation every year since the early 1970s. Statement2: This year the average cost of tuition at a private, four-year university is more than $ 31,000 per year. In 1971, tuition cost less than $ 2,000, even after adjusting for inflation. Statement3: The trend is similar at public, four-year institutions. Statement4: Fortunately, with the advent of the internet, independent learning is easier than ever before. Statement5: Students can learn hard skills online for relatively minimal costs, like coding and web design. Online courses can also replicate a traditional college education, with options such as math, science, and liberal arts courses. Statement6: As a result, high school students would be wise to weigh their options before choosing to attend a traditional four-y ear college.
hypothesis: Premise1: Employers increasingly value work experience and self-starters more than formal education.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Students who do not attend traditional college miss out on important life experiences.

premise: Statement1: Environmentalist : an increased number of oil spills and the consequent damage to the environment indicate the need for stricter safety standards for the oil industry. Statement2: Since the industry refuses to take action, it is the national government that must regulate industry safety standards. In particular, the government has to at least require oil companies to put double hulls on their tankers and to assume financial responsibility for accidents. Statement3: Industry representative : the industry alone should be responsible for devising safety standards because of its expertise in handling oil and its understanding of the cost entailed. Statement4: Implementing the double-hull proposal is not currently feasible because it creates new safety issues. Statement5: Furthermore, the cost would be burdensome to the industry and consumers.
hypothesis: Premise1: The only effective sources of increased stringency in safety standards for oil tankers are action by the industry itself or national government regulation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The requirement of two hulls on oil tankers, although initially costly, will save money over time by reducing cleanup costs.

premise: Statement1: After the second world war, the charter of the newly formed united nations established an eleven-member security council and charged it with taking collective action in response to threats to world peace. Statement2: The charter further provided that the five nations that were then the major powers would permanently have sole authority to cast vetoes. Statement3: The reason given for this arrangement was that the burden of maintaining world peace would rest on the world's major powers, and no nation should be required to assume the burden of enforcing a decision it found repugnant.
hypothesis: Premise1: No nation that was not among the major powers at the end of the second world war would become a major power
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Decisions reached by a majority of nations in response to threats to world peace would be biased in favor of one or more major powers

premise: Statement1: Until he was dismissed amid great controversy, hastings was considered one of the greatest intelligence agents of all time. Soon after the dismissal, however, it was shown that he had never been incompetent. Statement2: It is clear that if his dismissal was justified, then hastings was either incompetent or else disloyal. Statement3: Thus, one is forced to conclude that hastings must have been disloyal.
hypothesis: Premise1: Hastings's dismissal was justified.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If someone was disloyal or incompetent, then his dismissal was justified.

premise: Statement1: The press reports on political campaigns these days as if they were chess games. Statement2: One candidate's campaign advisor makes a move ; the other candidate's advisor makes a countermove. Statement3: The press then reports on the campaign advisors and not on the candidates. Statement4: The losers in this chess game are the voters. Statement5: They are deprived of the information they need to make informed decisions because the press is ignoring substantive policy issues and reporting only on the process of the campaign. Statement6: It is clear that the campaign advisors should stay out of the limelight and let the press report on the most revealing positions on substantive issues the candidates have taken.
hypothesis: Premise1: The candidates in the election are taking positions on substantive policy issues.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There is no difference between reporting on the political process and reporting on substantive issues.

premise: Statement1: Tiger sharks are common in the waters surrounding tenare island. Statement2: Usually tiger sharks feed on smaller sharks, but sometimes they have attacked tourists swimming and surfing at tenare's beaches. Statement3: This has hurt tenare's tourism industry, which is second only to its fishing industry in annual revenues. Statement4: In order to help the economy, therefore, the mayor of the island has proposed an ongoing program to kill any tiger sharks within a mile of the beaches.
hypothesis: Premise1: The small sharks on which tiger sharks prey feed on fish that are commercially important to the island's fisheries.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Not all tourists who come to tenare island enjoy swimming or surfing.

premise: Statement1: Columnist : shortsighted motorists learn the hard way about the wisdom of preventive auto maintenance ; such maintenance almost always pays off in the long run. Statement2: Our usually shortsighted city council should be praised for using similar wisdom when they hired a long-term economic development adviser. In hiring this adviser, the council made an investment that is likely to have a big payoff in several years. Statement3: Other cities in this region that have devoted resources to economic development planning have earned large returns on such an investment.
hypothesis: Premise1: The columnist's city has a much smaller population and economy than the other cities did when they began devoting resources to economic development planning.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Even some cars that receive regular preventive maintenance break down, requiring costly repairs.

premise: Statement1: Moralist : immoral actions are those that harm other people. Statement2: But since such actions eventually harm those who perform them, those who act immorally do so only through ignorance of some of their actions'consequences rather than through a character defect.
hypothesis: Premise1: Those who, in acting immorally, eventually harm themselves do not intend that harm.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: None of those who knowingly harm themselves lack character defects.

premise: Statement1: Mayor : the city council has proposed additional funding for the town library. Statement2: However, the library 's own studies show that fewer than 10 % of town residents use the library. The city council claims that the library is underutilized because the books, electronic media, and computer equipment are outdated, and that if these were updated, more residents would use the library. Statement3: However, the last time the town allocated additional funding to the library, usage of the library did not increase. Statement4: Therefore the town should not allocate additional funding for the library.
hypothesis: Premise1: The last time the town allocated additional funding for the library, the funding was used to repair the crumbling staircase and ramp leading to the library entrance.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Usage of the library did increase in 1994, when the town voted to allocate funds for an advertising campaign promoting reading.

premise: Statement1: Microbiologist : a lethal strain of salmonella recently showed up in a european country, causing an outbreak of illness that killed two people and infected twenty-seven others. Investigators blame the severity of the outbreak on the overuse of antibiotics, since the salmonella bacteria tested were shown to be drug-resistant. Statement2: But this is unlikely because patients in the country where the outbreak occurred can not obtain antibiotics to treat illness without a prescription, and the country 's doctors prescribe antibiotics less readily than do doctors in any other european country.
hypothesis: Premise1: People in the country where the outbreak occurred often consume foods produced from animals that eat antibiotics-laden livestock feed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Physicians in the country where the outbreak occurred have become hesitant to prescribe antibiotics since they are frequently in short supply.

premise: Statement1: Patti : most parents are eager for their preschoolers to learn as much as possible. Statement2: However, instead of providing general opportunities for their children to learn, parents often direct their children's learning to their own personal concerns. Statement3: Because children have a natural curiosity and thirst for knowledge, they learn an enormous amount simply through growing and adapting to the world. Statement4: Therefore, this type of directed learning is unlikely to improve a child's preschool education.
hypothesis: Premise1: The type of directed learning in question is not a necessary part of the process of growing and adapting to the world.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The type of directed learning in question is likely to enhance the general opportunities for children to learn.

premise: Statement1: In 1860 bavarian quarry workers discovered the impression of a feather in a limestone slab dating to the mesozoic era. But there in limestone lay the imprint of a fully aerodynamic, three-inch-long feather. Statement2: It had previously been assumed that birds developed only after the close of the mesozoic era and after the disappearance of pterosaurs, a species characteristic of that era. Statement3: This, therefore, must have been the earliest bird -- certainly, the earliest found to that date.
hypothesis: Premise1: In the mesozoic era, no creatures other than birds had such feathers.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The feather belonged to a mesozoic creature that was neither a pterosaur nor a bird, but an intermediate between them.

premise: Statement1: In a certain democratic country the legislature passed a new tax law over the principled objections of the parliamentary opposition. Statement2: Some opposition leaders broke the new law by refusing to pay the new tax. They defended their lawbreaking by citing the historical precedent in the country of activists'having to break laws in winning for women the right to vote.
hypothesis: Premise1: The opposition leaders, unlike the activists, were part of the democratic process that they are defying.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Although they had principled objections to the new law, the opposition leaders derived a personal monetary benefit from breaking the law.

premise: Statement1: Columnist : much of north america and western europe is more heavily forested and has less acid rain and better air quality now than five decades ago. Statement2: Though this may be due largely to policies advocated by environmentalists, it nonetheless lends credibility to the claims of people who reject predictions of imminent ecological doom and argue that environmental policies that excessively restrict the use of natural resources may diminish the wealth necessary to adopt and sustain the policies that brought about these improvements.
hypothesis: Premise1: Nations sustain their wealth largely through industrial use of the natural resources found within their boundaries.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The concern demonstrated by a nation for the health and integrity of its natural ecosystems leads to an increase in that nation's wealth.

premise: Statement1: An overly centralized economy, not the changes in the climate, is responsible for the poor agricultural production in country x since its new government came to power. Statement2: Neighboring country y has experienced the same climatic conditions, but while agricultural production has been falling in country x, it has been rising in country y.
hypothesis: Premise1: The crops that have always been grown in country x are different from those that have always been grown in country y.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Both country x and country y have been experiencing drought conditions.

premise: Statement1: Critic : works of literature often present protagonists who scorn allegiance to their society and who advocate detachment rather than civic-mindedness. However, modern literature is distinguished from the literature of earlier eras in part because it more frequently treats such protagonists sympathetically. Statement2: Sympathetic treatment of such characters suggests to readers that one should be unconcerned about contributing to societal good. Statement3: Thus, modern literature can damage individuals who appropriate this attitude, as well as damage society at large.
hypothesis: Premise1: It is to the advantage of some individuals that they be concerned with contributing to societal good.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some individuals must believe that their society is better than most before they can become concerned with benefiting it.

premise: Statement1: The profitability of a business is reduced by anything that undermines employee morale. Statement2: This is why paying senior staff with stock options, which allows them to earn more when the enterprise prospers, is not a wise policy because it increases dramatically the difference in income between senior staff and employees who are paid only a fixed salary.
hypothesis: Premise1: Large income differences between fixed-salary employees and senior staff tend to undermine employee morale.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Employees whose incomes rise as the profits of their employers rise are more productive than those paid only a fixed salary.

premise: Statement1: Harry trevalga : you and your publication have unfairly discriminated against my poems. Statement2: I have submitted thirty poems in the last two years and you have not published any of them! Statement3: It is all because i won the fenner poetry award two years ago and your poetry editor thought she deserved it. Statement4: Publisher : ridiculous! Statement5: Our editorial policy and practice is perfectly fair, since our poetry editor judges all submissions for publication without ever seeing the names of the poets, and hence can not possibly have known who wrote your poems.
hypothesis: Premise1: The poetry editor cannot recognize the poems submitted by harry trevalga as his unless trevalga's name is attached to them.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The poetry editor's decisions on which poems to publish are not based strictly on judgments of intrinsic merit.

premise: Statement1: It is widely assumed that people need to engage in intellectual activities such as solving crossword puzzles or mathematics problems in order to maintain mental sharpness as they age. In fact, however, simply talking to other people -- that is, participating in social interaction, which engages many mental and perceptual skills -- suffices. Statement2: Evidence to this effect comes from a study showing that the more social contact people report, the better their mental skills.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many medical conditions and treatments that adversely affect a person's mental sharpness also tend to increase that person's social isolation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The tasks evaluating mental sharpness for which data were compiled by the study were more akin to mathematics problems than to conversation.

premise: Statement1: A recent epidemiological study found that businesspeople who travel internationally on business are much more likely to suffer from chronic insomnia than are businesspeople who do not travel on business. International travelers experience the stresses of dramatic changes in climate, frequent disruption of daily routines, and immersion in cultures other than their own, stresses not commonly felt by those who do not travel. Statement2: Thus, it is likely that these stresses cause the insomnia.
hypothesis: Premise1: Businesspeople who already suffer from chronic insomnia are no more likely than businesspeople who do not to accept assignments from their employers that require international travel.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Experiencing dramatic changes in climate and disruption of daily routines through international travel can be beneficial to some people who suffer from chronic insomnia.

premise: Statement1: The everyday behavior of whales is particularly difficult to study because introducing novel stimuli, such as divers or submarines, into the whales'environment causes whales to behave in unusual ways. Statement2: Some biologists plan to train sea lions to carry video cameras on their backs and, on command, to swim along with whales. Statement3: They argue that since whales are accustomed to there being sea lions nearby, using the sea lions to film the whales would allow biologists to study the everyday behavior of the whales.
hypothesis: Premise1: The behavior of the sea lions under human command will be within the range of sea lion behavior to which the whales are accustomed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Sea lions carrying video cameras will be able to film whales at a much closer range than divers can.

premise: Statement1: Science fiction creates an appetite for interstellar space exploration among certain people. Unfortunately, this appetite can not be satisfied with any technology humanity will soon possess. Statement2: Since gaps between expectations and reality spur discontent, no doubt one effect of science fiction has been to create an unproductive dissatisfaction with the way the world actually is.
hypothesis: Premise1: The fact that the appetite for interstellar space exploration cannot be satisfied with any technology humanity will soon possess has created a gap between reality and some people's expectations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the appetites science fiction has created in people could all be satisfied with technologies that humanity will soon possess, then science fiction could not create an unproductive dissatisfaction with the way the world is.

premise: Statement1: Public health will improve more quickly in the wake of new medical discoveries if medical researchers abandon their practice of waiting until their findings are published in peer-reviewed journals before informing the press of important research results. Statement2: This is because the public release of new medical information allows people to use that information in order to improve their health, but the peer-review process is unavoidably very slow.
hypothesis: Premise1: People would use new medical information even if it were not first published in peer-reviewed journals.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: New medical information that is first published in peer-reviewed journals does not usually receive public attention.

premise: Statement1: Efforts to get the public to exercise regularly, which have emphasized the positive health effects of exercise rather than the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle, have met with little success. Statement2: In contrast, efforts to curb cigarette smoking, which have emphasized the dangers of smoking rather than the positive health effects of quitting, have been highly successful. Statement3: Thus, efforts to get the public to exercise regularly would be more successful if they emphasized the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle rather than the positive health effects of exercise.
hypothesis: Premise1: Efforts to curb cigarette smoking would not be more successful if they emphasized the positive health effects of quitting rather than the dangers of smoking.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The majority of people who successfully quit smoking cite health concerns as the primary motivation for their success.

premise: Statement1: The number of serious traffic accidents ( accidents resulting in hospitalization or death ) that occurred on park road from 1986 to 1990 was 35 percent lower than the number of serious accidents from 1981 to 1985. sent2: the speed limit on park road was lowered in 1986. sent3: hence, the reduction of the speed limit led to the decrease in serious accidents.
hypothesis: Premise1: The annual number of vehicles using park road decreased significantly and steadily from 1981 to 1990.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Until 1986 accidents were classified as "serious" only if they resulted in an extended hospital stay.

premise: Statement1: Researcher : we have found that some cases of high blood pressure can be treated effectively with medicine. Statement2: Since it is generally accepted that any illness caused by stress is treatable only by the reduction of stress, some cases of high blood pressure must not be caused by stress.
hypothesis: Premise1: Medicine used to treat high blood pressure does not itself reduce stress.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The reduction of stress in a person's life can at times lower that person's blood pressure.

premise: Statement1: Tenants who do not have to pay their own electricity bills do not have a financial incentive to conserve electricity. Statement2: Thus, if more landlords install individual electricity meters on tenant dwellings so that tenants can be billed for their own use, energy will be conserved as a result.
hypothesis: Premise1: Landlords who pay for their tenants' electricity have a strong incentive to make sure that the appliances they provide for their tenants are energy efficient.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many initiatives have been implemented to educate people about how much money they can save through energy conservation.

premise: Statement1: Radio stations with radio data system ( rds ) technology broadcast special program information that only radios with an rds feature can receive. Statement2: Between 1994 and 1996, the number of rds radio stations in verdland increased from 250 to 600. however, since the number of rds-equipped radios in verdland was about the same in 1996 as in 1994, the number of verdlanders receiving the special program information probably did not increase significantly.
hypothesis: Premise1: Few if any of the rds radio stations that began broadcasting in verdland after 1994 broadcast to people with rds-equipped radios living in areas not previously reached by rds stations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In 1996 most verdlanders who lived within the listening area of an rds station already had a radio equipped to receive rds.

premise: Statement1: For years, university administrators, corporations, and government agencies have been predicting an imminent and catastrophic shortage of scientists and engineers. But since there is little noticeable upward pressure on the salaries of scientists and engineers, and unemployment is as high in these fields as any other, these doomsayers are turning out to be wrong.
hypothesis: Premise1: The number of students in university programs in science and engineering has increased significantly in the last five years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The proportion of all research in science and engineering being carried out by corporations is larger than it was five years ago.

premise: Statement1: Literary critic : often the heirs of a successful writer decide to publish the manuscripts and the letters the dead writer left behind, regardless of the merit of the work. However, many writers have manuscripts that they judge to be unworthy of publication and with which they would not like to be publicly associated even after they die. Statement2: Hence a successful writer who decides not to publish a recently completed manuscript should destroy it immediately.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most successful writers' judgments of their recently completed work is unnecessarily harsh and is often later revised.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some writers whose work becomes both popular and respected after they die received no literary recognition during their lifetimes.

premise: Statement1: Several recent studies establish that most people would want to be informed if they had any serious medical condition. Statement2: In each study, over 80 percent of the people surveyed indicated that they would want to be told.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some of the researchers conducting the studies had no background in medicine.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In another recent study, most of the people surveyed indicated that they would not want to be told if they had a serious medical condition.

premise: Statement1: Tiger beetles are such fast runners that they can capture virtually any nonflying insect. However, when running toward an insect, the beetles intermittently stop, and then, a moment later, resume their attack. Statement2: Perhaps they can not maintain their pace and must pause for a moment's rest ; but an alternative hypothesis is that while running tiger beetles are unable to process the resulting rapidly changing visual information, and so quickly go blind and stop.
hypothesis: Premise1: The beetles maintain a fixed time interval between pauses, although when an insect that had been stationary begins to flee, the beetle increases its speed after its next pause.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If, when a beetle pauses, it has not gained on the insect it is pursuing, the beetle generally ends its pursuit.

premise: Statement1: Appliance dealer : appliance manufacturers commonly modify existing models without giving the modified versions new model names. Some people have complained that this practice makes it impossible for consumers to be certain that the appliance they are about to purchase is identical to the one they may have seen at a neighbor's or read about in a consumer magazine. Statement2: Yet manufacturers'modifications to existing models are invariably improvements that benefit the buyer. Statement3: Therefore, consumers have little reason to object to this practice.
hypothesis: Premise1: Improved versions of appliances typically become available before vendors have stopped selling the older versions of the appliance with the same model name.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Appliances usually carry a model number that provides substantially more detailed information about the product than does the model name.

premise: Statement1: Human beings can exhibit complex, goal-oriented behavior without conscious awareness of what they are doing. Statement2: Thus, merely establishing that nonhuman animals are intelligent will not establish that they have consciousness.
hypothesis: Premise1: Complex, goal-oriented behavior requires intelligence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some intelligent human behavior is neither complex nor goal-oriented.

premise: Statement1: Newspaper : increases in produce prices apparently have led to an increase in the planting of personal gardens. Statement2: The two largest retail seed companies each reported sales increases of around 19 percent in the last year alone, a year in which the price of produce spiked.
hypothesis: Premise1: A large retail seed company went out of business early last year.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Personal gardens are usually popular in economic downturns.

premise: Statement1: Advertisement : my five-year-old identical twins wear matching clothing. Statement2: I washed two of their soiled matching shirts, one in my regular laundry detergent, and one in new wave laundry detergent. Statement3: The shirt washed in wave came out cleaner than the other shirt, so wave cleans better than my regular laundry detergent.
hypothesis: Premise1: The shirt laundered in the regular laundry detergent was not significantly less clean than the other shirt before being laundered.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Clothes washed in wave laundry detergent will come out cleaner than clothes washed in any other brand of laundry detergent

premise: Statement1: There were several early attempts to forge a reconciliation between shintoism and buddhism based on mutual respect among their adherents. Statement2: The evidence for this includes extant sculptures depicting shinto gods wearing buddhist vestments.
hypothesis: Premise1: The depiction of shinto gods wearing buddhist vestments was not intended to represent the triumph of shintoism over buddhism.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No sculptures that have not survived depicted shinto gods wearing buddhist vestments.

premise: Statement1: Art critic : abstract paintings are nonrepresentational, and so the only measure of their worth is their interplay of color, texture, and form. But for a painting to spur the viewer to political action, instances of social injustice must be not only represented, but also clearly comprehensible as such. Statement2: Therefore, abstract painting can never be a politically significant art form.
hypothesis: Premise1: Paintings that fail to move a viewer to political action cannot be politically significant.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Abstract painting cannot stimulate people to act.

premise: Statement1: In a study conducted in pennsylvania, servers in various restaurants wrote" thank you" on randomly selected bills before presenting the bills to their customers. Statement2: Tips on these bills were an average of three percentage points higher than tips on bills without the message. Statement3: Therefore, if servers in pennsylvania regularly wrote" thank you" on restaurant bills, their average income from tips would be significantly higher than it otherwise would have been.
hypothesis: Premise1: Regularly seeing "thank you" written on their bills would not lead restaurant patrons to revert to their earlier tipping habits.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The "thank you" messages would have the same impact on regular patrons of a restaurant as they would on occasional patrons of the same restaurant.

premise: Statement1: Manufacturers of writing paper need to add mineral" filler" to paper pulp if the paper made from the pulp is to look white. To make writing paper that looks white from recycled paper requires more filler than is required to make such paper from other sources. Statement2: Without such filler, paper products look grayish. Statement3: Therefore, barring the more efficient use of fillers in paper manufacturing or the development of paper-whitening technologies that do not require mineral fillers, if writing paper made from recycled paper comes to replace other types of writing paper, paper manufacturers will have to use more filler than they now use.
hypothesis: Premise1: Grayish writing paper will not be a universally acceptable alternative to white writing paper.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Certain kinds of paper cannot be manufactured from recycled paper.

premise: Statement1: Acme corporation offers unskilled workers excellent opportunities for advancement. Statement2: As evidence, consider the fact that the president of the company, ms. garon, worked as an assembly line worker, an entry-level position requiring no special skills, when she first started at acme.
hypothesis: Premise1: Acme regularly hires top graduates of business schools and employs them briefly in each of a succession of entry-level positions before promoting them to management.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Acme's vice president of operations also worked as an assembly line worker when he first started at acme.

premise: Statement1: Computer manufacturers have sought to make computer chips ever smaller, since decreasing the size of a computer's central processing unit ( cpu ) chip -- without making that cpu chip any less sophisticated -- will proportionally increase the speed of the cpu chip and the computer containing it. Statement2: But since cpu chips can not be made significantly smaller without decreasing their sophistication, computers can not currently be made significantly faster.
hypothesis: Premise1: Computers cannot currently be made faster unless their cpu chips are made smaller.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If both the size and the sophistication of a cpu chip are decreased, the speed of that chip will decrease.

premise: Statement1: Commentator : many people argue that the release of chlorofluorocarbons into the atmosphere is harming humans by damaging the ozone layer, thus allowing increased amounts of ultraviolet radiation to reach earth. But 300,000 years ago a supernova greatly damaged the ozone layer, with no significant effect on our earliest ancestors. Statement2: Because the supernova's disruption was much greater than the estimated effect of chlorofluorocarbons today, there is no reason to think that these chemicals in the atmosphere harm humans in this way.
hypothesis: Premise1: Our earliest ancestors possessed genetic characteristics making them more resistant than we are to the harmful effects of ultraviolet radiation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Extraterrestrial influences on the ozone layer tend to occur less often than terrestrial ones.

premise: Statement1: In his book, published in 1892, grey used the same metaphor that jordan used in her book, which was published in 1885. the metaphor is so unusual that there is little chance that two different people independently created it. Statement2: Therefore, it is highly likely that grey read jordan's book.
hypothesis: Premise1: A text that was probably known to both jordan and grey was published in 1860 and also contained the same unusual metaphor.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: According to most scholars, grey was generally a more inventive writer than jordan and developed many original metaphors.

premise: Statement1: Native speakers perceive sentences of their own language as sequences of separate words. This is shown by the fact that travelers who do not know a local language hear an unintelligible, uninterrupted stream of sound, not sentences with distinct words. Statement2: But this perception is an illusion.
hypothesis: Premise1: Native speakers' perceptions of their own language are not more accurate than are the perceptions of persons who do not know that language.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: It is impossible to understand sentences if they are in fact uninterrupted streams of sound.

premise: Statement1: Even if many more people in the world excluded meat from their diet, world hunger would not thereby be significantly reduced.
hypothesis: Premise1: The amount of land needed to produce enough meat to feed one person for a week can grow enough grain to feed more than ten people for a week.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Both herds and crops are susceptible to devastating viral and other diseases.

premise: Statement1: Half a dozen q40 airliners have crashed in the past two years. Statement2: The manufacturer has countered claims that the q40's design is faulty by pointing out that, in each of the accidents, investigations indicated pilot error as the cause.
hypothesis: Premise1: In four of the crashes, the pilot's error involved disregarding instructions or information supplied by air-traffic control
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: One of the crashes occurred in very poor visibility when the pilot was attempting to land.

premise: Statement1: Journalist : in physics journals, the number of articles reporting the results of experiments involving particle accelerators was lower last year than it had been in previous years. Statement2: Several of the particle accelerators at major research institutions were out of service the year before last for repairs, so it is likely that the low number of articles was due to the decline in availability of particle accelerators.
hypothesis: Premise1: Recent changes in the editorial policies of several physics journals have decreased the likelihood that articles concerning particle accelerator research will be accepted for publication.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Every article based on experiments with particle accelerators that was submitted for publication last year actually was published.

premise: Statement1: A study of kindergarten students has shown that children from households that own many books, an average of 54 books, show average or high reading achievement levels, while the households of children with lower reading achievement levels tend to own fewer books, 5 books on average.
hypothesis: Premise1: When children have books of their own, they develop more advanced reading skills because adults are more likely to read to them regularly.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Children whose parents buy them books feel happier about their lives.

premise: Statement1: Over 40,000 lead seals from the early byzantine empire remain today. Statement2: Apart from the rare cases where the seal authenticated a document of special importance, most seals had served their purpose when the document was opened. Statement3: Lead was not expensive, but it was not free : most lead seals would have been recast once they had served their purpose. Statement4: Thus the number of early byzantine documents sealed in such a fashion must have been many times the number of remaining lead seals.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most of the lead seals produced during the early byzantine empire were affixed to documents that were then opened during that period.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: During the time of the early byzantine empire there were at most 40, 000 documents of enough importance to prevent the removing and recycling of the seal.

premise: Statement1: Tv meteorologist : our station's weather forecasts are more useful and reliable than those of the most popular news station in the area. Statement2: After all, the most important question for viewers in this area is whether it will rain, and on most of the occasions when we have forecast rain for the next day, we have been right. The same can not be said for either of our competitors.

premise: Statement1: Environmentalist : it takes less energy to make molten glass from recycled glass than from raw materials. Statement3: Therefore, by using recycled glass instead of glass made from raw materials, bottlers can lower their costs and benefit the environment at the same time.

premise: Statement1: To increase the productivity of its attorneys, x law firm has decided to implement a part-time program, which would allow attorneys to have more flexibility in the number of days per week they work. premise: Statement1: Department store manager : sent2: there is absolutely no reason to offer our customers free gift wrapping again this holiday season. premise: Statement1: Even though trading in ivory has been outlawed by international agreement, some piano makers still use ivory, often obtained illegally, to cover piano keys. premise: Statement1: Rhizobium bacteria living in the roots of bean plants or other legumes produce fixed nitrogen, which is one of the essential plant nutrients and which for nonlegume crops, such as wheat, normally must be supplied by applications of nitrogen-based fertilizer. premise: Statement1: During the period in which there are no competitive races, two runners -- runners a and runners b -- take part in an experiment measuring their vo2 max, the volume of oxygen an athlete can use. premise: Statement1: In a town containing a tourist attraction, hotel and restaurant revenues each increased more rapidly over the past year than did revenue from the sale of passes to the attraction, which are valid for a full year. premise: Statement1: The retail price of decaffeinated coffee is considerably higher than that of regular coffee. premise: Statement1: In modern deep-diving marine mammals, such as whales, the outer shell of the bones is porous. premise: Statement1: Guidebook writer : i have visited hotels throughout the country and have noticed that in those built before 1930 the quality of the original carpentry work is generally superior to that in hotels built afterward. premise: Statement1: On average, corporations that encourage frequent social events in the workplace show higher profits than those that rarely do. Statement2: This suggests that the ez corporation could boost its profits by having more staff parties during business hours.

premise: Statement1: A study followed a group of teenagers who had never smoked and tracked whether they took up smoking and how their mental health changed. premise: Statement1: Waste management companies, which collect waste for disposal in landfills and incineration plants, report that disposable plastics make up an ever-increasing percentage of the waste they handle. premise: Statement1: Political scientist : the dissemination of political theories is in principle able to cause change in existing social structures. premise: Statement1: In defending the hyperion school of journalism from charges that its program is of little or no value to its students, the dean of the school pointed to its recent success in placing students : 65 percent of its graduates went on to internships or jobs in print or broadcast journalism.

premise: Statement1: The current pattern of human consumption of resources, in which we rely on nonrenewable resources, for example metal ore, must eventually change. premise: Statement1: Recently discovered prehistoric rock paintings on small islands off the northern coast of norway have archaeologists puzzled. This theory can not be right, because the painters must have needed to eat the sea animals populating the waters north of norway if they were to make the long journey to and from the islands, and there are no paintings that unambiguously depict such creatures.

premise: Statement1: Regular weightlifting is necessary for good health. Statement2: Weightlifting with heavy resistance, especially with compound movements, helps break down and rebuild stronger muscle fibers, resulting in strength and size gains.

premise: Statement1: The level of triglycerides in the blood rises when triglycerides are inadequately metabolized. premise: Statement1: A professor of business placed a case-study assignment for her class on her university's computer network. premise: Statement1: The city's transportation department has noticed that the city's extensive public bus system generally runs behind schedule on days when traffic is heavier in the city. premise: Statement1: Patel : it is often thought that direct experience, unlike language and culture, can always serve as common ground for communication among human beings. premise: Statement1: Politician : the cohesion of a society depends on its members'accepting a set of basic principles. When these principles are routinely called into question, the society begins to erode. premise: Statement1: In 1992 outlaw fishing boats began illegally harvesting lobsters from the territorial waters of the country of belukia. premise: Statement1: A favored theory to explain the extinction of dinosaurs, together with many other species, has been the globally catastrophic collision of a large asteroid with the earth. premise: Statement1: In the past, when there was no highway speed limit, the highway accident rate increased yearly, peaking a decade ago. premise: Statement1: Voters commonly condemn politicians for being insincere, but politicians often must disguise their true feelings when they make public statements. premise: Statement1: Professor robinson : a large meteorite impact crater in a certain region was thought to be the clue to explaining the mass extinction of plant and animal species that occurred at the end of the mesozoic era. premise: Statement1: An independent computer service company tallied the service requests it receives for individual brands of personal computers. premise: Statement1: The continents of south america and africa were once joined together as one landmass. Statement2: Evidence for this claim can be found in the fact that fossils of mesosaurs, extinct reptiles, have been unearthed in both western africa and eastern south america.

premise: Statement1: Climate change is caused by an increase of carbon dioxide in the earth's atmosphere. Overall, deforestation has led to the loss of half the world's trees. premise: Statement1: Over the last 25 years, the average price paid for a new car has steadily increased in relation to average individual income. premise: Statement1: Manager : one reason productivity in our office is not as high as it could be is that office workers spend too much time taking unauthorized breaks. premise: Statement1: The existence of polluting industries near schools does not lead to a decrease in the air quality at those schools. premise: Statement1: Lines can be parallel in a euclidean system of geometry. premise: Statement1: Alan : government subsidies have been proposed in cariana to encourage farmers in rochelle, the country's principal agricultural region, to implement certain new farming techniques. premise: Statement1: Bram stoker's 1897 novel dracula portrayed vampires -- the" undead" who roam at night to suck the blood of living people -- as able to turn into bats. As a result of the pervasive influence of this novel, many people now assume that a vampire's being able to turn into a bat is an essential part of vampire myths. premise: Statement1: In a study, one group of volunteers was fed a high-protein, low-carbohydrate diet ; another group was fed a low-protein, high-carbohydrate diet. Both diets contained the same number of calories, and each volunteer's diet prior to the experiment had contained moderate levels of proteins and carbohydrates. Statement2: After ten days, those on the low-carbohydrate diet had lost more weight than those on the high-carbohydrate diet. Statement3: Thus, the most effective way to lose body fat is to eat much protein and shun carbohydrates.
hypothesis: Premise1: A low-protein, high-carbohydrate diet causes the human body to retain water, the added weight of which largely compensates for the weight of any body fat lost, whereas a high-protein, low-carbohydrate diet does not.

premise: Statement1: It is obvious that one ought to have a will stating how one wishes one's estate to be distributed. premise: Statement1: Editorial : our political discussions tend to focus largely on the flaws of our nation's leaders, but we need to remind ourselves that these leaders were chosen democratically. Statement3: Thus, to focus our attention on the flaws of our leaders is to indulge in a pointless distraction.
hypothesis: Premise1: Examining an individual leader's personal flaws does not reveal anything about how the nation's institutions and procedures influence the selection of leaders.

premise: Statement1: Archaeologist : neanderthals, a human-like species living 60,000 years ago, probably preserved meat by smoking it. Statement2: Burnt lichen and grass have been found in many neanderthal fireplaces. Statement3: A fire of lichen and grass produces a lot of smoke but does not produce nearly as much heat or light as a wood fire.

premise: Statement1: Campaigning for election to provincial or state office frequently requires that a candidate spend much time and energy catering to the interests of national party officials who can help the candidate to win office. premise: Statement1: Professor : economists argue that buying lottery tickets is an unwise use of resources, because the average payoff for the tickets sold in a lottery is much lower than the cost of a ticket. premise: Statement1: Stage performances are judged to be realistic to the degree that actors reproduce on stage the behaviors generally associated by audiences with the emotional states of the characters portrayed. premise: Statement1: Only engineering is capable of analyzing the nature of a machine in terms of the successful working of the whole ; physics and chemistry determine the material conditions necessary for this success, but can not express the notion of purpose. premise: Statement1: In any field, experience is required for a proficient person to become an expert. Although computers have the ability to store millions of bits of information, the knowledge of human experts, who benefit from the experience of thousands of situations, is not stored within their brains in the form of rules and facts. premise: Statement1: Wind farms, which generate electricity using arrays of thousands of wind-powered turbines, require vast expanses of open land. premise: Statement1: Eating turmeric, a spice commonly found in curries, probably helps prevent alzheimer's disease. premise: Statement1: The giant chicxulub crater in mexico provides indisputable evidence that a huge asteroid, about six miles across, struck earth around the time many of the last dinosaur species were becoming extinct. premise: Statement1: Although many seventeenth-century broadsides, popular ballads printed on a single sheet of paper and widely sold by street peddlers, were moralizing in nature, this is not evidence that most seventeenth-century people were serious about moral values. premise: Statement1: A newly discovered painting seems to be the work of one of two seventeenth-century artists, either the northern german johannes drechen or the frenchman louis birelle, who sometimes painted in the same style as drechen. premise: Statement1: In 1563, in florence's palazzo vecchio, giorgio vasari built in front of an existing wall a new wall on which he painted a mural. Statement3: Historians believe that leonardo da vinci had painted, but left unfinished, a mural on the original wall ; some historians had also believed that by 1563 the mural had been destroyed. hypothesis: Premise1: Vasari probably would not have built the palazzo vecchio wall with a gap behind it except to preserve something behind the new wall.

premise: Statement1: Columnist : even if the primary purpose of university education is to make students employable, such education should emphasize the liberal arts rather than the more narrow kind of technical training that prepares one for a particular sort of job. premise: Statement1: In 1974 the speed limit on highways in the united states was reduced to 55 miles per hour in order to save fuel. Over the next 10 years, the fatality rate declined by another 25 percent. Statement3: It follows that the 1974 reduction in the speed limit saved many lives.

premise: Statement1: The more frequently employees take time to exercise during working hours each week, the fewer sick days they take. premise: Statement1: At one sitting, a typical doughnut eater consumes 4 doughnuts containing a total of 680 calories and 40 grams of fat. premise: Statement1: Philosopher : both the consequences and the motives of human actions have bearing on the moral worth of those actions. Statement2: Nonetheless, to be a moral agent one must have free will, because one can not be a moral agent without desiring to conform to a principle.

premise: Statement1: Bacteria from food can survive for several days on the surface of plastic cutting boards, but bacteria can penetrate wooden cutting boards almost immediately, leaving the surface free of contamination. premise: Statement1: Last year a chain of fast-food restaurants, whose menu had always centered on hamburger, added its first vegetarian sandwich, much lower in fat than the chain 's other offerings. premise: Statement1: A museum has been offered an undocumented statue, supposedly greek and from the sixth century b.c. However, an ancient surface usually has uneven weathering, whereas the surface of this statue has the uniform quality characteristically produced by a chemical bath used by forgers to imitate a weathered surface. premise: Statement1: A naturally occurring deposit of radioactive material similar in composition to the dangerous waste produced by applications of nuclear technology has been found deep within the earth's crust. Statement3: Furthermore, on average, a physician now spends only 15 minutes with a patient on the patient's first visit.
hypothesis: Premise1: Five years ago, the average first patient-physician visit lasted 10 minutes.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The average length of all patient-physician visits is 20 minutes.

premise: Statement1: Medical research findings are customarily not made public prior to their publication in a medical journal that has had them reviewed by a panel of experts in a process called peer review. Statement2: It is claimed that this practice delays public access to potentially beneficial information that, in extreme instances, could save lives. Statement3: Yet prepublication peer review is the only way to prevent erroneous and therefore potentially harmful information from reaching a public that is ill equipped to evaluate medical claims on its own. Statement4: Therefore, waiting until a medical journal has published the research findings that have passed peer review is the price that must be paid to protect the public from making decisions based on possibly substandard research.
hypothesis: Premise1: Unless medical research findings are brought to peer review by a medical journal, peer review will not occur
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Anyone who does not serve on a medical review panel does not have the necessary knowledge and expertise to evaluate medical research findings

premise: Statement1: If a civilization as technologically advanced as human civilization existed on another planet and that planet were within 50 light years of earth, that civilization would have found evidence of intelligent life on earth and could have easily contacted us. Statement2: Scientists can thus rule out the possibility of finding a civilization as technologically advanced as our own within 50 light years of earth.
hypothesis: Premise1: A technologically advanced civilization on another planet would want to communicate with intelligent life that it detected on earth.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There is no reason to doubt the possibility that there are technologically advanced civilizations on planets more than 50 light years from earth.

premise: Statement1: After a hepadnavirus inserts itself into a chromosome of an animal, fragments of the virus are passed on to all of that animal's descendants. Statement2: A hepadnavirus fragment is present in a chromosome of the zebra finch and in precisely the same location in a corresponding chromosome of the dark-eyed junco. Statement3: The fact that these two bird species diverged from each other about 25 million years ago therefore means that the hepadnavirus is at least 25 million years old.
hypothesis: Premise1: When a virus inserts itself into an animal's chromosome, the insertion occurs at a random spot.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Viruses can affect the evolution of an organism and can thereby influence the likelihood of their diverging into two species.

premise: Statement1: Doctor : while a few alternative medicines have dangerous side effects, some, such as many herbs, have been proven safe to consume. Statement2: Thus, though there is little firm evidence of medicinal effect, advocates of these herbs as remedies for serious illnesses should always be allowed to prescribe them, since their patients will not be harmed, and might be helped, by the use of these products.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many practitioners and patients neglect more effective conventional medicines in favor of herbal remedies.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some patients may have allergic reactions to certain medicines that have been tolerated by other patients.

premise: Statement1: In an experiment, volunteers walked individually through a dark, abandoned theater. Half of the volunteers had been told that the theater was haunted and the other half that it was under renovation. Statement2: The first half reported significantly more unusual experiences than the second did. Statement3: The researchers concluded that reports of encounters with ghosts and other supernatural entities generally result from prior expectations of such experiences.
hypothesis: Premise1: All of the volunteers in the first half believed that the researchers' statement that the theater was haunted was a lie.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Each unusual experience reported by the volunteers had a cause that did not involve the supernatural.

premise: Statement1: Recently, highly skilled workers in eastern europe have left jobs in record numbers to emigrate to the west. Statement2: It is therefore likely that skilled workers who remain in eastern europe are in high demand in their home countries.
hypothesis: Premise1: Major changes in eastern european economic structures have led to the elimination of many positions previously held by the highly skilled emigrants.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Eastern european factories prefer to hire workers from their home countries rather than to import workers from abroad.

premise: Statement1: The prevailing view among historians is that medieval european peasants were deeply dedicated to religion. Statement2: But the record keepers in medieval europe were members of the clergy. It should not surprise us that chroniclers who were professionally occupied with religion would exaggerate people's religious devotion. Statement3: Thus, there is reason to doubt the prevailing view of medieval peasants'piety.
hypothesis: Premise1: Written records produced by clergy in medieval europe very rarely portray merchants or nobles as being dedicated to religion.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many members of the clergy in medieval europe spent more time among peasants than among people of any other social class.

premise: Statement1: Drivers in the country of holston want highway tolls eliminated. Statement2: The cost of maintaining the highways is paid entirely out of revenue from the highway tolls. Holston must maintain its highways. Statement3: Thus, it follows that if the tolls are eliminated, then the entire cost of maintaining the highways will have to be paid for by an increase in general taxes.
hypothesis: Premise1: The amount of money saved by eliminating the costs associated with toll collection would not be available to pay part of the total cost of maintaining the highways.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the highway tolls are eliminated and the general taxes are increased to pay for maintaining the highways, there will be less emphasis on preventive maintenance.

premise: Statement1: High blood cholesterol levels are bad for the heart. Like meat, eggs, and poultry, shellfish contains cholesterol. Statement2: But shellfish is not necessarily bad for the heart ; it is very low in saturated fat, which affects blood cholesterol levels much more than dietary cholesterol does.
hypothesis: Premise1: Foods low in saturated fat promote low blood cholesterol.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Small quantities of foods high in saturated fat are not bad for the heart

premise: Statement1: The government has proposed a plan requiring young people to perform services to correct various current social ills, especially those in education and housing. Statement2: Government service, however, should be compelled only in response to a direct threat to the nation's existence. Statement3: For that reason, the proposed program should not be implemented.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some of the social ills that currently afflict the nation do not pose a direct threat to the nation's existence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Crises in education and housing constitute a threat to the nation's existence.

premise: Statement1: During the recent economic downturn, banks contributed to the decline by loaning less money. Statement2: Prior to the downturn, regulatory standards for loanmaking by banks were tightened. Statement3: Clearly, therefore, banks will lend more money if those standards are relaxed.
hypothesis: Premise1: The downturn did not cause a significant decrease in the total amount of money on deposit with banks which is the source of funds for banks to lend
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No relaxation of standards for loanmaking by banks would compensate for the effects of the downturn

premise: Statement1: Fire ants from brazil now infest the southern united states. Statement2: Unlike queen fire ants in brazil, two queens in the united states share a nest. Ants from these nests are more aggressive than those from single-queen nests. Statement3: By destroying virtually all insects in the nest area, these aggressive ants gain sole access to food sources, and the ant population skyrockets. Statement4: Since certain predator insects in brazil limit the fire-ant population there, importing such predator insects into the united states would be of overall benefit to the environment by stopping the increase of the fire-ant population in the united states.
hypothesis: Premise1: The predator insects would stop the increase of the ant population before the ants spread to states that are farther north.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The imported insects would not prove more damaging to the environment in the united states than are the fire ants themselves.

premise: Statement1: When volcanic lava solidifies, it becomes uniformly magnetized in the direction in which the earth's magnetic field points. There are significant differences in the direction of magnetization among solidified lava flows from different volcanoes that erupted at different times over the past several million years. Statement2: Therefore, it must be that the direction of the earth's magnetic field has changed over time. Statement3: Since lava flows differing by thousands of years in age often have very similar directions of magnetization, the change in the direction of the earth's magnetic field must take place very gradually over hundreds of thousands of years.
hypothesis: Premise1: Not all solidified lava has changed the direction of its magnetization unpredictably
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: As lava flows down the side of a volcano, it picks up magnetized rocks

premise: Statement1: Omega corporation has hired two management consultant firms, managefix! And efficiencyrun, to help cut unnecessary costs by streamlining its corporate structure. Statement2: By following managefix! 's plan, omega corp. will save more money than it would by following efficiencyrun's plan. Statement3: Therefore, by following managefix! 'sent4: s plan, omega corp. will be doing the most that can be done to cut unnecessary costs due to inefficient corporate structure.
hypothesis: Premise1: Unnecessary costs cannot be reduced more by implementing both plans together than by implementing managefix! 's alone.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Other than in its corporate structure, omega corp. does not waste money in other areas.

premise: Statement1: Industrial scientists have hypothesized that much of the nitrous oxide that contributes to air pollution originates from the burning of organic matter in forest fires. Statement2: The results of one extensive study in which smoke samples taken from forest fires were bottled and then analyzed in a research laboratory supports this hypothesis, since the analysis showed that all of the samples contained high concentrations of nitrous oxide.
hypothesis: Premise1: When in a closed container, the gases in smoke produced by forest fires quickly react with each other thereby producing high concentrations of nitrous oxide.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The production of synthetic products such as nylon is responsible for up to 10 percent of the nitrous oxide present in the atmosphere.

premise: Statement1: Taken together, some 2,000 stocks recommended on a popular television show over the course of the past 12 years by the show's guests, most of whom are successful consultants for multibillion-dollar stock portfolios, performed less successfully than the market as a whole for this 12-year period. Statement2: So clearly, no one should ever follow any recommendations by these so-called experts.
hypothesis: Premise1: Performance of the stocks recommended on the television show was measured independently by a number of analysts, and the results of all the measurements concurred.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Taken together, the stocks recommended on the television show performed better than the market as a whole for the past year.

premise: Statement1: Economist : some sociologists argue that because capitalism intrinsically involves competition, it weakens the ties between the people of a community. Statement2: Although this may formerly have been true, modern capitalism requires that there be large corporations. Statement3: Thus, modern capitalism promotes, rather than weakens, communal ties.
hypothesis: Premise1: Having large corporations in a modern capitalist system promotes the strength of communal ties.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Modern capitalism is designed primarily to distribute goods and services, not to create communal ties between people.

premise: Statement1: With a record number of new companies starting up in derderia, and with previously established companies adding many new jobs, a record number of new jobs were created last year in the derderian economy. This year, previously established companies will not be adding as many new jobs overall as such companies added last year. Statement2: Therefore, unless a record number of companies start up this year, derderia will not break its record for new jobs created.
hypothesis: Premise1: This year, the new companies starting up will not provide substantially more jobs per company than did new companies last year.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In a given year, new companies starting up create more new jobs on average than do previously established companies.

premise: Statement1: To prevent a newly built dam on the chiff river from blocking the route of fish migrating to breeding grounds upstream, the dam includes a fish pass, a mechanism designed to allow fish through the dam. Statement2: Before the construction of the dam and fish pass, several thousand fish a day swam upriver during spawning season. But in the first season after the project's completion, only 300 per day made the journey. Statement3: Clearly, the fish pass is defective.
hypothesis: Premise1: The construction of the dam stirred up potentially toxic river sediments that were carried downstream.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: During spawning season, the dam releases sufficient water for migratory fish below the dam to swim upstream.

premise: Statement1: It has been claimed that television networks should provide equal time for the presentation of opposing views whenever a television program concerns scientific issues -- such as those raised by the claims of environmentalists -- about which people disagree. Statement2: However, although an obligation to provide equal time does arise in the case of any program concerning social issues, it does so because social issues almost always have important political implications and seldom can definitely be settled on the basis of available evidence. Statement3: If a program concerns scientific issues, that program gives rise to no such equal time obligation.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many scientific issues have important political implications and cannot be definitely settled on the basis of the available evidence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No scientific issues raised by the claims of environmentalists have important political implications.

premise: Statement1: Tissue biopsies taken on patients who have undergone throat surgery show that those who snored frequently were significantly more likely to have serious abnormalities in their throat muscles than those who snored rarely or not at all. Statement2: This shows that snoring can damage the throat of the snorer.
hypothesis: Premise1: The abnormalities in the throat muscles discovered in the study do not cause snoring.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People who have undergone throat surgery are no more likely to snore than people who have not undergone throat surgery.

premise: Statement1: Copyright laws protect the rights of writers to profits earned from their writings, whereas patent laws protect inventors'rights to profits earned from their inventions. In jawade, when computer-software writers demanded that their rights to profit be protected, the courts determined that information written for a machine does not fit into either the copyright or the patent category. Statement2: Clearly, therefore, the profit rights of computer-software writers remain unprotected in jawade.
hypothesis: Premise1: No laws exist, other than copyright laws and patent laws, that would protect the profit rights of computer-software writers in jawade.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most of the computer software used in jawade is imported from other countries.

premise: Statement1: A successful coffee stand that serves the workers in a large office building plans to increase its profits by adding oatmeal-a hot cereal popular among the workers-to its menu. Statement2: The manager says that oatmeal is" the ultimate convenience food" because it can be served by the cup, as coffee is ; further, offering oatmeal will enable the stand to get into the breakfast business without making a large investment, because it does not require elaborate cooking equipment.
hypothesis: Premise1: The ingredients needed to make oatmeal are inexpensive and do not spoil easily the way many fresh breakfast foods typically sold at coffee stands do.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Oatmeal manufacturers have recently reduced their oatmeal offerings, which had included oatmeal in microwaveable cups and convenient cereal bars made with rolled oats.

premise: Statement1: Rabbits were introduced to numa island in the nineteenth century. Statement2: Overgrazing by the enormous population of rabbits now menaces the island's agriculture. The government proposes to reduce the population by using a virus that has caused devastating epidemics in rabbit populations elsewhere. There is, however, a chance that the virus will infect the bilby, an endangered native marsupial. Statement3: The government's plan, therefore, may serve the interests of agriculture but will clearly increase the threat to native wildlife.
hypothesis: Premise1: Overgrazing by rabbits endangers many of the plants on which bilbies feed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The virus that the government proposes to use has been successfully used elsewhere to control populations of rabbits.

premise: Statement1: The stated goal of the government's funding program for the arts is to encourage the creation of works of artistic excellence. Statement2: Senator beton claims, however, that a government-funded artwork can never reflect the independent artistic conscience of the artist because artists, like anyone else who accepts financial support, will inevitably try to please those who control the distribution of that support. Statement3: Senator beton concludes that government funding of the arts not only is a burden on taxpayers but also can not lead to the creation of works of true artistic excellence.
hypothesis: Premise1: A contemporary work of art that does not reflect the independent artistic conscience of the artist cannot be a work of true artistic excellence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Distribution of government funds for the arts is based on a broad agreement as to what constitutes artistic excellence.

premise: Statement1: Many people suffer an allergic reaction to sulfites, including those that are commonly added to wine as preservatives. Statement2: However, since there are several winemakers producing wine to which no sulfites are added, those who would like to drink wine but are allergic to sulfites can drink these wines without risking an allergic reaction to sulfites.
hypothesis: Premise1: Sulfites occur naturally in most wine.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Apart from sulfites, there are other substances commonly present in wine that can trigger allergic reactions.

premise: Statement1: Public health experts have waged a long-standing educational campaign to get people to eat more vegetables, which are known to help prevent cancer. Statement2: Unfortunately, the campaign has had little impact on people's diets. Statement3: The reason is probably that many people simply dislike the taste of most vegetables. Statement4: Thus, the campaign would probably be more effective if it included information on ways to make vegetables more appetizing.
hypothesis: Premise1: People who dislike the taste of most vegetables would eat many more vegetables if they knew how to make them more appetizing.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some ways of making vegetables more appetizing diminish vegetables' ability to help prevent cancer.

premise: Statement1: Scientists have shown that older bees, which usually forage outside the hive for food, tend to have larger brains than do younger bees, which usually do not forage but instead remain in the hive to tend to newly hatched bees. Statement2: Since foraging requires greater cognitive ability than does tending to newly hatched bees, it appears that foraging leads to the increased brain size of older bees.
hypothesis: Premise1: The brains of older bees that never learn to forage are the same size as those of their foraging counterparts of the same age.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Those bees that travel a long distance to find food do not have significantly larger brains than do bees that locate food nearer the hive.

premise: Statement1: Science writer : all scientists have beliefs and values that might slant their interpretations of the data from which they draw their conclusions. However, serious scientific papers are carefully reviewed by many other scientists before publication. Statement2: These reviewers are likely to notice and object to biases that they do not share. Statement3: Thus, any slanted interpretations of scientific data will generally have been removed before publication.
hypothesis: Premise1: In general, biases that slant interpretations of data in serious scientific papers being reviewed for publication are not shared among all scientists.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Biases that are present in published scientific papers and shared by most scientists, including those who review the papers, are unlikely to impair the scientific value of those papers.

premise: Statement1: Many of those who are most opposed to cruelty to animals in the laboratory, in the slaughterhouse, or on the farm are people who truly love animals and who keep pets. The vast majority of domestic pets, however, are dogs and cats, and both of these species are usually fed meat. Statement2: Therefore, many of those who are most opposed to cruelty to animals do, in fact, contribute to such cruelty.
hypothesis: Premise1: Feeding meat to pets contributes to cruelty to animals.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many popular pets are not usually fed meat.

premise: Statement1: Museum curator : sent2: our ancient egyptian collection includes an earthenware hippopotamus that resembles a child's toy. Statement2: It was discovered in a tomb, upside down, with its legs broken off. We know that the ancient egyptians believed the dead had to wage eternal war with beasts. Statement3: Breaking the legs off a representation of an animal was thought to help a deceased person in this war. Statement4: We conclude that, far from being a toy, this hippopotamus was a religious object.
hypothesis: Premise1: The hippopotamus' legs were not broken through some natural occurrence after it was placed in the tomb.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Earthenware figures were never used as children's toys in ancient egypt.

premise: Statement1: Art historian : successful forgeries tend to be those most recently painted. While such a phenomenon may sound counterintuitive, a forger is able to exploit current modes of aesthetics to create paintings that appeal to the eye of his or her contemporaries. Statement2: This very quality, however, is what makes those paintings seem amateurish to subsequent generations. Statement3: As a result, a majority of forgeries are identified as such roughly twenty-five years after their creation.
hypothesis: Premise1: Those who expose the majority of paintings as forgeries are guided by aesthetic considerations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: What is deemed aesthetically pleasing does not change in the course of twenty-five years.

premise: Statement1: John works five days each week except when on vacation or during weeks in which national holidays occur. Four days a week he works in an insurance company ; on fridays he works as a blacksmith. Statement2: Last week there were no holidays, and john was not on vacation. Statement3: Therefore, he must have worked in the insurance company on monday, tuesday, wednesday, and thursday last week.
hypothesis: Premise1: Last week john worked neither on saturday nor on sunday.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: John does not take vacations in weeks in which national holidays occur.

premise: Statement1: Essayist : many social critics have claimed that modern society has become so heavily regulated by corporate and government bureaucracies that individuals lead lives over which they feel they have nearly no control. Statement2: That such a perceived loss of freedom is a complete myth, however, should be obvious from the fact that people who live in modern society voluntarily become members of clubs, political movements, and other small groups that place additional restrictions on their decisions.
hypothesis: Premise1: Only people who do not feel highly restricted are willing to assume further restrictions on their decisions.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Only people who think their lives are controlled by large, impersonal bureaucracies are willing to accept additional restrictions on their decisions.

premise: Statement1: The symptoms of mental disorders are behavioral, cognitive, or emotional problems. Statement2: Some patients with mental disorders can be effectively treated with psychotherapy. But it is now known that in some patients mental disorders result from chemical imbalances affecting the brain. Statement3: Thus these patients can be effectively treated only with medication that will reduce or correct the imbalance.
hypothesis: Premise1: Treatment by psychotherapy can produce no effective reduction in or correction of chemical imbalances that cause mental disorders.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most mental disorders are not the result of chemical imbalances affecting the brain.

premise: Statement1: The state has proposed a plan requiring all young people to perform community service as a requirement for graduation from high school. Statement2: The goal is to use the young people to provide services to correct social ills, especially those in housing and crime. Statement3: Young people, however, should only be required to participate in educational activities to graduate from high school. Statement4: For that reason, the proposed program should not be implemented.
hypothesis: Premise1: Community service does not provide any educational benefit to young people.
