alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some of the social ills that could be helped are being ignored by this program.

premise: Statement1: Company spokesperson : household products magazine claims that our filterator x water filter does not remove chemical contaminants in significant amounts. This attack on the quality of our product is undermined by the experience of the millions of filterator x owners who are satisfied with the product's performance.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most filterator x owners have no way to determine how effectively the product removes chemical contaminants from water.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Household products did not evaluate whether the filterator x water filter significantly improved the taste of drinking water.

premise: Statement1: Among people who live to the age of 100 or more, a large proportion have led" unhealthy" lives : smoking, consuming alcohol, eating fatty foods, and getting little exercise. Since such behavior often leads to shortened life spans, it is likely that exceptionally long-lived people are genetically disposed to having long lives.
hypothesis: Premise1: Nearly all people who live to 100 or more have siblings who are also long-lived.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some of the exceptionally long-lived people who exercise regularly and avoid fatty foods do smoke or consume alcohol.

premise: Statement1: A recent study found that snoring, though not common in either group, is more common among smokers than among nonsmokers. Statement2: On the basis of this evidence, the author hypothesized that smoking by itself can induce snoring.
hypothesis: Premise1: Stress induces both snoring and smoking in certain individuals.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most smokers do not snore.

premise: Statement1: Birds have been said to be descended from certain birdlike dinosaur species with which they share distinctive structural features. Statement2: The fossil record, however, shows that this can not be so, since there are bird fossils much older than the earliest birdlike dinosaur fossils that have been found.
hypothesis: Premise1: There are no birdlike dinosaur fossils that are older than the bird fossils but have not yet been unearthed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The birdlike dinosaurs have no descendants.

premise: Statement1: Modern flamingos derive their pink coloration from pigments stored in tiny shrimp that they filter from shallow, salty waters. The shrimp get this pigment from tiny red algae that they filter through their leg bristles. Statement2: In the jurassic period ( about 200 million years ago ), both algae and shrimp were an excellent source of food for any larger animal equipped to sieve them out of the water through an anatomical strainer. The argentine pterodactyl possessed a row of thin, bristlelike teeth through which it pumped water, straining out any tiny food particles in the process. Statement3: Thus, because it was able to filter both algae and shrimp, it is reasonable to conclude that the pterodactyl acquired a pink coloration.
hypothesis: Premise1: The argentine pterodactyl's habitat included shallow seas that were particularly rich in red algae and shrimp.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the argentine pterodactyl did not eat a diet containing red algae, its color was determined by factors other than diet.

premise: Statement1: The number of codfish in the north atlantic has declined substantially as the population of harp seals has increased from two million to more than three million. Some blame the seal for the shrinking cod population, but cod plays a negligible role in the seal's diet. Statement2: It is therefore unlikely that the increase in the seal population has contributed significantly to the decline in the cod population.
hypothesis: Premise1: Cod feed almost exclusively on capelin, a fish that is a staple of the harp seal's diet.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Water pollution poses a more serious threat to cod than to the harp seal.

premise: Statement1: Until now, only injectable vaccines against influenza have been available. Statement2: Parents are reluctant to subject children to the pain of injections, but adults, who are at risk of serious complications from influenza, are commonly vaccinated. Statement3: A new influenza vaccine, administered painlessly in a nasal spray, is effective for children. However, since children seldom develop serious complications from influenza, no significant public health benefit would result from widespread vaccination of children using the nasal spray.
hypothesis: Premise1: Adults do not contract influenza primarily from children who have influenza.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The injectable vaccine is affordable for all adults.

premise: Statement1: Eight large craters run in a long straight line across a geographical region. Although some of the craters contain rocks that have undergone high-pressure shocks characteristic of meteorites slamming into earth, these shocks could also have been caused by extreme volcanic events. Statement2: Because of the linearity of the craters, it is very unlikely that some of them were caused by volcanoes and others were caused by meteorites. Statement3: Thus, since the craters are all different ages, they were probably caused by volcanic events rather than meteorites.
hypothesis: Premise1: No known natural cause would likely account for eight meteorite craters of different ages forming a straight line.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There is no independent evidence of a volcanic event strong enough to have created the high-pressure shocks that are characteristic of meteorites slamming into earth.

premise: Statement1: Carl is clearly an incompetent detective. Statement2: He has solved a smaller percentage of the cases assigned to him in the last 3 years -- only 1 out of 25 -- than any other detective on the police force.
hypothesis: Premise1: Because the police chief regards carl as the most capable detective, she assigns him only the most difficult cases, ones that others have failed to solve.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Carl was previously a detective in a police department in another city, and in the 4 years he spent there, he solved only 1 out of 30 crimes.

premise: Statement1: Farmer : agricultural techniques such as crop rotation that do not use commercial products may solve agricultural problems at least as well as any technique, such as pesticide application, that does use such products. Nonetheless, no private for-profit corporation will sponsor research that is unlikely to lead to marketable products. Statement2: Thus, for the most part, only government-sponsored research investigates agricultural techniques that do not use commercial products.
hypothesis: Premise1: Investigations of agricultural techniques are rarely sponsored by individuals or by any entity other than private for-profit corporations or the government.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most if not all investigations of agricultural techniques that use commercial products are sponsored by private for-profit corporations.

premise: Statement1: Ethicist : studies have documented the capacity of placebos to reduce pain in patients who believe that they are receiving beneficial drugs. Statement2: Some doctors say that they administer placebos because medically effective treatment reinforced by the placebo effect sometimes helps patients recover faster than good treatment alone. Statement3: But administering placebos is nonetheless ethically questionable, for even if a placebo benefits a patient, a doctor might, for example, have prescribed it just to give the patient satisfaction that something was being done.
hypothesis: Premise1: The motivation for administering a placebo can be relevant to the ethical justification for doing so.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The pain relief produced by the placebo effect justifies the deception involved in administering a placebo.

premise: Statement1: In recent years, networks of fiber-optic cable have been replacing electrical wire for transmitting telecommunications signals. Statement2: Signals running through fiber-optic cables deteriorate, and so the signals must be run through a piece of equipment called a regenerator before being transmitted farther. Light-line is the leading manufacturer of signal regenerators. Statement3: Therefore, light-line is one of the companies that will most benefit if new long-distance fiber-optic telecommunications networks are constructed.
hypothesis: Premise1: Several manufacturers are developing regenerator technologies that will allow signals to be transmitted many times farther than at present before requiring regeneration.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Expanding its production of regenerators will require light-line to spend proportionately more on labor and materials.

premise: Statement1: Red admiral butterflies fly in a highly irregular fashion, constantly varying their speed, wing strokes, and flight path. Statement2: While predators avoid poisonous butterfly species, nonpoisonous butterflies like the red admiral need to elude predators to survive. Statement3: Scientists therefore hypothesize that the red admiral's flight style, which is clearly not energy efficient, evolved as a means of avoiding predators.
hypothesis: Premise1: No species of poisonous butterfly has an irregular flight style like that of the red admiral.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: It is much more energy efficient for butterflies to fly in an irregular fashion than it is for heavier varieties of insects.

premise: Statement1: Mayor of plainsville : in order to help the economy of plainsville, i am using some of our tax revenues to help bring a major highway through the town and thereby attract new business to plainsville. Statement2: Citizens'group : you must have interests other than our economy in mind. Statement3: If you were really interested in helping our economy, you would instead allocate the revenues to building a new business park, since it would bring in twice the business that your highway would.
hypothesis: Premise1: The mayor accepts that a new business park would bring in more new business than would the new highway.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Plainsville's economy will not be helped unless a new business park of the sort envisioned by the citizens' group is built.

premise: Statement1: A survey of address changes filed with post offices and driver's license bureaus over the last ten years has established that households moving out of the city of weston outnumbered households moving into the city two to one. Statement2: Therefore, we can expect that next year's census, which counts all residents regardless of age, will show that the population of weston has declined since the last census ten years ago.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most adults moving out of weston were parents who had children living with them, whereas most adults remaining in or moving into the city were older people who lived alone.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many people moving into weston failed to notify either the post office or the driver's license bureau that they had moved to the city.

premise: Statement1: While the total enrollment of public elementary and secondary schools in sondland is one percent higher this academic year than last academic year, the number of teachers there increased by three percent. Statement2: Thus, the sondland education commission's prediction of a teacher shortage as early as next academic year is unfounded.
hypothesis: Premise1: A new law has mandated that there be ten percent more teachers per pupil in sondland's public schools next academic year than there were this academic year.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Funding for public elementary schools in sondland is expected to increase over the next ten years.

premise: Statement1: There are far fewer independent bookstores than there were 20 years ago, largely because chain bookstores prospered and multiplied during that time. Statement2: Thus, chain bookstores'success has been to the detriment of book consumers, for the shortage of independent bookstores has prevented the variety of readily available books from growing as much as it otherwise would have.
hypothesis: Premise1: Book consumers would be better off if there were a greater variety of readily available books than there currently is.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Independent bookstores typically do not sell the kinds of books that are available in chain bookstores.

premise: Statement1: Ramirez : the film industry claims that pirated dvds, which are usually cheaper than legitimate dvds and become available well before a film's official dvd release date, adversely affect its bottom line. But the industry should note what the spread of piracy indicates : consumers want lower prices and faster dvd releases. Statement2: Lowering prices of dvds and releasing them sooner would mitigate piracy's negative effect on film industry profits.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some people who would otherwise purchase pirated dvds would be willing to purchase legitimate dvds if they were less expensive and released earlier than they are now.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Releasing legitimate dvds earlier would not cause any reduction in the revenue the film industry receives from the films' theatrical release.

premise: Statement1: Egidio's gym has been in operation for seven years, and offers regular weekly weight training and aerobic classes, as well as occasional programs in dance and martial arts. Statement2: The gym has seen a surge of new members in the past twelve months, despite keeping the same membership fee over the past three years. Statement3: Of the 450 current members, more than 250 have joined in the past year. Statement4: Clearly, the membership of egidio's gym has risen a significant amount because of its superior facilities.
hypothesis: Premise1: Fewer than 250 members ended their membership at egidio's gym over the last year.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: None of the other gyms in this town saw any increase in membership during the past year.

premise: Statement1: The higher the average cholesterol intake among the residents of a nation, the higher the incidence of heart disease in that nation ; the lower the average cholesterol intake, the lower the incidence of heart disease. Statement2: So people who want to decrease their risk of heart disease should reduce their cholesterol intake.
hypothesis: Premise1: Individual residents of high cholesterol nations who consume low cholesterol diets have the same rate of heart disease as the national average.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Any individual resident of a nation whose population has a high average cholesterol intake may have a diet low in cholesterol.

premise: Statement1: Until now, only injectable vaccines against influenza have been available. A new vaccine administered in a nasal spray has proven effective in preventing influenza in children. Statement2: They have been used primarily by older adults, who are at risk for complications from influenza. Statement3: Since children are significantly more likely than adults to contract and spread influenza, making the new vaccine widely available for children will greatly reduce the spread of influenza across the population.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many parents would be more inclined to have their children vaccinated against influenza if the vaccination did not require an injection.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The new vaccine uses the same mechanism to ward off influenza as injectable vaccines do.

premise: Statement1: Editorial : the roof of northtown council's equipment-storage building collapsed under the weight of last week's heavy snowfall. The building was constructed recently and met local building-safety codes in every particular, except that the nails used for attaching roof supports to the building's columns were of a smaller size than the codes specify for this purpose. Statement2: Clearly, this collapse exemplifies how even a single, apparently insignificant, departure from safety standards can have severe consequences.
hypothesis: Premise1: The amount of snow that accumulated on the roof of the equipment-storage building was greater than the predicted maximum that was used in drawing up the safety codes.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Because the equipment-storage building was not intended for human occupation, some safety-code provisions that would have applied to an office building did not apply to it.

premise: Statement1: Most people in the united states view neither big nor small business as particularly efficient or dynamic and regard both as providing consumers with fairly priced goods and services. Statement2: However, most people consistently perceive small business as a force for good in society, whereas big business is perceived as socially responsible only in times of prosperity.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many people in the united states regard the social responsibility of big business as extending beyond providing consumers with fairly priced goods and services.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most people in the united states give little thought to the value of business to society.

premise: Statement1: All oceangoing ships carry seawater ballast tanks whose weight improves stability. Statement2: To maintain the ship's proper stability, water must be pumped out of these tanks when cargo is loaded and into them when cargo is unloaded. As a result, sea creatures often get into the tanks and are then inadvertently deposited into new habitats, where they can wreak ecological havoc. Statement3: One viable way of addressing this problem would be to empty and then immediately refill the tanks in midocean, since midocean creatures and coastal sea creatures usually can not survive in one another's habitats.
hypothesis: Premise1: There are at least some oceangoing ships whose stability could be adequately maintained while emptying and refilling their ballast tanks in midocean.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Sea creatures have rarely, if ever, wreaked ecological havoc in a new habitat, unless they have been able to survive in that habitat after having been deposited there by oceangoing ships.

premise: Statement1: Advertisement : when your car 's engine is running at its normal operating temperature, any major brand of motor oil will protect it about as well as tuff does. Statement2: When the engine is cold, it is a different story : tuff motor oil flows better at lower temperatures than its major competitors do. Statement3: So, if you want your car 's engine to have maximum protection, you should use tuff.
hypothesis: Premise1: Tuff motor oil provides above-average protection for engines that happen to overheat.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Tuff motor oil is manufactured at only one refinery and shipped from there to all markets.

premise: Statement1: Since mayor drabble always repays her political debts as soon as possible, she will almost certainly appoint lee to be the new head of the arts commission. Statement2: Lee has wanted that job for a long time, and drabble owes lee a lot for his support in the last election.
hypothesis: Premise1: Mayor drabble has no political debt that is both of longer standing than the one she owes to lee and could as suitably be repaid by an appointment to be the new head of the arts commission.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Whether lee is qualified to head the arts commission is irrelevant to mayor drabble's decision.

premise: Statement1: Doctor : recent pharmaceutical advances will lead the way in weight loss. Statement2: Prior to these advancements, obesity-related deaths outnumbered all other causes of death by a wide margin. Statement3: The new drugs will curb appetite and increase metabolism. Statement4: Thanks to these advancements, obesity will dramatically decline in the near future.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most people will not be able to afford these prescriptions since the majority of health care plans will not cover the new drugs.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Increasing metabolism would significantly reduce obesity across the country.

premise: Statement1: Humanitarian considerations aside, sheer economics dictates that country x should institute, as country y has done, a nationwide system of air and ground transportation for conveying seriously injured persons to specialized trauma centers. Timely access to the kind of medical care that only specialized centers can provide could save the lives of many people. Statement2: The earnings of these people would result in a substantial increase in country x's gross national product, and the taxes paid on those earnings would substantially augment government revenues.
hypothesis: Premise1: There would be a net increase in employment in country x if more persons survived serious injury
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are no specialized trauma centers in country x at present

premise: Statement1: The habit many students on today's campuses have of scribbling in their textbooks is inexcusable. Statement2: It is harmful to books, aesthetically displeasing, and distracting to readers who buy the textbooks used.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most students who buy used textbooks say they are aesthetically indifferent to the scribbling and sometimes find it helpful.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In most areas, scribbling in textbooks is not illegal.

premise: Statement1: After being subjected to clinical tests like those used to evaluate the effectiveness of prescription drugs, a popular nonprescription herbal remedy was found to be as effective in treating painful joints as is a certain prescription drug that has been used successfully to treat this condition. Statement2: The manufacturer of the herbal remedy cited the test results as proof that chemical agents are unnecessary for the successful treatment of painful joints.
hypothesis: Premise1: The herbal remedy contains no chemical agents that are effective in treating painful joints.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: People are likely to switch from using prescription drugs to using herbal remedies if the herbal remedies are found to be as effective as the prescription drugs.

premise: Statement1: In the next decade, a large percentage of municipalities throughout the country will experience power failures if they do not modernize their electrical substations. Statement2: The vast majority of substations in the country already handle currents between 60 % and 80 % of their maximum capacity, and currents are expected to rise, perhaps by as much as a factor of 3 in the next decade, because of population increase and the increased demand from both industry and electronics in individual homes.
hypothesis: Premise1: An electrical substation fails when the current it handles rises above its maximum capacity.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many electrical substations in operation today are old, and have several aged components that could break down, leading to power failures, even if current levels don't increase substantially.

premise: Statement1: Several recent studies establish that most people would not quit their jobs if they won the lottery. Statement2: In each study, over 80 percent of the people surveyed indicated that they would not quit their jobs.
hypothesis: Premise1: Several of the researchers performing the surveys had no background in business.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The people surveyed were all college students in first-year business courses.

premise: Statement1: Among the many temptations of the digital age, manipulation of photographs has proved particularly troublesome for science. Statement2: Recently, a journal of cellular biology began using a software tool to examine the digital images submitted along with articles for publication. It discovered that dozens of authors had submitted digital images that had been manipulated in ways that violated the journal's guidelines. Statement3: Clearly, scientific fraud is a widespread problem among the authors submitting to that journal.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many of the scientists who submitted articles with manipulated images did so in order to misrepresent the information conveyed by those images.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Scientific fraud is possible in the field of cellular biology only if the research is documented with digital images.

premise: Statement1: In 1992, a major newspaper circulated throughout north america paid its reporters an average salary that was much lower than the average salary paid by its principal competitors to their reporters. An executive of the newspaper argued that this practice was justified, since any shortfall that might exist in the reporters'salaries is fully compensated by the valuable training they receive through their assignments.
hypothesis: Premise1: Most of the newspaper's reporters had worked there for more than ten years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The circulation of the newspaper had recently reached a plateau, after it had increased steadily throughout the 1980s.

premise: Statement1: At many electronics retail stores, the consumer has the option of purchasing product warranties that extend beyond the manufacturer's warranty. Most problems with electronic goods occur within the period covered by the manufacturer's warranty. Statement2: However, consumers are generally better off not buying extended warranties.
hypothesis: Premise1: Problems with electronic goods that occur after the manufacturer's warranty expires are generally inexpensive to fix in comparison with the cost of an extended warranty.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Retail stores sell extended warranties in part because consumers who purchase them are likely to purchase other products from the same store.

premise: Statement1: Psychology researchers observed that parents feel emotion while singing to their infants. The researchers concluded that their hypothesis was correct. Statement2: The researchers hypothesized that this emotion noticeably affects the sound of the singing. Statement3: To test this hypothesis the parents were recorded while singing to their infants and while singing with no infant present. They were instructed to make the two renditions as similar as possible. Statement4: These recordings were then played for psychologists who had not observed the recordings being made. For 80 percent of the recordings, these psychologists were able to correctly identify, by listening alone, which recordings were of parents singing to their children.
hypothesis: Premise1: When a person feels emotion, that emotion provokes involuntary physiological responses that affect the vocal cords and lungs.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A separate study by the same researchers found that parents feel more emotion when singing to their own children than when singing to other children.

premise: Statement1: Statistician : a financial magazine claimed that its survey of its subscribers showed that north americans are more concerned about their personal finances than about politics. One question was :" which do you think about more : politics or the joy of earning money? " Statement2: This question is clearly biased. Also, the readers of the magazine are a self-selecting sample. Statement3: Thus, there is reason to be skeptical about the conclusion drawn in the magazine's survey.
hypothesis: Premise1: Other surveys suggest that north americans are concerned not only with politics and finances, but also with social issues.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The credibility of the magazine has been called into question on a number of occasions.

premise: Statement1: The introduction of symbols for numbers is an event lost in prehistory, but the earliest known number symbols, in the form of simple grooves and scratches on bones and stones, date back 20,000 years or more. Statement2: Nevertheless, since it was not until 5,500 years ago that systematic methods for writing numerals were invented, it was only then that any sort of computation became possible.
hypothesis: Premise1: Computation of any sort required a systematic method for writing numerals.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some kinds of surfaces upon which numeric symbols could have been made in the period before 5, 500 years ago were not used for that purpose.

premise: Statement1: Problems caused by the leaching of pollutants from dumps and landfills are worst in countries with an annual per capita economic output of $ 4,000 to $ 5,000, and less severe for considerably poorer and considerably richer countries. This is so because pollution problems increase during the early stages of a country's industrial development but then diminish as increasing industrial development generates adequate resources to tackle such problems. Statement2: Therefore, problems caused by such leaching in country x, where the annual per capita economic output is now $ 5,000, should begin to diminish in the next few years.
hypothesis: Premise1: Industrial development in country x will increase in the next few years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Countries surrounding country x will reduce the amount of pollution that their factories release into the air and water.

premise: Statement1: Loggerhead turtles live and breed in distinct groups, of which some are in the pacific ocean and some are in the atlantic. Statement2: New evidence suggests that juvenile pacific loggerheads that feed near the baja peninsula hatch in japanese waters 10,000 kilometers away. Statement3: Ninety-five percent of the dna samples taken from the baja turtles match those taken from turtles at the japanese nesting sites.
hypothesis: Premise1: Ninety-five percent of the dna samples taken from the baja turtles match those taken from atlantic loggerhead turtles.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Commercial aquariums have been successfully breeding atlantic loggerheads with pacific loggerheads for the last five years.

premise: Statement1: Though earth's human population is increasing, it currently uses only a relatively small fraction of the supply of fresh water. Statement2: Thus, claims that water shortages will plague humankind in the near future unless population growth trends change are simply mistaken.
hypothesis: Premise1: The amount of fresh water available to meet the needs of earth's population varies significantly from region to region.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Population growth trends are notoriously hard to predict with reasonable accuracy.

premise: Statement1: Ordinary mountain sickness, a common condition among mountain climbers, and one from which most people can recover, is caused by the characteristic shortage of oxygen in the atmosphere at high altitudes. Cerebral edema, a rarer disruption of blood circulation in the brain that quickly becomes life-threatening if not correctly treated from its onset, can also be caused by a shortage of oxygen. Statement2: Since the symptoms of cerebral edema resemble those of ordinary mountain sickness, cerebral edema is especially dangerous at high altitudes.
hypothesis: Premise1: The treatment for ordinary mountain sickness differs from the treatment for cerebral edema.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Cerebral edema can cause those who suffer from it to slip into a coma within a few hours.

premise: Statement1: Start-up companies financed by venture capitalists have a much lower failure rate than companies financed by other means. Statement2: Source of financing, therefore, must be a more important causative factor in the success of a start-up company than are such factors as the personal characteristics of the entrepreneur, the quality of strategic planning, or the management structure of the company.
hypothesis: Premise1: Venture capitalists base their decisions to fund start. Up companies on such factors as the characteristics of the entrepreneur and quality of strategic planning of the company.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The strategic planning of a start-up company is a less important factor in the long-term success of the company than are the personal characteristics of the entrepreneur.

premise: Statement1: Printwell's ink jet division manufactures ink-jet printers and the ink cartridges they use. Statement2: Sales of its ink-jet printers have increased. Unfortunately, printwell has been unable to bring down the cost of manufacturing a printer. Statement3: Monthly revenues from those sales, however, have not increased, because competition has forced printwell to cut the prices of its printers. Statement4: Thus, despite the increase in printer sales, the ink jet division must be contributing less to the company's profits than it used to.
hypothesis: Premise1: Ink-jet printers in regular use frequently need new ink cartridges, and printwell's printers only accept printwell's ink cartridges.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Unlike some competing companies, printwell sells all of its printers through retailers, and these retailers' costs account for a sizable proportion of the printers' ultimate retail price.

premise: Statement1: Zoologist : plants preferentially absorb heavy nitrogen from rainwater. Statement2: Heavy nitrogen consequently becomes concentrated in the tissues of herbivores, and animals that eat meat in turn exhibit even higher concentrations of heavy nitrogen in their bodily tissues. We compared bone samples from european cave bears of the ice age with blood samples from present-day bears fed meat-enriched diets, and the levels of heavy nitrogen present in these samples were identical. Statement3: Thus, the prehistoric european cave bears were not exclusively herbivores.
hypothesis: Premise1: Bone samples from present-day bears fed meat-enriched diets exhibit the same levels of heavy nitrogen as do their blood samples.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The level of heavy nitrogen in the bones of any bear fed a meat-enriched diet is the same as that in the bones of any other meat-eating bear.

premise: Statement1: One variety of partially biodegradable plastic beverage container is manufactured from small bits of plastic bound together by a degradable bonding agent such as cornstarch. Statement2: Since only the bonding agent degrades, leaving the small bits of plastic, no less plastic refuse per container is produced when such containers are discarded than when comparable nonbiodegradable containers are discarded.
hypothesis: Premise1: The partially biodegradable plastic beverage containers are made with more plastic than comparable nonbiodegradable ones in order to compensate for the weakening effect of the bonding agents.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many consumers are ecology-minded and prefer to buy a product sold in the partially biodegradable plastic beverage containers rather than in nonbiodegradable containers, even if the price is higher.

premise: Statement1: Because of the threat of medical malpractice suits, many physicians are subjecting their patients to unnecessary medical testing. Statement2: Physicians want to be able to explain their actions if anything goes wrong, and they want to be able to show that they tried everything to properly diagnose and treat an ailment. Statement3: It is difficult for physicians to judge exactly how thorough they need to be in order to avoid potential liability. Statement4: It is generally wise, therefore, for patients to avoid going to a physician unless the patients are very sick.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many diseases, if they are not caught early, can cause death.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Patients are unable to determine what is the appropriate level of testing.

premise: Statement1: When exercising the muscles in one's back, it is important, in order to maintain a healthy back, to exercise the muscles on opposite sides of the spine equally. Statement2: After all, balanced muscle development is needed to maintain a healthy back, since the muscles on opposite sides of the spine must pull equally in opposing directions to keep the back in proper alignment and protect the spine.
hypothesis: Premise1: Exercising the muscles on opposite sides of the spine unequally tends to lead to unbalanced muscle development.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Muscles on opposite sides of the spine that are equally well developed will be enough to keep the back in proper alignment.

premise: Statement1: The soaring prices of scholarly and scientific journals have forced academic libraries used only by academic researchers to drastically reduce their list of subscriptions. Statement2: Some have suggested that in each academic discipline subscription decisions should be determined solely by a journal's usefulness in that discipline, measured by the frequency with which it is cited in published writings by researchers in the discipline.
hypothesis: Premise1: Researchers often will not cite a journal article that has influenced their work if they think that the journal in which it appears is not highly regarded by the leading researchers in the mainstream of the discipline.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The increasingly expensive scholarly journals are less and less likely to be available to the general public from nonacademic public libraries.

premise: Statement1: In wareland last year, 16 percent of licensed drivers under 21 and 11 percent of drivers ages 21-24 were in serious accidents. By contrast, only 3 percent of licensed drivers 65 and older were involved in serious accidents. Statement2: These figures clearly show that the greater experience and developed habits of caution possessed by drivers in the 65-and-older group make them far safer behind the wheel than the younger drivers are.
hypothesis: Premise1: Drivers 65 and older do not, on average, drive very many fewer miles per year than drivers 24 and younger.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Drivers 65 and older are less likely than are drivers 24 and younger to drive during weather conditions that greatly increase the risk of accidents.

premise: Statement1: Mice that have been given morphine are very likely to develop blood poisoning because bacteria that normally reside in the intestine typically respond to morphine by migrating into the bloodstream. However, when mice are given both morphine and the new drug naltrexone, blood poisoning is much less frequent, although it does still occur. Statement2: These results provide support for researchers'prediction that naltrexone will turn out to be toxic to certain types of bacteria.
hypothesis: Premise1: Naltrexone inhibits morphine from triggering the migration of intestinal bacteria into the bloodstream.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Mice that have been given naltrexone but not morphine have no greater risk of developing blood poisoning than do mice that have not been given either substance.

premise: Statement1: Although early jazz music featured a great deal of improvisation, this improvisation did not stray too far from the rather simple, catchy melodies upon which it was based. Statement2: So, however interesting it may be, later music featuring improvisation that strays far from the melody ought not to be classified as jazz.
hypothesis: Premise1: The later music featuring improvisation has much more in common with early jazz than with any other type of music.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Many types of music other than jazz feature a great deal of improvisation.

premise: Statement1: Herbalist : many herbal medicines work best when they have a chance to influence the body gently over several months. Statement2: However, many of these herbal medicines have toxic side effects when taken daily for such long periods. Statement3: Therefore, at least some people who use herbal medicines daily should occasionally skip their usual dose for a day or two, to give the body a chance to recuperate.
hypothesis: Premise1: At least some people who use herbal medicines daily use them for periods long enough for the medicines to have side effects.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: At least some herbal medicines work less well in achieving their desired effects if one occasionally skips one's usual dose than if one does not.

premise: Statement1: The top 50 centimeters of soil on tiliga island contain bones from the native birds eaten by the islanders since the first human immigration to the island 3,000 years ago. A comparison of this top layer with the underlying 150 centimeters of soil -- accumulated over 80,000 years -- reveals that before humans arrived on tiliga, a much larger and more diverse population of birds lived there. Statement2: Thus, the arrival of humans dramatically decreased the population and diversity of birds on tiliga.
hypothesis: Premise1: The arrival of a species of microbe, carried by some birds but deadly to many others, immediately preceded the first human immigration to tiliga.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The bird species known to have been eaten by the islanders had few natural predators on tiliga.

premise: Statement1: The more demand there is for something, the easier it is to sell. Statement2: Any junkyard will buy a used car that is less than ten years old, because the parts can easily be resold. However, because there is little demand for car parts that are ten years old or older, junkyards tend not to buy those cars. Statement3: Thus, used cars that are less than ten years old are generally easier to sell than are cars that are ten years old or older.
hypothesis: Premise1: The salability of cars that are ten years old or older is largely a function of the level of demand for their parts.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The salability of something is not influenced by any factors other than the level of demand for it.

premise: Statement1: Although fullerenes -- spherical molecules made entirely of carbon -- were first found in the laboratory, they have since been found in nature, formed in fissures of the rare mineral shungite. Statement2: Since laboratory synthesis of fullerenes requires distinctive conditions of temperature and pressure, this discovery should give geologists a test case for evaluating hypotheses about the state of the earth's crust at the time these naturally occurring fullerenes were formed.
hypothesis: Premise1: The naturally occurring fullerenes are arranged in a previously unknown crystalline structure.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some fullerenes have also been found on the remains of a small meteorite that collided with a spacecraft.

premise: Statement1: In a recent study, one group of participants watched video recordings of themselves running on treadmills, and a second group watched recordings of other people running on treadmills. When contacted later, participants in the first group reported exercising, on average, 1 hour longer each day than did the other participants. Statement2: This shows that watching a recording of yourself exercising can motivate you to exercise more.
hypothesis: Premise1: In studies of identical twins, participants who observed their twin reading overreported by a significant amount how much time they themselves spent reading in the days that followed.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Participants who were already highly motivated to exercise did not report exercising for any longer each day than they had before the study.

premise: Statement1: Sociologist : the intended function of news is to give us information on which to act. Statement2: But in a consumer society, news becomes a product to be manufactured and dispensed to the consumer. Statement3: An enormous industry for the production and consumption of news has evolved, and we ingest news with an insatiable appetite. Statement4: Under such circumstances, news is primarily entertaining and can not, therefore, serve its intended function.
hypothesis: Premise1: News that primarily entertains does not give us information on which to act.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: News that serves its intended function should not be entertaining.

premise: Statement1: Psychologists have found that candidates for top political offices who blink excessively during televised debates are judged by viewers to have done less well than competing candidates who exhibit average blink rates. Statement2: Any impact this phenomenon has on election results is surely deleterious : many features -- knowledgeableness, confidence, and so forth -- contribute to a political official's ability to perform well in office, but having an average blink rate is certainly not such a feature.
hypothesis: Premise1: Excessive blinking has been shown to be a mostly reliable indicator of a lack of confidence.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Voters' judgments about candidates' debate performances rarely affect the results of national elections.

premise: Statement1: Gartland has long been narrowly self-sufficient in both grain and meat. However, as per capita income in gartland has risen toward the world average, per capita consumption of meat has also risen toward the world average, and it takes several pounds of grain to produce one pound of meat. Statement2: Therefore, since per capita income continues to rise, whereas domestic grain production will not increase, gartland will soon have to import either grain or meat or both.
hypothesis: Premise1: People in gartland who increase their consumption of meat will not radically decrease their consumption of grain.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In gartland, neither meat nor grain is subject to government price controls.

premise: Statement1: In order to reduce dependence on imported oil, the government of jalica has imposed minimum fuel-efficiency requirements on all new cars, beginning this year. Statement2: The more fuel-efficient a car, the less pollution it produces per mile driven. Statement3: As jalicans replace their old cars with cars that meet the new requirements, annual pollution from car traffic is likely to decrease in jalica.
hypothesis: Premise1: Jalicans who get cars that are more fuel-efficient tend to do more driving than before.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In jalica, domestically produced oil is more expensive than imported oil.

premise: Statement1: Some people think that in every barrel of politicians there are only a few rotten ones. But if deceit is a quality of rottenness, i believe all effective politicians are rotten. Statement2: They must be deceitful in order to do the job properly. Statement3: Someone who is scrupulously honest about obeying the rules of society will never be an effective politician.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some scrupulously honest politicians are effective.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some politicians are scrupulously honest.

premise: Statement1: Agricultural societies can not exist without staple crops. All the recorded staple crops grown in western africa were introduced from elsewhere, beginning, at some unknown date, with rice and yams. Statement2: Several food plants, such as kola and okra, are known to have been domesticated in western africa, but they are all supplemental, not staple, foods. Statement3: Therefore, discovering when rice and yams were introduced into western africa would establish the earliest date at which agricultural societies could have arisen there.
hypothesis: Premise1: People in western africa did not develop staple crops that they stopped cultivating once rice and yams were introduced.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Kola and okra were domesticated in western africa before rice and yams were introduced there.

premise: Statement1: By examining fossilized beetles, a research team has produced the most detailed description yet of temperatures in britain over the past 22,000 years. Statement2: Fossils of species that still exist were selected and dated. Statement3: When individuals of several species found in the same place were found to date to the same period, the known temperature tolerances of the existing beetle species were used to determine the maximum summer temperature that could have existed at that place and period.
hypothesis: Premise1: The temperature tolerances of the beetle species did not change significantly during the 22, 000-year period.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Fossils of different species found in the same place belonged to different period.

premise: Statement1: The biocarb company wants to build a sterilization plant to treat contaminated medical waste in a city neighborhood where residents and environmental activists fear that such a facility will pollute the area. Statement2: Biocarb's president argues that the operation of the plant can not cause pollution because the waste would be sterile after processing by the plant. Statement3: He claims that after exposure for an hour to superheated steam in the autoclave, such refuse would be far cleaner than food prepared in the cleanest kitchen.
hypothesis: Premise1: Handling of the waste before treatment in the proposed facility will not pose a threat of pollution to the area.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Fear of pollution is the only argument against construction of an autoclave facility for medical waste.

premise: Statement1: One of the advantages of bacillus thuringiensis ( b.t. ) Toxins over chemical insecticides results from their specificity for pest insects. The toxins have no known detrimental effects on mammals or birds. In addition, the limited range of activity of the toxins toward insects means that often a particular toxin will kill pest species but not affect insects that prey upon the species. Statement2: This advantage makes b.t. Statement3: Toxins preferable to chemical insecticides for use as components of insect pest management programs.
hypothesis: Premise1: Insects build up resistance more readily to b. t. toxins than to chemical insecticides.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Chemical insecticides cause harm to a greater number of insect species than do b. t. toxins.

premise: Statement1: Aviation analyst : to combat human error involved in air traffic control, miro jets inc. has recently installed each of its dc-10 passenger planes with a special anti-collision device. Statement2: The device alerts the dc-10, via a red light, when another plane is slightly more than three minutes away from a potential collision. The red light comes on and stays on until the approaching plane is safely out of range. Statement3: Aviation experts at miro jet inc. have determined that three minutes is ample time for a plane to divert its respective flight path to avoid a collision. Statement4: Therefore, if the red light on the anti-collision device is off, the dc-10 is more than three minutes flying time from any plane.
hypothesis: Premise1: The anti-collision device only signals planes of oncoming directions, not those planes that are flying in parallel.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Recently, a near collision in which both planes were less than 90 seconds flying distance from each other was averted only by the prompt actions of air traffic controllers.

premise: Statement1: Surveys consistently show that the best-selling ice cream flavor is vanilla, although those who prefer chocolate rarely order vanilla. Statement2: Vanilla-flavored candy, then, probably sells better than chocolate-flavored candy.
hypothesis: Premise1: Preferences for certain ice cream flavors are similar to preferences for candy flavors.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Because someone prefers vanilla ice cream does not mean he prefers vanilla-flavored candy.

premise: Statement1: In countries in which new life-sustaining drugs can not be patented, such drugs are sold at widely affordable prices ; those same drugs, where patented, command premium prices because the patents shield patent-holding manufacturers from competitors. Statement2: These facts show that future access to new life-sustaining drugs can be improved if the practice of granting patents on newly developed life-sustaining drugs were to be abolished everywhere.
hypothesis: Premise1: Pharmaceutical companies can afford the research that goes into the development of new drugs only if patents allow them to earn high profits.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Countries that grant patents on life-sustaining drugs almost always ban their importation from countries that do not grant such patents.

premise: Statement1: Studies suggest that, for the vast majority of people who have normal blood pressure, any amount of sodium greater than that required by the body is simply excreted and does not significantly raise blood pressure. Statement2: So only persons who have high blood pressure and whose bodies are incapable of safely processing excess sodium need to restrict their sodium intake.
hypothesis: Premise1: Excess sodium intake over time often destroys the body's ability to process excess sodium.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Every human being has a physiological need for at least some sodium.

premise: Statement1: A new law gives ownership of patents-documents providing exclusive right to make and sell an invention-to universities, not the government, when those patents result from government-sponsored university research. Statement2: Administrators at logos university plan to sell any patents they acquire to corporations in order to fund programs to improve undergraduate teaching.
hypothesis: Premise1: Government-sponsored research conducted at logos university for the most part duplicates research already completed by several profit-making corporations.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Profit-making corporations interested in developing products based on patents held by universities are likely to try to serve as exclusive sponsors of ongoing university research projects.

premise: Statement1: A newly discovered painting seems to be the work of one of two 17th-century artists, either the northern german johannes drechen or the frenchman louis birelle, who sometimes painted in the same style as drechen. Statement2: Analysis of the carved picture frame, which has been identified as the painting's original 17th century frame, showed that it is made of wood found widely in northern germany at the time, but rare in the part of france where birelle lived. Statement3: This shows that the painting is most likely the work of drechen.
hypothesis: Premise1: The frame was made from wood local to the region where the picture was painted.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Sometimes a painting so closely resembles others of its era that no expert is able to confidently decide who painted it.

premise: Statement1: In ancient greece, court witnesses were not crossexamined and the jury, selected from the citizenry, received no guidance on points of law ; thus, it was extremely important for litigants to make a good impression on the jurors. Statement2: For this reason, courtroom oratory by litigants is a good source of data on the common conceptions of morality held by the citizens of ancient greece.
hypothesis: Premise1: Litigants believed jurors were likely to be impressed by litigants whose professed moral code most resembled their own.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Litigants believed jurors were more likely to be impressed by litigants whose personality they preferred.

premise: Statement1: Workers may complain about many things at work, but stress is not high on the list. Statement2: In fact, in a recent survey a majority placed boredom at the top of their list of complaints. Statement3: The assumption that job-related stress is the most serious problem for workers in the corporate world is thus simply not warranted.
hypothesis: Premise1: Workers who complain of boredom exhibit more stress-related symptoms than do those who claim their work is interesting.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Workers are less likely to complain about work if they feel that their jobs are secure.

premise: Statement1: A certain auto repair shop services a particular make of car in the downtown region of a particular city. Statement2: Three years ago, 4 % of all repairs at this shop were not successful --- the mechanics were not able to restore the car to a drivable state. This year, only 2 % of all repairs were not successful. Statement3: Clearly, the mechanics training course, given over a year ago, has considerably increased the skill of mechanics at this particular shop, so that fewer cars are beyond their ability to repair.
hypothesis: Premise1: The total number of cars served per year by this shop has increased by 35% over the past three years.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: A new shop uptown specializes in repairing cars of this make more than six years old, and most of these older cars are taken to this shop.

premise: Statement1: Ethicist : on average, animals raised on grain must be fed sixteen pounds of grain to produce one pound of meat. Statement2: A pound of meat is more nutritious for humans than a pound of grain, but sixteen pounds of grain could feed many more people than could a pound of meat. Statement3: With grain yields leveling off, large areas of farmland going out of production each year, and the population rapidly expanding, we must accept the fact that consumption of meat will soon be morally unacceptable.
hypothesis: Premise1: Often, cattle or sheep can be raised to maturity on grass from pastureland that is unsuitable for any other kind of farming.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Nutritionists agree that a diet composed solely of grain products is not adequate for human health.

premise: Statement1: Doctors in britain have long suspected that patients who wear tinted eyeglasses are abnormally prone to depression and hypochondria. Statement2: Psychological tests given there to hospital patients admitted for physical complaints like heart pain and digestive distress confirmed such a relationship. Statement3: Perhaps people whose relationship to the world is psychologically painful choose such glasses to reduce visual stimulation, which is perceived as irritating. Statement4: At any rate, it can be concluded that when such glasses are worn, it is because the wearer has a tendency to be depressed or hypochondriacal.
hypothesis: Premise1: The tinting does not dim light to the eye enough to depress the wearer's mood substantially.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: For hypochondriacs wearing tinted glasses, the glasses serve as a visual signal to others that the wearer's health is delicate.

premise: Statement1: Newspaper editorial : many pharmaceutical companies develop" me too" drugs, drugs designed to duplicate, more or less, the effect of another company's product that is already on the market. Statement2: Some critics object that such development constitutes an unnecessary, redundant use of resources that provides no new benefits to consumers. However, the entry of" me too" drugs into the market can result in a price reduction for the drugs they resemble. Statement3: Therefore, " me too" drugs can indeed benefit consumers.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some "me too" drugs turn out to be more effective than the drugs they were designed to imitate.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If all pharmaceutical companies developed "me too" drugs, fewer innovative drugs would be developed.

premise: Statement1: The ergonomic society conducted a study that indicated that many people develop severe back problems during adulthood, and that virtually all such people who received chiropractic treatment showed great improvement. Statement2: Therefore, in order to minimize the proportion of the population that suffers from back pain, the ergonomic society recommended that chiropractic treatment be directed toward those adults who suffer from severe back problems.
hypothesis: Premise1: Individuals who receive chiropractic or other treatment prior to developing severe back problems are not less likely to develop back pain than those who do not.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Any person who receives chiropractic treatment for back pain may also benefit from other forms of treatment.

premise: Statement1: Because a large disparity in pay between the public and private sectors has developed in recent years, many experienced and extremely capable government administrators have quit their posts and taken positions in private-sector management. Statement2: Government will be able to recapture these capable administrators by raising salaries to a level comparable to those of the private sector. Statement3: In that way, the functioning of public agencies will be improved.
hypothesis: Premise1: People who moved from jobs in government administration to private-sector management would choose to change careers again.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: If the disparity in pay between government administration and private-sector management increases, administrators will move to the public sector in large numbers.

premise: Statement1: Editorialist : some people propose that, to raise revenues and encourage conservation, our country's taxes on oil, gasoline, and coal should be increased. Statement2: Such a tax increase, however, would do more harm than good. Statement3: By raising energy costs, the tax increase would decrease our competitiveness with other countries. Statement4: Many families would be unfairly burdened with higher transportation costs. Statement5: Finally, by reducing the demand for energy, the tax increase would reduce the number of energy production jobs.
hypothesis: Premise1: The proposed tax increase would be larger for some energy sources than for others.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The editorialist's country's budget deficit will decrease if the energy tax increase is implemented, thus benefiting the economy.

premise: Statement1: A drug that is highly effective in treating many types of infection can, at present, be obtained only from the bark of the ibora, a tree that is quite rare in the wild. It takes the bark of 5,000 trees to make one kilogram of the drug. Statement2: It follows, therefore, that continued production of the drug must inevitably lead to the ibora 's extinction.
hypothesis: Premise1: The ibora can be propagated from cuttings and grown under cultivation.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The drug made from ibora bark is expensive to produce.

premise: Statement1: Geologist : a geological fault in the mountain under which the proposed nuclear waste storage facility would be buried could, after a few thousand years, cause the contents to seep out or water to seep in. Since nuclear waste remains dangerous for up to 25,000 years, such seepage would be disastrous. Statement2: So we should not place a nuclear waste storage facility under this mountain until scientists investigate whether this mountain has any geological faults.
hypothesis: Premise1: The scientists' investigation would conclusively show whether or not the mountain has any geological faults.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Nuclear waste could be stored in the proposed facility on a temporary basis.

premise: Statement1: For over two centuries, no one had been able to make damascus blades-blades with a distinctive serpentine surface pattern-but a contemporary sword maker may just have rediscovered how. Statement2: Using iron with trace impurities that precisely matched those present in the iron used in historic damascus blades, this contemporary sword maker seems to have finally hit on an intricate process by which he can produce a blade indistinguishable from a true damascus blade.
hypothesis: Premise1: Production of damascus blades by sword makers of the past ceased abruptly after those sword makers' original source of iron became exhausted.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The iron with which the contemporary sword maker made damascus blades came from a source of iron that was unknown two centuries ago.

premise: Statement1: There are two supposedly conflicting hypotheses as to what makes for great national leaders : one is that such leaders successfully shape public opinion, and the other is that they are adept at reacting to it. However, treating these hypotheses as mutually exclusive is evidently a mistake. Statement2: All leaders who have had success getting their programs passed by their country's legislature have been adroit both in shaping and reacting to public opinion.
hypothesis: Premise1: Having success getting programs passed by the legislature is indicative of being a great national leader.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: It is impossible to successfully shape public opinion without in some way reacting to it.

premise: Statement1: Editor : sent2: many candidates say that if elected they will reduce governmental intrusion into voters'lives. But voters actually elect politicians who instead promise that the government will provide assistance to solve their most pressing problems. Statement2: Governmental assistance, however, costs money, and money can come only from taxes, which can be considered a form of governmental intrusion. Statement3: Thus, governmental intrusion into the lives of voters will rarely be substantially reduced over time in a democracy.
hypothesis: Premise1: Politicians who win their elections usually keep their campaign promises.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Politicians never promise what they really intend to do once in office.

premise: Statement1: Economist : technology now changes so rapidly that workers need periodic retraining. Such retraining can be efficient only if it allows individual companies to meet their own shortterm needs. Statement2: Hence, large governmental job retraining programs are no longer a viable option in the effort to retrain workers efficiently.
hypothesis: Premise1: Large governmental job retraining programs do not meet the short-term needs of individual companies.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: No single type of retraining program is most efficient at retraining technological workers.

premise: Statement1: Researchers hope to find clues about the a'mk peoples who lived in the kaumpta region about one thousand years ago but who left few obvious traces. Statement2: The researchers plan to hire the few remaining shamans of the modern-day indigenous people in kaumpta, who are believed to be descended from the a'mk, to lead them to ancestral sites that may be the remains of a'mk buildings or ceremonial spaces. Statement3: The shamans were taught the location of such sites as part of their traditional training as youths, and their knowledge of traditional kaumpta customs may help determine the nature of any sites the researchers find.
hypothesis: Premise1: The shamans have a reasonably accurate memory of a'mk sites they learned about as youths.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most or all a'mk sites are likely to be found within the kaumpta region.

premise: Statement1: Many important types of medicine have been developed from substances discovered in plants that grow only in tropical rain forests. Statement2: There are thousands of plant species in these rain forests that have not yet been studied by scientists, and it is very likely that many such plants also contain substances of medicinal value. Statement3: Thus, if the tropical rain forests are not preserved, important types of medicine will never be developed.
hypothesis: Premise1: There are substances of medicinal value contained in tropical rain forest plants not yet studied by scientists that differ from those substances already discovered in tropical rain forest plants.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Any substance of medicinal value contained in plant species indigenous to tropical rain forests will eventually be discovered if those species are studied by scientists.

premise: Statement1: Over the last five years, every new major alternative-energy initiative that initially was promised government funding has since seen that funding severely curtailed. In no such case has the government come even close to providing the level of funds initially earmarked for these projects. Statement2: Since large corporations have made it a point to discourage alternative-energy projects, it is likely that the corporations'actions influenced the government's funding decisions.
hypothesis: Premise1: The only research projects whose government funding has been severely curtailed are those that large corporations have made it a point to discourage.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The funding initially earmarked for a government project is always subject to change, given the mechanisms by which the political process operates.

premise: Statement1: Reducing stress lessens a person's sensitivity to pain. Statement2: This is the conclusion reached by researchers who played extended audiotapes to patients before they underwent surgery and afterward while they were recovering. Statement3: One tape consisted of conversation ; the other consisted of music. Statement4: Those who listened only to the latter tape required less anesthesia during surgery and fewer painkillers afterward than those who listened only to the former tape.
hypothesis: Premise1: Listening to music reduces stress.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Both anesthesia and painkillers tend to reduce stress.

premise: Statement1: There are many structural and thematic similarities between piers plowman by langland ( 1330-1400 ) and house of fame by chaucer ( 1342-1400 ), two middle english poems relating dream visions. Statement2: Some critics have argued that because a number of the shared elements are uncommon in middle english poetry, and because langland's poem probably predates chaucer's by a few years, chaucer was most likely influenced by piers plowman when writing house of fame.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many of the themes and structures of piers plowman are also found in latin, italian, french works with which chaucer could well have been familiar.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: House of fame survives in only three manuscript copies, substantially fewer than the number of manuscript copies that exist of piers plowman.

premise: Statement1: Each of two drugs, s and t, greatly reduces the effects of potentially fatal heart attacks if given as soon as possible after the attack begins, but a trial has shown that use of drug t instead of drug s would prevent death in one additional case out of 120. sent2: drug t, however, costs $ 2,000 more per treatment than drug s. sent3: therefore society is presented with a stark policy decision : whether or not to pay the $ 240,000 it would cost to use drug t in order to save one additional patient.
hypothesis: Premise1: There is no quick, practical, and relatively inexpensive way of telling for any individual case whether drug s will be as effective as drug t.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: After a heart attack, drug t remains relatively effective if given at a time at which drug s is no longer effective.

premise: Statement1: Medical researcher : scientists compared a large group of joggers who habitually stretch before jogging to an equal number of joggers who do not stretch before jogging. Statement2: Both groups of joggers incurred roughly the same number of injuries. Statement3: This indicates that stretching before jogging does not help to prevent injuries.
hypothesis: Premise1: The more prone a jogger is to jogging injuries, the more likely he or she is to develop the habit of performing stretches before jogging.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Most jogging injuries result from falls, collisions, and other mishaps on which the flexibility resulting from stretching would have little if any effect.

premise: Statement1: Medical researcher : as expected, records covering the last four years of ten major hospitals indicate that babies born prematurely were more likely to have low birth weights and to suffer from health problems than were babies not born prematurely. Statement2: These records also indicate that mothers who had received adequate prenatal care were less likely to have low birth weight babies than were mothers who had received inadequate prenatal care. Statement3: Adequate prenatal care, therefore, significantly decreases the risk of low birth weight babies.
hypothesis: Premise1: Mothers giving birth prematurely are routinely classified by hospitals as having received inadequate prenatal care when the record of that care is not available.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The hospital records indicate that many babies that are born with normal birth weights are born to mothers who had inadequate prenatal care.

premise: Statement1: Magazine article : sugar consumption may exacerbate attention deficit disorder ( add ) in children. Statement2: A recent study found that children produce large amounts of adrenaline within hours after consuming large amounts of sugar. This increase in adrenaline is especially noticeable if the source of sugar is candy, in which case the sugar's effects are not ameliorated by the ingestion of other foodstuffs.
hypothesis: Premise1: Increased adrenaline production can make add more severe in children.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Sugar consumed with food substances other than candy does not substantially increase the level of adrenaline in the bloodstream of children with add.

premise: Statement1: Traditional hatcheries raise fish in featureless environments and subject them to dull routines, whereas new, experimental hatcheries raise fish in visually stimulating environments with varied routines. When released into the wild, fish from the experimental hatcheries are bolder than those from traditional hatcheries in exploring new environments and trying new types of food. Statement2: Fish raised in the experimental hatcheries, therefore, are more likely to survive after their release.
hypothesis: Premise1: Some fish raised in traditional hatcheries die because they are too timid in their foraging for food.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Fish in the wild always live in visually stimulating environments.

premise: Statement1: People with higher-than-average blood levels of a normal dietary by-product called homocysteine are twice as likely to be diagnosed with alzheimer's disease as are those with average or below-average homocysteine levels. Statement2: Thus, it is likely that the risk of developing alzheimer's disease could be reduced by including in one's diet large amounts of b vitamins and folic acid, which convert homocysteine into substances known to have no relation to alzheimer's disease.
hypothesis: Premise1: Alzheimer's disease tends to increase the levels of homocysteine in the blood.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: B vitamins and folic acid are not metabolized by the body very efficiently when taken in the form of vitamin-mineral supplements.

premise: Statement1: In a transportation company, a certain syndrome often attributed to stress by medical experts afflicts a significantly higher percentage of workers in department f than in any other department. Statement2: We can conclude, therefore, that the work done in department f subjects workers to higher stress levels than does the work in the other departments in the company.
hypothesis: Premise1: Many workers who transfer into department f from elsewhere in the company soon begin to develop the syndrome.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Some experts believe that the syndrome can be caused by various factors, only one of which is high stress.

premise: Statement1: In mountainous regions, the timberline is the highest altitude at which trees grow. Statement2: In the rocky mountains, the current timberline is at the altitude above which growing season temperatures remain cooler than 10 degrees centigrade. Statement3: Fossilized remains of trees that grew 10,000 years ago have been found 100 meters above the current rocky mountain timberline. Statement4: Clearly, therefore, the climate of the rocky mountains is cooler now than it was 10,000 years ago.
hypothesis: Premise1: The fossils are not of species of trees that were able to tolerate cooler growing temperatures than are the species that currently grow near the timberline.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: In the past 10, 000 years, the only trees to have grown above today's timberline are the trees whose fossilized remains been found.

premise: Statement1: The population of peregrine falcons declined rapidly during the 1950's and 1960's and reached an all-time low in the early 1970's. Statement2: The decline was attributed by scientists to the widespread use of the pesticide ddt in rural areas.
hypothesis: Premise1: In the time since the use of ddt was banned in 1972, the population of peregrine falcons has been steadily increasing.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Peregrine falcons, like other birds of prey, abandon eggs that have fallen out of the nest, even if the eggs remain intact.

premise: Statement1: The reason j.s. Bach is remembered is not that he had a high ratio of outstanding compositions to mediocre compositions. It is rather because he was such a prolific composer. Statement2: He wrote more than a thousand full-fledged compositions, so it was inevitable that some of them would be outstanding and, being outstanding, survive the ages.
hypothesis: Premise1: Several of bach's contemporaries who produced more works than he did have been largely forgotten.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: There are a few highly regarded composers who wrote a comparatively small number of compositions.

premise: Statement1: Most small children are flat-footed. Statement2: This failure of the foot to assume its natural arch, if it persists past early childhood can sometimes result in discomfort and even pain later in life. Statement3: Traditionally, flat-footedness in children has been treated by having the children wear special shoes that give extra support to the foot, in order to foster the development of the arch.
hypothesis: Premise1: Flat-footed children who do not wear the special shoes are as likely to develop natural arches as are flat-footed children who wear the special shoes.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Although most children's flat-footedness is corrected by the time the children reach puberty, some people remain flat-footed for life.

premise: Statement1: A survey of entrepreneurs who started companies last year shows that while virtually all did substantial preparatory research and planning, only half used that work to produce a formal business plan. Statement2: Since, on average, the entrepreneurs without formal plans secured the capital they needed in half the time of those with plans, these survey results indicate that, in general, formal plans did not help the entrepreneurs who produced them to secure the capital they needed.
hypothesis: Premise1: Among the entrepreneurs surveyed, those who did not produce formal business plans sought and received a much larger proportion of their capital from investors with whom they had a long-standing business relationship.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: Companies started by entrepreneurs who had used formal business plans to attract investment were on the whole as profitable in their first year as were companies started by entrepreneurs who had not produced such plans.

premise: Statement1: On the caribbean island of guadeloupe, a researcher examined 35 patients with atypical parkinson's disease and compared their eating habits to those of 65 healthy adults. Statement2: She found that all of the patients with atypical parkinson's regularly ate the tropical fruits soursop, custard apple, and pomme cannelle, whereas only 10 of the healthy adults regularly ate these fruits. Statement3: From this, she concluded that eating these fruits causes atypical parkinson's.
hypothesis: Premise1: For many of the atypical parkinson's patients, their symptoms stopped getting worse, and in some cases actually abated, when they stopped eating soursop, custard apple, and pomme cannelle.
alt_hypothesis: Premise2: The 10 healthy adults who regularly ate soursop, custard apple, and pomme cannelle ate significantly greater quantities of these fruits, on average, than did the 35 atypical parkinson's patients.

premise: Statement1: In the nation of partoria, large trucks currently have a much higher rate of traffic accidents per mile driven than other vehicles do. Statement2: However, the very largest trucks-those with three trailers-had less than a third of the accident rate of single- and double-trailer trucks.
